questions.question_text: A map uses a scale of 1 cm : 250 m. On the map, point A is located at (3.2 cm, 5.6 cm) and point B at (8.7 cm, 9.1 cm). What is the bearing from A to B measured clockwise from north? (Round to the nearest degree.)
questions.explanation:
Scale conversion: 1 cm = 250 m.
ΔE = (8.7‑3.2) cm × 250 = 5.5 cm × 250 = 1 375 m.
ΔN = (9.1‑5.6) cm × 250 = 3.5 cm × 250 = 875 m.
Bearing = arctan(ΔE/ΔN) = arctan(1375/875) ≈ arctan(1.5714) ≈ 57.5°. Rounded to the nearest degree gives 58°, which corresponds to option B.
Option A (45°) would require ΔE = ΔN, which is not the case.
Option C (73°) would be a larger eastward component.
Option D (90°) would imply no northward component.

questions.question_text: A volcano located 200 km inland from the nearest plate boundary erupts basaltic lava flows that form a broad shield shape. Which tectonic setting most likely explains this volcanic activity?
questions.explanation: Shield‑type basaltic volcanoes far from plate margins are characteristic of intra‑plate hotspot volcanism, where a mantle plume rises independently of plate boundaries. Subduction zones produce andesitic to rhyolitic volcanoes, divergent ridges are under oceans, and transform faults are not associated with volcanism.

questions.question_text: At a latitude of \(45^{\circ}\text{N}\), the day length \(L\) (in hours) on a given day can be approximated by \[ L = \frac{2}{15}\,\arccos\!\bigl(-\tan\phi\,\tan\delta\bigr) \] where \(\phi\) is the latitude and \(\delta\) is the solar declination. Using \(\delta = +23.44^{\circ}\) for the summer solstice, what is the approximate day length? (Round to the nearest tenth of an hour.)
questions.explanation:
Compute the components: \(	an\phi = 	an45^{\circ}=1\), \(	an\delta = 	an23.44^{\circ}\approx0.4335\).
Product \(	an\phi	an\delta = 0.4335\).
\(-	an\phi	an\delta = -0.4335\).
\(\arccos(-0.4335) \approx 115.7^{\circ}\).
Day length \(L =  rac{2}{15}	imes115.7 \approx 15.4\) h.  Option C matches this value.  The other options correspond to different latitudes or declinations.

questions.question_text: According to the Hoyt sector model, which of the following land‑use patterns is most likely to develop along a major highway that extends outward from the central business district?
questions.explanation: The sector model proposes that growth radiates outward in wedge‑shaped sectors following major transportation routes. High‑income residential areas often occupy a sector along a desirable corridor such as a highway, matching option B.  Concentric rings (A) describe the Burgess model, random scattering (C) conflicts with the model’s ordered growth, and a centralized industrial zone (D) does not fit a sector extending from the CBD.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, the workflow shows the stages of creating a vector GIS layer from raw satellite imagery. Which step directly follows "Georeferencing"?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="20" y="30" width="100" height="30" fill="#f0f0f0" stroke="#000"/><text x="70" y="52" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle">Image Acquisition</text><line x1="120" y1="45" x2="150" y2="45" stroke="#000" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect x="150" y="30" width="100" height="30" fill="#f0f0f0" stroke="#000"/><text x="200" y="52" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle">Georeferencing</text><line x1="250" y1="45" x2="280" y2="45" stroke="#000" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect x="280" y="30" width="100" height="30" fill="#f0f0f0" stroke="#000"/><text x="330" y="52" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle">Image Classification</text><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L0,6 L6,3 z" fill="#000"/></marker></defs></svg>
questions.explanation: After georeferencing, the image is correctly positioned in spatial coordinates, allowing the next logical step—classifying the spectral information into thematic categories (e.g., land‑cover). This produces a raster classification that can later be vectorized. Vector digitization (B) follows classification, attribute assignment (C) follows digitization, and spatial analysis (D) is a later application.

questions.question_text: A topographic map is drawn at a scale of 1 cm : 2500 m. On the map, point P is at coordinates (4.3 cm, 2.1 cm) and point Q is at (9.7 cm, 7.8 cm). What is the true bearing from P to Q, expressed in degrees clockwise from north (rounded to the nearest degree)?
questions.explanation: Calculate the differences in the map coordinates: \(\Delta x = 9.7 - 4.3 = 5.4\) cm and \(\Delta y = 7.8 - 2.1 = 5.7\) cm. Because the scale is uniform, the ratio \(\Delta x/\Delta y\) is the same on the ground.\nThe bearing from north is given by \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(\Delta x/\Delta y)\). Substituting, \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(5.4/5.7) \approx \tan^{-1}(0.9474) \approx 43.5°\). Both \(\Delta x\) and \(\Delta y\) are positive, placing the direction in the northeast quadrant, so the true bearing is north plus 43.5°, i.e., about 44°.\nOption A matches this value. Options B, C and D are either off by a few degrees or represent different quadrants, so they are incorrect.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a volcano displays a broad, gently sloping shield shape. The central magma chamber lies at a depth of 12 km beneath the surface. Which type of magma is most likely feeding this eruption?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="60" y="30" width="200" height="150" fill="#e0e0e0" stroke="#000"/><circle cx="160" cy="80" r="30" fill="#ff9900" stroke="#000"/><text x="160" y="150" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">Surface</text></svg>
questions.explanation: Shield volcanoes are characterized by low‑profile, gently sloping cones built from highly fluid lava that can travel long distances. This fluid lava is typically basaltic, which has low silica content and low viscosity. Andesitic, rhyolitic, and dacitic magmas are more viscous and produce steeper volcanic edifices such as stratovolcanoes. Therefore the magma feeding the described shield volcano is most likely basaltic (Option A).

questions.question_text: At a latitude of 60° N on the day of the summer solstice, the solar declination is approximately +23.5°. Using the approximation \[ L = \frac{24}{\pi}\,\arccos\bigl(-\tan\phi\,\tan\delta\bigr) \] where \(L\) is the daylight length in hours, \(\phi\) is latitude and \(\delta\) is declination, what is the approximate length of daylight on that day? (Round to the nearest hour.)
questions.explanation: Compute the trigonometric terms: \(\tan\phi = \tan60° \approx 1.732\), \(\tan\delta = \tan23.5° \approx 0.433\). Their product is \(1.732 \times 0.433 \approx 0.75\). Then \(-\tan\phi\,\tan\delta = -0.75\).\n\(\arccos(-0.75)\) ≈ 138.6° = 2.42 rad.\nNow \(L = \frac{24}{\pi} \times 2.42 \approx 7.639 \times 2.42 \approx 18.5\) h. Rounded to the nearest hour, the daylight length is about 18 h (Option A). The other options are inconsistent with the calculation.

questions.question_text: A region had a population of 2.5 million in 2020. The annual natural increase rate is 1.2 % and the net migration rate is +0.4 % per year. Assuming these rates remain constant, what will be the population at the end of 2025 (after 5 years)?
questions.explanation: Combine the rates: total annual growth rate \(r = 1.2\% + 0.4\% = 1.6\% = 0.016\). Using the compound growth formula \(P_{5}=2.5\,(1+0.016)^{5}\).\n\((1.016)^{5} \approx 1.082\). Thus \(P_{5} \approx 2.5 \times 1.082 = 2.705\) million, which rounds to 2.70 million. Option A matches this result. The other options result from using slightly higher or lower growth rates or forgetting to compound, so they are incorrect.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, the workflow for converting raw satellite imagery into a usable GIS vector layer is shown. Which step is performed immediately after data acquisition?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="10" y="30" width="140" height="40" fill="#cce5ff" stroke="#000"/><text x="80" y="55" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">Acquisition</text><rect x="170" y="30" width="140" height="40" fill="#cce5ff" stroke="#000"/><text x="240" y="55" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">Pre‑processing</text><line x1="150" y1="50" x2="170" y2="50" stroke="#000" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="7" refX="0" refY="3.5" orient="auto"><polygon points="0 0,10 3.5,0 7" fill="#000"/></marker></defs></svg>
questions.explanation: The standard remote‑sensing workflow begins with data acquisition (capturing the raw satellite image). The next logical step is to prepare the data for analysis, which includes radiometric and geometric corrections, noise removal, and formatting—collectively known as pre‑processing. Classification, vectorisation, and validation occur later in the chain. Therefore, the step immediately after acquisition is pre‑processing (Option A).

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a map shows a straight road drawn between towns X and Y. The length of the road on the map measures 6 cm. The map scale is 1 cm : 5 km. The bearing of the road from X to Y is shown as 70°. What is the actual ground distance between the towns, and what is the azimuth (bearing) of the line from Y to X?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="50" y1="150" x2="250" y2="150" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="250" y1="150" x2="230" y2="130" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="140" font-size="14" text-anchor="middle">Scale 1 cm : 5 km</text></svg>
questions.explanation: 1. Convert map distance to ground distance using the scale: 6 cm × 5 km/cm = 30 km.\n2. The reverse bearing is obtained by adding 180° to the forward bearing and reducing modulo 360°: 70° + 180° = 250°.\n3. Option A matches both the calculated distance (30 km) and the reverse bearing (250°). Options B and D keep the forward bearing, which is incorrect for the direction from Y to X. Option C uses the map distance as a ground distance, which ignores the scale.

questions.question_text: Which type of volcano is most likely to form an island chain such as the Hawaiian Islands, and what is the typical magma composition associated with this volcano type?
questions.explanation: Island chains over oceanic hotspots are built by repeated eruptions of low‑viscosity basaltic magma that creates broad, gently sloping shield volcanoes. Stratovolcanoes, cinder cones, and lava domes have steeper profiles and different magma compositions, making them unsuitable explanations for the Hawaiian chain.

questions.question_text: A city had a population of 1.20 million in 2018. The annual natural increase rate is 1.5 % and net migration contributes ‑0.3 % per year (more people leave than arrive). Assuming the rates remain constant, what is the projected population in 2023 (after 5 years)?
questions.explanation:
1. Net annual growth rate = natural increase + net migration = 1.5 % ‑ 0.3 % = 1.2 % = 0.012.
2. Apply the compound growth formula: \[ P_{5}=1.20	imes10^{6}\,(1+0.012)^{5} \].
3. \( (1.012)^{5}\approx1.0614 \).
4. \( 1.20	imes10^{6}	imes1.0614\approx1.2737	imes10^{6} \) ≈ 1.27 million.
5. Option A matches this calculation. The other options either use a higher growth rate or round incorrectly.

questions.question_text: According to the Hoyt sector model, which type of land‑use is most likely to develop in a sector extending outward from a city along a major highway?

options.text: Expansion of the central business district

questions.explanation: Hoyt’s sector model proposes that transportation corridors such as highways favor the growth of industrial and warehousing activities because they need easy access to roads for freight movement. High‑density residential sectors usually form along desirable amenities (e.g., water fronts), not along raw transport routes. Therefore, option B correctly reflects the model’s prediction.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A satellite sensor flies at an altitude of 800 km and has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.2 mrad. What is the ground sampling distance (GSD) – i.e., the size of one pixel on the ground – for this sensor?
questions.explanation: Convert altitude to metres: 800 km = 800,000 m.\nGSD = H \times IFOV = 800,000 m \times 0.0002 rad = 160 m.\nOption C provides the correct ground sampling distance. The other options result from using an incorrect conversion factor or forgetting to convert the IFOV to radians.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, two points are plotted on a topographic map. Point A is located at (2 cm, 3 cm) and point B at (7 cm, 9 cm) measured from the map origin. What is the bearing from A to B measured clockwise from true north?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='50' cy='150' r='5' fill='red'/><text x='40' y='165' font-size='12'>A</text><circle cx='250' cy='50' r='5' fill='blue'/><text x='260' y='45' font-size='12'>B</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
First compute the coordinate differences: \(\Delta x = 7	ext{ cm} - 2	ext{ cm} = 5	ext{ cm}\) and \(\Delta y = 9	ext{ cm} - 3	ext{ cm} = 6	ext{ cm}\). The bearing \(	heta\) measured clockwise from north is given by \(	heta = \arctan\left( rac{\Delta x}{\Delta y}ight)\). Substituting, \(	heta = \arctan\left( rac{5}{6}ight) \approx 39.8^{\circ}\), which is rounded to 040°.

Option A matches this value.

Why the other options are wrong:
- B (050°) and D (060°) over‑estimate the angle.
- C (030°) under‑estimates it.
Thus the correct bearing is 040°.

questions.question_text: Which statement best explains why magmas generated at convergent plate margins often evolve from basaltic to andesitic or rhyolitic compositions?

options.text: The thickened continental crust causes extensive fractional crystallisation of mafic minerals, enriching the residual melt in silica.
Higher mantle temperatures at convergent margins produce initially felsic magmas that later assimilate basaltic crust.
Subduction introduces large volumes of carbonate rocks that directly melt into rhyolite.
Rapid ascent of magma prevents crystal settling, preserving a basaltic composition.

questions.explanation:
At convergent margins, especially continental‑continent collisions, the crust is thick (30‑70 km). Basaltic magma rising through this thick crust undergoes prolonged residence, during which mafic minerals (olivine, pyroxene) crystallise and settle out (fractional crystallisation). The remaining melt becomes progressively richer in silica, evolving to andesite and rhyolite.

Option B is incorrect because mantle temperatures are not higher at convergent margins; they are generally lower than at divergent settings.
Option C incorrectly attributes rhyolite generation to carbonate melting, which yields soda‑rich magmas, not typical rhyolites.
Option D describes a scenario that would retain basaltic composition, the opposite of what is observed.
Therefore, option A correctly captures the process of crust‑induced fractional crystallisation leading to felsic magmas.

questions.question_text: At a latitude of \(60^{\circ}\text{N}\) on the summer solstice (solar declination \(\delta = +23.44^{\circ}\)), the approximate day length can be estimated by \[ L = 24 \left(1 - \frac{1}{\pi}\arccos\bigl(\tan\phi\,\tan\delta\bigr)\right) \] where \(\phi\) is latitude. What is the calculated day length (in hours) to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation:
First compute \(	an\phi\) and \(	an\delta\):
\(	an 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}\approx1.732\)
\(	an 23.44^{\circ}\approx0.433\)
Product: \(1.732 	imes 0.433 \approx 0.75\).

Now \(\arccos(0.75) \approx 41.41^{\circ}=0.7227\) rad.
Plug into the formula:
\[L = 24\left(1- rac{0.7227}{\pi}ight)=24\left(1-0.2299ight)=24	imes0.7701\approx18.48	ext{ h}\]
Rounded to the nearest tenth gives 18.5 h.

Option A matches this value. The other options are either too low (B, D) or too high (C).

questions.question_text: Country X had a population of 10.0 million in 2010. Its annual natural increase rate is 1.2 % and it experiences a net migration rate of –0.3 % per year (net emigration). Assuming the rates remain constant, what is the estimated population in 2015?
questions.explanation:
Combine the natural increase and migration rates to obtain the net annual growth rate:
\(r = 0.012 - 0.003 = 0.009\) (0.9 % per year).
Apply the compound growth formula for \(t = 5\) years:
\[P = 10.0	ext{ million}	imes(1+0.009)^{5}\]
Calculate:
\(1.009^{5} \approx 1.0458\)
Thus \(P \approx 10.0 	imes 1.0458 = 10.458\) million, which rounds to **10.45 million**.

Option A is correct.
Option B assumes a higher growth rate, Option C assumes a lower rate, and Option D ignores any change.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: Which of the following sequences correctly represents the standard workflow for producing a vector land‑use layer from raw multispectral satellite imagery?

options.text: Radiometric correction → Geometric correction → Supervised classification → Vectorization → Attribute assignment
Geometric correction → Radiometric correction → Unsupervised classification → Direct digitisation → Spatial indexing
Geometric correction → Atmospheric correction → Object‑based classification → Raster stacking → Export to CAD
Radiometric correction → Mosaic creation → Manual digitisation → Supervised classification → Topology validation

questions.explanation:
The accepted workflow is:
1. **Radiometric correction** – removes sensor‑related errors.
2. **Geometric correction** – aligns the image to a coordinate system.
3. **Supervised classification** – assigns each pixel to a land‑use class using training sites.
4. **Vectorization** – converts the classified raster into vector polygons.
5. **Attribute assignment** – adds descriptive fields (e.g., land‑use type) to the vector features.

Option A follows this exact order.
Option B mixes up the correction order and uses unsupervised classification, which is not the standard for accurate land‑use mapping.
Option C introduces atmospheric correction (useful but not part of the core vector‑layer workflow) and skips vectorisation.
Option D incorrectly places manual digitisation before classification and ends with topology validation, which is a later quality‑control step, not a core workflow stage.
Therefore, option A is the correct sequence.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, points A and B are plotted on a topographic map. The map scale is 1 cm : 4 km. The coordinates of A are (2.0 cm, 3.0 cm) and B are (6.5 cm, 7.0 cm) measured from the map origin. What is the bearing from A to B, expressed as a three‑digit bearing measured clockwise from true north?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="80" cy="150" r="5" fill="red"/><text x="80" y="145" font-size="12">A</text><circle cx="240" cy="50" r="5" fill="blue"/><text x="240" y="45" font-size="12">B</text><line x1="80" y1="150" x2="240" y2="50" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/></svg>
questions.explanation: First find the map‑distance differences: \(\Delta x = 6.5\,\text{cm} - 2.0\,\text{cm} = 4.5\,\text{cm}\) (eastward) and \(\Delta y = 7.0\,\text{cm} - 3.0\,\text{cm} = 4.0\,\text{cm}\) (northward).  The bearing \(\theta\) from north is given by \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(\Delta x/\Delta y)\).  Substituting, \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(4.5/4.0) \approx \tan^{-1}(1.125) \approx 48^{\circ}\).  Expressed as a three‑digit bearing this is 048°.  Option A matches; the other options result from incorrect angle calculations or mis‑interpreting the quadrant.

questions.question_text: Which volcanic landform is most characteristically produced at a continental convergent margin where subduction of oceanic crust generates magma that must ascend through a thickened continental crust?
questions.explanation: Continental convergent margins (e.g., the Andes) involve subduction of dense oceanic lithosphere beneath a buoyant continental plate.  Melts generated by slab dehydration are relatively silica‑rich and must travel through a thick crust, allowing differentiation and the build‑up of steep, layered edifices—stratovolcanoes.  Shield volcanoes are typical of intraplate hot‑spot settings with low‑viscosity basaltic magma; lava plateaus and flood basalts arise from massive outpourings of low‑viscosity lava, not the steep, explosive edifices of continental arcs.

questions.question_text: Which statement best explains the onset of the summer monsoon over South Asia?

options.text: Differential heating of the Asian landmass and the Indian Ocean creates a low‑pressure zone over the continent that draws moist oceanic air inland.
A sudden shift of the Inter‑tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ) to the south of the equator forces southerly winds over the region.
The retreat of the polar jet stream allows cold continental air to move southward, pulling moist air from the ocean.
Increased solar radiation over the Himalayas causes katabatic winds that push moisture toward the plains.

questions.explanation: During boreal summer the Asian landmass heats up much more rapidly than the adjacent Indian Ocean.  This strong land‑sea temperature contrast produces a deep low‑pressure cell over the continent.  The pressure gradient between the oceanic high and continental low drives moist onshore flow— the classic summer monsoon.  Options B, C, and D describe unrelated atmospheric processes and cannot account for the observed seasonal reversal of winds over South Asia.

questions.question_text: A region had a population of 5.0 million in 2018. The annual natural increase rate is 1.2 % and net migration contributes –0.3 % per year. Assuming the rates remain constant, what is the projected population at the end of 2023 (5 years later)?
questions.explanation:
Effective annual growth rate = natural increase + net migration = 1.2 % – 0.3 % = 0.9 % = 0.009.
Using compound growth: \(P_{5}=5{,}000{,}000\times(1+0.009)^{5}\).
\((1.009)^{5}\approx 1.0458\) (calculated via binomial expansion or a calculator).  Hence \(P_{5}\approx 5{,}000{,}000\times1.0458\approx 5{,}229{,}000\), i.e., about **5.23 million**.  Option B matches; the other options arise from using only natural increase, ignoring migration, or rounding errors.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A satellite sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.5 milliradians and orbits at an altitude of 700 km above the Earth’s surface. What is the ground‑sample distance (GSD) produced by this sensor, expressed in metres?
questions.explanation: Convert IFOV to radians: 0.5 milliradians = 0.5 × 10⁻³ rad = 0.0005 rad.  Altitude = 700 km = 700,000 m.  \(\text{GSD}=700,000\,\text{m}\times0.0005\,\text{rad}=350\,\text{m}\).  Therefore the sensor records each pixel as a 350‑metre ground foot‑print.  Option D is correct; the other choices result from misplaced decimal‑point conversion.

questions.question_text: A topographic map has a scale of 1 cm : 50 000 cm (i.e., 1 cm on the map represents 0.5 km on the ground). The distance between Town A and Town B measures 7.2 cm on the map. What is the approximate ground distance between the two towns?
questions.explanation: The map scale 1 cm : 50 000 cm means 1 cm on the map = 0.5 km on the ground. Multiply the measured map distance by the ground distance per centimetre: 7.2 cm × 0.5 km/cm = 3.6 km. Option B (36 km) would be obtained if the scale were 1 cm : 5 000 cm, C is too small, and D ignores the scale conversion.

questions.question_text: Which type of magma is most commonly associated with eruptions at divergent mid‑ocean ridges?
questions.explanation: Mid‑ocean ridges are characterised by decompression melting of the upwelling mantle, which produces low‑viscosity, silica‑poor basaltic magma. Andesitic and rhyolitic magmas are typical of convergent margins, while ultramafic magmas are rare at the surface. Therefore the correct answer is Basaltic (A).

questions.question_text: At a latitude of \(40^{\circ}\text{N}\), the day length on the summer solstice can be approximated by \[ L = 12 + \frac{2}{15}\arcsin\bigl(\tan\phi\,\tan\delta\bigr) \] where \(\phi\) is latitude and \(\delta = 23.44^{\circ}\) is the solar declination. What is the estimated day length (in hours) on the summer solstice?
questions.explanation: First compute \(\tan\phi = \tan 40^{\circ} \approx 0.8391\) and \(\tan\delta = \tan 23.44^{\circ} \approx 0.4335\). Their product is 0.3639. \(\arcsin(0.3639) \approx 21.3^{\circ}\). Convert the angle to hours: \(\frac{2}{15}\times 21.3 \approx 2.84\) h. Adding the base 12 h gives \(L \approx 14.84\) h, which rounds to 14.8 h. The other options are either too low (B, D) or too high (C).

questions.question_text: A region had a population of 5.0 million in 2019. The annual natural increase rate is 1.2 % and the net migration rate is +0.3 % per year. Assuming the rates remain constant, what is the projected population at the end of 2022 (after three years)?
questions.explanation: Combined annual growth rate \(r = 1.2\% + 0.3\% = 1.5\% = 0.015\). Using the compound growth formula: \(P = 5.0\times10^{6}(1+0.015)^{3}\). \((1.015)^{3} \approx 1.04568\). Multiply: \(5.0\times10^{6} \times 1.04568 \approx 5.23\times10^{6}\). Option A underestimates (uses simple addition), C overestimates (uses higher rate), D ignores growth.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, the workflow shows the sequential steps used to create a raster land‑use map from raw satellite imagery. Which step is performed first?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='10' y='20' width='300' height='30' fill='#cce5ff'/><text x='20' y='40' font-size='12' fill='black'>Satellite Image</text><rect x='10' y='80' width='300' height='30' fill='#d4edda'/><text x='20' y='100' font-size='12' fill='black'>Radiometric Correction</text><rect x='10' y='140' width='300' height='30' fill='#fff3cd'/><text x='20' y='160' font-size='12' fill='black'>Classification</text></svg>
questions.explanation: The first operation after acquiring raw satellite data is to correct sensor and atmospheric effects, known as radiometric correction. This prepares the data for reliable further processing. Georeferencing (A) comes after radiometric correction, classification (B) follows georeferencing, and accuracy assessment (D) is the final validation step. Hence the correct answer is C.

questions.question_text: A topographic map of a hilly region is drawn at a scale of 1 cm : 250 m. Two hilltops, H1 and H2, are located at map coordinates (12.4 cm, 7.6 cm) and (18.9 cm, 13.2 cm) respectively. Determine the bearing from H1 to H2 to the nearest degree and the ground distance between the hilltops in kilometres.
questions.explanation: First find the differences in the map coordinates: \(\Delta E = 18.9 - 12.4 = 6.5\) cm, \(\Delta N = 13.2 - 7.6 = 5.6\) cm. The bearing from north is \(\theta = \arctan(6.5/5.6) \approx 49^{\circ}\). The straight‑line distance on the map is \(\sqrt{6.5^2 + 5.6^2}=\sqrt{73.61}=8.58\) cm. With a scale of 1 cm = 250 m = 0.25 km, the ground distance is \(8.58\times0.25=2.145\) km, i.e. about 2.15 km. Option A matches both the bearing and the distance; the other options contain either an incorrect bearing, an incorrect distance, or both.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements correctly explains why basaltic magma generated at a divergent plate boundary typically results in low‑viscosity lava flows?

options.text: It contains a high proportion of silica, which breaks down polymer chains and reduces viscosity.
It is relatively hot (≈1200 °C) and low in silica, so the melt remains fluid.
It is enriched in water vapor, which increases surface tension and lowers viscosity.
It crystallises rapidly on ascent, creating a highly viscous glassy lava.

questions.explanation: Basaltic magma at divergent boundaries is derived from decompression melting of the upwelling mantle. It is hot (about 1200 °C) and chemically mafic, containing low silica (~50 %). Low silica means fewer polymerised Si‑O chains, resulting in low viscosity. Option B states this correctly. Option A is the opposite (high silica gives high viscosity). Option C confuses the effect of water, which actually lowers viscosity but basalt at ridges is not water‑rich. Option D describes rapid crystallisation, which would increase viscosity, not decrease it.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, the line chart shows the average monthly temperature (°C) of City X. Which month experiences the greatest temperature deviation from the city’s annual mean temperature?
questions.explanation: The monthly temperatures are: Jan 22, Feb 24, Mar 27, Apr 30, May 33, Jun 36, Jul 38, Aug 37, Sep 34, Oct 29, Nov 25, Dec 22 °C. The annual mean is \(\frac{22+24+27+30+33+36+38+37+34+29+25+22}{12}=29.75\) °C. The absolute deviations are: Jan 7.75, Feb 5.75, Mar 2.75, Apr 0.25, May 3.25, Jun 6.25, Jul 8.25, Aug 7.25, Sep 4.25, Oct 0.75, Nov 4.75, Dec 7.75 °C. The largest deviation (8.25 °C) occurs in July, making option B correct. All other months have smaller deviations.

questions.question_text: A region had a population of 4.2 million in 2015. The annual natural increase rate is 1.3 % and the net migration rate is –0.4 % (net out‑migration). Assuming the rates remain constant, what is the projected population in 2020?
questions.explanation: Overall annual growth rate \(r = 1.3\% - 0.4\% = 0.9\% = 0.009\). Using the compound growth formula \(P = P_0(1+r)^t\) with \(P_0 = 4.2\) million, \(t = 5\) years: \((1+0.009)^5 \approx 1.0459\). Hence \(P \approx 4.2 \times 1.0459 = 4.390\) million, i.e. about 4.39 million. Option B matches this calculation. The other options result from using incorrect rates or rounding errors.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A satellite sensor captures multispectral imagery with a spatial resolution of 30 m and a spectral resolution of 8 bands. Which of the following applications would benefit most from this combination?

options.text: Mapping detailed urban infrastructure (e.g., individual buildings)
Monitoring large‑scale vegetation health using indices such as NDVI
Detecting fine‑scale mineral deposits requiring sub‑meter resolution
Measuring surface temperature variations with a thermal band

questions.explanation: A 30 m spatial resolution is adequate for observing patterns over agricultural fields and natural vegetation, while 8 spectral bands provide the necessary wavelengths to compute vegetation indices (e.g., NDVI, EVI). Therefore, large‑scale vegetation health monitoring is the most suitable application. Option A would need finer (<5 m) resolution, Option C requires sub‑meter resolution, and Option D relies on thermal bands rather than the number of visible/near‑infrared bands.

questions.question_text: On a topographic map with a scale of 1 cm : 500 m, two landmarks A and B are located at map coordinates A(3.2 cm, 4.5 cm) and B(7.8 cm, 1.6 cm). What is the bearing from A to B measured clockwise from north? (Round to the nearest degree.)
questions.explanation:
First find the east‑west (ΔE) and north‑south (ΔN) differences in map units:
\[\Delta E = 7.8\,	ext{cm} - 3.2\,	ext{cm} = 4.6\,	ext{cm}\]
\[\Delta N = 1.6\,	ext{cm} - 4.5\,	ext{cm} = -2.9\,	ext{cm}\]

Because ΔN is negative, the direction lies in the southeast quadrant. The bearing is calculated with the navigation formula
\[	heta = \left(\arctan rac{\Delta E}{\Delta N} 	imes  rac{180}{\pi} + 360ight)  mod 360\]

Using a calculator, \(\arctan(4.6 / -2.9) = -57.8^{\circ}\). Adding 360° and taking modulo 360 gives 302.2°, but bearings are measured clockwise from north, so we convert the result to the 0‑180° sector for southeast directions by computing \(180^{\circ} - 57.8^{\circ} = 122.2^{\circ}\). Rounded to the nearest degree, the bearing is 122°, which matches option A.

Option B (68°) would correspond to a direction mainly east‑north, option C (238°) points southwest, and option D (312°) points northwest, none of which fit the calculated ΔE and ΔN.

questions.question_text: Basaltic magma produced at a divergent plate boundary can erupt either effusively or explosively. Which factor is most directly responsible for an explosive basaltic eruption at a continental rift zone?

options.text: High silica content of the magma
High volatile (water) content dissolved in the magma
Low temperature of the magma at the surface

questions.explanation: Basaltic magma is naturally low in silica, which makes it low‑viscosity and usually favors gentle effusive eruptions. However, when significant amounts of volatiles—primarily water—are dissolved in the magma, the rapid exsolution of these gases during ascent creates high internal pressure, driving explosive fragmentation even for low‑silica basalts. High silica (option A) would increase viscosity but is not characteristic of basalt. Low temperature (option C) would actually increase viscosity but does not alone cause explosivity. A high crystal content (option D) can impede flow but is not the primary cause of explosive basaltic eruptions. Therefore, the presence of abundant dissolved volatiles is the key factor, making option B correct.

questions.question_text: A climate diagram for a city shows the following average monthly data (°C): Jan 22, Feb 23, Mar 24, Apr 25, May 26, Jun 27, Jul 27, Aug 27, Sep 26, Oct 25, Nov 23, Dec 22, and average monthly precipitation (mm): Jan 80, Feb 70, Mar 60, Apr 50, May 40, Jun 30, Jul 20, Aug 20, Sep 30, Oct 50, Nov 70, Dec 80. According to the Köppen classification, which climate type best characterizes this city?
questions.explanation:
Key Köppen criteria:
1. The coldest month is above 0 °C (here 22 °C) → climate is not continental (D) nor polar.
2. All months are above 18 °C, satisfying the “tropical” temperature threshold, but the precipitation pattern does not show a marked dry season; the driest month (July‑August) receives 20 mm, which is > (100 – annual‑mean / 25) and therefore does not meet the Aw dry‑season requirement.
3. The climate has a summer maximum in temperature and a fairly even distribution of rainfall, with a slight summer dip – characteristic of the humid subtropical (Cfa) group, where the warmest month exceeds 22 °C and at least four months exceed 10 °C.
Option B (Cfb) requires the warmest month < 22 °C, which is not true here. Options C and D are ruled out by temperature constraints. Hence the best match is Cfa, option A.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 150 000 in 2015. The annual natural increase rate (births minus deaths) is 1.4 % and net migration adds 2 000 persons each year. Assuming the rates remain constant, what will be the population at the beginning of 2021? (Round to the nearest whole number.)
questions.explanation:
Let
\(P_0 = 150{,}000\)
\(r = 0.014\) (1.4 % natural increase)
\(M = 2{,}000\) persons per year
\(n = 2021 - 2015 = 6\) years.

First compute the growth factor:
\[(1+r)^n = (1.014)^6 \approx 1.08664\]

Natural increase contribution:
\[P_{nat} = 150{,}000 	imes 1.08664 \approx 162{,}996\]

Cumulative migration contribution (geometric series):
\[P_{mig} = M rac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r} = 2{,}000 	imes  rac{0.08664}{0.014} \approx 2{,}000 	imes 6.1885 \approx 12{,}377\]

Total population at the start of 2021:
\[P = 162{,}996 + 12{,}377 \approx 175{,}373\]
Rounded to the nearest whole number, this is 175 373, which is closest to the option 175 400 (option C).

Option A under‑estimates both growth and migration, option B is still low, and option D exceeds the calculated total.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor with an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.5 mrad scans the Earth's surface at an altitude of 700 km. What is the approximate ground sample distance (GSD) for a pixel at nadir?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="160" y1="20" x2="160" y2="180" stroke="black"/><circle cx="160" cy="20" r="10" fill="gray"/><text x="160" y="10" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">Satellite</text><text x="160" y="190" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">Ground</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
Ground Sample Distance (GSD) at nadir is the product of the sensor altitude (H) and the instantaneous field of view (θ) expressed in radians.
\[	ext{GSD} = H 	imes 	heta\]

Given:
\(H = 700\,	ext{km} = 700{,}000\,	ext{m}\)
\(	heta = 0.5\,	ext{mrad} = 0.5 	imes 10^{-3}\,	ext{rad} = 0.0005\,	ext{rad}\)

Calculate:
\[	ext{GSD} = 700{,}000\,	ext{m} 	imes 0.0005 = 350\,	ext{m}\]

Thus the pixel size on the ground is approximately 350 m, which corresponds to option C.

Option A (0.35 m) would require an altitude of only 0.7 km, option B (35 m) would correspond to an altitude of 70 km, and option D (3.5 km) would need an altitude of 7 000 km—none of which match the given parameters.

questions.question_text: A topographic map has a scale of 1 cm : 250 m. On the map, the distance between two villages A and B measured with a ruler is 6.4 cm. What is the actual ground distance between the villages in kilometres?
questions.explanation: The map scale tells us that 1 cm on the map represents 250 m on the ground. Multiply the measured map distance by this factor: 6.4 cm × 250 m/cm = 1600 m. Convert to kilometres: 1600 m ÷ 1000 = 1.6 km. Therefore option A is correct. Options B–D correspond to larger distance calculations that ignore the scale conversion.

questions.question_text: Which magma type is most commonly produced at a mid‑ocean ridge and what is its typical silica (SiO₂) weight‑percent range?
questions.explanation: Mid‑ocean ridges are divergent plate boundaries where mantle material partially melts to form basaltic magma. Basaltic magma is low in silica, typically between 45 % and 52 % SiO₂. Andesitic, rhyolitic and komatiitic magmas are associated with convergent margins, continental rifts or Archean settings respectively, so options B–D are incorrect.

questions.question_text: A region had a population of 12.0 million in 2015. The annual natural increase rate is 1.2 % and net migration contributes an additional 0.3 % per year. Assuming these rates remain constant, what is the projected population at the end of 2020 (5 years later)?
questions.explanation:
Combine the two rates: \(r = 0.012 + 0.003 = 0.015\) (1.5 % per year). Using the exponential growth formula:
\[ P = 12.0\text{ million}\times(1+0.015)^{5} \]
\(1.015^{5} \approx 1.0763\). Thus \(P \approx 12.0 \times 1.0763 = 12.915\) million, which rounds to 12.9 million. Option B matches this calculation. Options A, C, and D arise from using incorrect rates or rounding errors.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A satellite sensor orbits at an altitude of 700 km and has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.5 milliradians. What is the ground spatial resolution (cell size) produced by this sensor, in metres?
questions.explanation: First convert IFOV to radians: 0.5 mrad = 0.5 × 10⁻³ rad = 5\times10^{-4}\,rad.\nGround resolution = altitude × IFOV = 700 km × 5\times10^{-4} = 700,000 m × 5\times10^{-4} = 350 m. Therefore option B is correct. The other options correspond to using incorrect unit conversions or arithmetic mistakes.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, point A is located at (50,150) and point B at (250,50) on a map where the y‑axis points north. What is the bearing from A to B measured clockwise from true north?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='0' y='0' width='320' height='200' fill='none' stroke='black'/><circle cx='50' cy='150' r='4' fill='red'/><text x='55' y='155' font-size='12' fill='red'>A</text><circle cx='250' cy='50' r='4' fill='blue'/><text x='255' y='55' font-size='12' fill='blue'>B</text><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='green' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
questions.explanation: Compute the coordinate differences: \(\Delta E = x_B - x_A = 250 - 50 = 200\) (eastward). \(\Delta N = y_B - y_A = 50 - 150 = -100\) (northward, negative because B is north of A on the map). Bearing \(\theta\) is given by \(\theta = \operatorname{atan2}(\Delta E, \Delta N)\) expressed in degrees clockwise from north. \(\operatorname{atan2}(200, -100)\) ≈ 116.6°, which rounds to 117°. Hence option B is correct. Option A would be the acute angle from east, option C is the opposite direction (180°+), and option D is the complement of that opposite direction.

questions.question_text: A topographic map has a scale of 1 cm : 2 km. On the map, the measured distance between two villages is 6.5 cm and the bearing from Village X to Village Y is 135°. What is the true ground distance between the villages, and what is the compass direction from X to Y?
questions.explanation: The map scale 1 cm : 2 km means each centimetre on the map represents 2 km on the ground. The measured map distance is 6.5 cm, so the ground distance is 6.5 cm × 2 km/cm = 13 km. A bearing of 135° is measured clockwise from true north, placing the direction exactly halfway between east (90°) and south (180°), i.e., Southeast. Option A matches both the distance and direction, while the other options either have the wrong distance or an incorrect compass point.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements best explains why magma generated at divergent plate boundaries typically has a lower silica content than magma formed at convergent margins?

options.text: Divergent boundaries involve melting of subducted oceanic crust, which is silica‑rich, whereas convergent margins melt only the mantle.

At divergent boundaries, upwelling mantle peridotite undergoes partial melting, producing basaltic magma low in silica; convergent margins add water‑rich sediments that promote melting of crustal material, yielding more silica‑rich andesitic magma.
Magma at divergent boundaries cools more rapidly, preventing silica enrichment, while convergent‑margin magma cools slowly, allowing silica crystals to form.
The higher pressure at divergent boundaries forces silica to remain in the solid phase, whereas lower pressure at convergent margins releases silica into the melt.

questions.explanation: At divergent (mid‑ocean ridge) settings the mantle peridotite rises, undergoes decompression melting, and yields basaltic magma that is low in SiO₂ (~45–52%). At convergent margins, subducting oceanic slabs release water and melt sediments and altered crust, producing magmas that incorporate more silica‑rich material and evolve toward andesitic compositions (~55–65% SiO₂). Options A, C, and D misstate the source material or the effect of cooling/pressure, so only option B correctly explains the silica contrast.

questions.question_text: Country Z had a population of 8.0 million in 2015. Its annual natural increase rate is 1.5 % and net migration adds 20 000 persons per year. Assuming these rates remain constant, what will be the estimated population at the end of 2020?
questions.explanation:
Let \(P_{0}=8{,}000{,}000\), \(r=0.015\) (1.5 % natural increase) and \(M=20{,}000\) net migrants per year. For \(n=5\) years:
\[ (1+r)^{5}=1.015^{5}\approx1.077284.\]
Natural growth component: \(8{,}000{,}000\times1.077284\approx8{,}618{,}272.\)
Cumulative migration component: \[ M\frac{(1+r)^{5}-1}{r}=20{,}000\times\frac{1.077284-1}{0.015}\approx20{,}000\times5.1523\approx103{,}046.\]
Total population ≈ 8 618 272 + 103 046 = 8 721 318 ≈ 8.72 million. Option B matches this calculation. The other choices are off by more than 0.01 million.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor with an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.4 milliradians orbits at an altitude of 600 km. What is the spatial resolution (ground sampling distance) of the sensor?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='30' fill='none' stroke='black'/><line x1='160' y1='100' x2='300' y2='30' stroke='black'/><text x='160' y='20' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Satellite</text></svg>
questions.explanation: Ground sampling distance (GSD) is obtained by multiplying the sensor altitude by the IFOV (in radians). Convert altitude to metres: 600 km = 600 000 m. Convert IFOV to radians: 0.4 mrad = 0.4 × 10⁻³ rad = 0.0004 rad. Thus \(\text{GSD}=600{,}000\,\text{m}\times0.0004\,\text{rad}=240\,\text{m}.\) Option A is correct; the other values result from using incorrect conversion factors.

questions.question_text: According to Christaller’s Central Place Theory, which of the following best describes the pattern of towns that provide high-order services (e.g., specialized medical centers) in a region?

options.text: High-order centers are uniformly spaced in a regular grid, each serving a small, equal-sized market area.
They are located at the centers of low-order service areas, supplying the entire region with specialized services.
High-order centers are situated farther apart, each serving a large, overlapping market area, forming a hierarchical hexagonal pattern.
They occur randomly with no discernible spatial pattern, reflecting economic agglomeration.

questions.explanation: Central Place Theory predicts a nested hierarchy of settlements. Low‑order services (e.g., groceries) are abundant and close together, while high‑order services (e.g., specialized hospitals) are fewer, located farther apart, and each serves a larger hinterland. The spatial arrangement approximates a hexagonal lattice that minimizes travel distance for consumers. Option C captures this hierarchy; options A and B misstate the size of market areas, and D ignores the systematic pattern central place theory describes.

questions.question_text: A topographic map has a contour interval of 20 m. On the map the horizontal distance between two successive contour lines measured along a line of steepest ascent is 2.5 cm. If the map scale is 1 cm : 250 m, what is the average slope percentage between the contours?
questions.explanation: First convert the map distance to ground distance: 2.5 cm × 250 m/cm = 625 m. The rise between contours is the contour interval, 20 m. Slope % = (rise/run) × 100 = (20 m / 625 m) × 100 = 0.032 × 100 = 3.2 %. Therefore option C is correct. Options A and B underestimate the slope, while D overestimates it.

questions.question_text: Which volcanic landform is most characteristic of a continental rift zone where the lithosphere is being pulled apart?
questions.explanation: Continental rift zones are characterized by extensional tectonics that create linear fractures in the crust. Magma ascends through these fractures, producing elongated eruptions known as fissure vents or rift volcanoes. Shield volcanoes form over mantle plumes, stratovolcanoes at convergent margins, and calderas result from collapse of formerly large magma chambers, none of which are typical of rift settings.

questions.question_text: The climate data for City Y are: Avg. temperature (°C): Jan 12, Feb 13, Mar 15, Apr 18, May 22, Jun 25, Jul 27, Aug 26, Sep 23, Oct 18, Nov 14, Dec 11. Avg. precipitation (mm): Jan 80, Feb 70, Mar 55, Apr 45, May 30, Jun 20, Jul 15, Aug 20, Sep 35, Oct 55, Nov 70, Dec 80. Based on the Köppen climate classification, which type best describes City Y?
questions.explanation: Köppen criteria: • No month below 0 °C, so the climate is not a 'D' type. • Warmest month >22 °C (27 °C) → 'a' (hot summer). • Precipitation is year‑round with the driest month ≥30 mm, so there is no dry season, giving the 'f' designation. Combining these gives Cfa – a humid subtropical climate. Cfb requires the warmest month ≤22 °C, which does not apply. BWh requires arid conditions, which are absent.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most direct driver of the rapid expansion of primate cities in many sub‑Saharan African countries?

options.text: High rate of natural increase in rural areas

questions.explanation: Primate cities grow disproportionately because economic activities, especially high‑value services, cluster in a single dominant urban centre, creating agglomeration economies that attract migrants and investment. While rural natural increase and universities contribute indirectly, the primary catalyst is the concentration of jobs and services. Decentralization policies would tend to limit, not promote, primate‑city growth.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A remote‑sensing satellite flies at an altitude of 600 km and its sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.8 milliradians. Using the relationship \( \text{GSD}=H\times\text{IFOV} \), where GSD is the ground sample distance (spatial resolution) and H is the orbital altitude, what is the approximate spatial resolution on the ground?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox=\"0 0 320 200\" xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\"><rect x=\"130\" y=\"20\" width=\"60\" height=\"30\" fill=\"#aaa\" stroke=\"#000\"/><text x=\"160\" y=\"40\" font-size=\"12\" text-anchor=\"middle\" fill=\"#000\">Satellite</text><line x1=\"160\" y1=\"50\" x2=\"160\" y2=\"150\" stroke=\"red\" stroke-width=\"2\" marker-end=\"url(#arrow)\"/><rect x=\"120\" y=\"150\" width=\"80\" height=\"30\" fill=\"#cfc\" stroke=\"#000\"/><text x=\"160\" y=\"170\" font-size=\"12\" text-anchor=\"middle\" fill=\"#000\">Ground</text><defs><marker id=\"arrow\" markerWidth=\"6\" markerHeight=\"6\" refX=\"3\" refY=\"3\" orient=\"auto\"><path d=\"M0,0 L0,6 L6,3 z\" fill=\"red\"/></marker></defs></svg>
questions.explanation: Convert IFOV to radians: 0.8 milliradians = 0.8 × 10⁻³ rad = 0.0008 rad. Multiply by the altitude: GSD = 600 km × 0.0008 = 0.48 km. Converting to metres gives 0.48 km = 480 m. Option C matches this value. Option A expresses the same number in kilometres but the question asks for spatial resolution, commonly given in metres; Option B is three orders of magnitude too small, and Option D is ten times larger than the correct value.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, two points are plotted on a simple map where the y‑axis points north and the x‑axis points east. Point A is at (50,150) and point B is at (250,50). Calculate the bearing from point A to point B measured clockwise from true north. Use the bearing formula \( \theta = \arctan\left(\frac{\Delta E}{\Delta N}\right) \).
questions.diagram_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 200'><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='50' cy='150' r='4' fill='red'/><circle cx='250' cy='50' r='4' fill='blue'/><text x='35' y='155' font-size='12'>A</text><text x='260' y='55' font-size='12'>B</text></svg>
questions.explanation: First find the differences: \(\Delta E = 250-50 = 200\) m (east) and \(\Delta N = 50-150 = -100\) m (north). The bearing is given by \(\theta = \arctan2(\Delta E, \Delta N)\). Using \(\arctan2(200,-100)\) gives an angle of 116.6° measured clockwise from north. Option A matches this value. Options B, C and D correspond to the other quadrantal equivalents and are therefore incorrect.

questions.question_text: Which type of volcano is most commonly formed at convergent (subduction) plate boundaries and is associated with highly viscous, silica‑rich magma that often produces explosive eruptions?
questions.explanation: Subduction zones generate magma that evolves to high silica (andesitic to rhyolitic) compositions, which are viscous. This leads to the buildup of pressure and explosive eruptions characteristic of composite or stratovolcanoes. Shield volcanoes are built from low‑silica basaltic lava at divergent settings; cinder cones are smaller eruptive features, and lava plateaus are broad basaltic flows. Hence, option B is correct.

questions.question_text: On the day of the March equinox, the solar declination \(\delta\) is 0°. For a location at latitude \(\phi = 35^{\circ}\) N, what is the solar altitude angle \(\alpha\) at solar noon? Use the formula \(\alpha = 90^{\circ} - |\phi - \delta|\).
questions.explanation: Insert the values into the formula: \(\alpha = 90^{\circ} - |35^{\circ} - 0^{\circ}| = 90^{\circ} - 35^{\circ} = 55^{\circ}\). Therefore the solar altitude at noon on the equinox for 35° N is 55°, which corresponds to option A. The other options result from incorrect subtraction or forgetting the absolute value.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 2.5 million in the year 2020. The annual natural increase rate is 2.1 % (continuous growth). Assuming no migration, what will be the projected population in 2025? Use the continuous growth formula \(P = P_{0} e^{rt}\).
questions.explanation: Here \(P_{0}=2.5\) million, \(r=0.021\) yr⁻¹, \(t=5\) yr. Compute the exponent: \(rt = 0.021 \times 5 = 0.105\). Then \(e^{0.105} \approx 1.1106\). Multiply: \(P = 2.5 \times 1.1106 \approx 2.7765\) million, which rounds to 2.78 million. Option A matches this result. The other options arise from rounding errors or using a discrete compounding formula.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A remote‑sensing satellite orbits at an altitude of 800 km above the Earth’s surface and carries a sensor with an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.5 milliradians. What is the ground sample distance (GSD) represented by one pixel of the sensor on the ground?
questions.explanation: Convert the altitude to metres: \(H = 800\,\text{km} = 800,000\,\text{m}\). Convert IFOV to radians: \(0.5\,\text{mrad} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{rad} = 0.0005\,\text{rad}\). The ground sample distance is \(GSD = H \times IFOV = 800,000\,\text{m} \times 0.0005 = 400\,\text{m}\). Therefore, option A is correct. Options B, C and D result from using incorrect unit conversions or arithmetic errors.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, two towns A and B are plotted on a map. The map scale is 1 cm : 2 km. The coordinates (in cm) are A(40, 30) and B(130, 110) where the y‑axis points north and the x‑axis points east. What is the true bearing from A to B, expressed as a three‑digit bearing, and the ground distance between the towns?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='170' x2='130' y2='90' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='40' cy='170' r='4' fill='red'/><text x='30' y='185' font-size='12'>A</text><circle cx='130' cy='90' r='4' fill='blue'/><text x='135' y='85' font-size='12'>B</text></svg>
questions.latex: \(\Delta x = 90\text{ cm},\ \Delta y = 80\text{ cm},\ \text{bearing}=\arctan\left(\frac{\Delta x}{\Delta y}\right)\)
questions.explanation:
Δx = 130 cm – 40 cm = 90 cm (east). Δy = 110 cm – 30 cm = 80 cm (north).

Map distance = \(\sqrt{90^2 + 80^2}=\sqrt{14500}\approx120.4\) cm. With a scale of 1 cm : 2 km, true distance = 120.4 cm × 2 km = 240.8 km ≈ 241 km.

Bearing is measured clockwise from north: \(	heta = \arctan\left( rac{\Delta x}{\Delta y}ight)=\arctan\left( rac{90}{80}ight)\approx48.4^{\circ}\). Rounded to a three‑digit bearing gives 048°.

Option A matches both the bearing and the distance; the other options contain either an incorrect bearing or an incorrect scaling of the map distance.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements best explains why basaltic magma generated at a mid‑ocean ridge typically erupts effusively rather than explosively?

options.text: It has low silica content, resulting in low viscosity and allowing gases to escape easily.
It contains high water content, which increases explosivity.
It crystallizes at high temperatures, forming a solid plug that traps gases.
It interacts with continental crust, producing ash‑rich eruptions.

questions.explanation: Basaltic magma is mafic, containing < 55 % SiO₂. The low silica makes the melt fluid (low viscosity), so volatile gases can exsolve and rise smoothly, producing gentle lava flows (effusive eruptions). High water content (option B) would raise explosivity, but basaltic mid‑ocean‑ridge magma is relatively dry. Option C describes high‑viscosity magmas that form plugs, which is characteristic of rhyolitic systems, not basalt. Option D involves crustal contamination that can increase silica and explosivity, which is not typical at divergent boundaries.

questions.question_text: For a location at latitude \(\phi = 45^{\circ}\) north on the day of the summer solstice (\(\delta = +23.5^{\circ}\)), what is the approximate length of daylight in hours? Use the hour‑angle formula \(\cos H_0 = -\tan\phi\,\tan\delta\) and daylight \(= \frac{2H_0}{15^{\circ}}\).
questions.explanation:
First compute the tangent values: \(	an45^{\circ}=1\) and \(	an23.5^{\circ}\approx0.433\).

\(\cos H_0 = -1	imes0.433 = -0.433\).

The hour angle is \(H_0 = \arccos(-0.433) \approx 115.7^{\circ}\).

Daylight hours = \( rac{2	imes115.7^{\circ}}{15^{\circ}} =  rac{231.4}{15} \approx 15.43\) h, i.e. about 15.4 h. Option C matches this result; the other options are far from the calculated value.

questions.question_text: A city had a population of 1.2 million in 2015. It experienced an annual natural increase of 1.2 % (continuous growth) and net migration of +8 000 persons per year. What will be its estimated population at the end of 2020? Use the continuous growth formula \(P = P_0 e^{rt}\) and add migration linearly.
questions.explanation:
Natural increase (continuous):
\(P_0 = 1{,}200{,}000\), \(r = 0.012\), \(t = 5\) yr.
\(P_{	ext{nat}} = 1{,}200{,}000 	imes e^{0.012	imes5}=1{,}200{,}000	imes e^{0.06}\approx1{,}200{,}000	imes1.061836 = 1{,}274{,}203\).

Migration over 5 years: \(8{,}000	imes5 = 40{,}000\).

Total population = 1{,}274{,}203 + 40{,}000 ≈ 1{,}314{,}203 ≈ 1.31 million. Option B is correct; the other options either ignore migration or mis‑apply the growth formula.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A satellite sensor has a swath width of 120 km and an orbital repeat cycle of 16 days. If the sensor's instantaneous field of view (IFOV) is 0.7 mrad and the satellite altitude is 700 km, calculate the ground sample distance (GSD) in metres and determine how many distinct orbital passes are required to achieve 100 % coverage of a square study area measuring 240 km × 240 km, assuming adjacent swaths are tiled without overlap.
questions.explanation:
Ground Sample Distance:
\(	ext{GSD}=0.7	ext{ mrad}	imes700{,}000	ext{ m}=0.0007	imes700{,}000=490	ext{ m}\).

The study area side = 240 km. Each pass covers a swath of 120 km, so the number of passes needed = \(\lceil 240/120 ceil = 2\). No overlap is required, so two adjacent passes give full coverage. Option A states both the correct GSD and the correct number of passes; the other options mismatch one or both values.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, two landmarks P and Q are shown on a topographic map. The map scale is 1 cm : 250 m. The coordinates of P are (45 mm, 80 mm) and Q are (125 mm, 140 mm) measured from the lower‑left corner of the map, where the y‑axis points north. Determine the bearing from P to Q (expressed as a three‑figure bearing) and the ground distance between the two landmarks.
questions.diagram_svg:
<svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 200'>
  <rect width='320' height='200' fill='white' stroke='black'/>
  <circle cx='70' cy='120' r='5' fill='red'/>
  <text x='70' y='115' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>P</text>
  <circle cx='190' cy='80' r='5' fill='blue'/>
  <text x='190' y='75' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Q</text>
  <defs>
    <marker id='arrow' markerWidth='10' markerHeight='10' refX='0' refY='3' orient='auto'>
      <path d='M0,0 L0,6 L9,3 z' fill='green'/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <line x1='70' y1='120' x2='190' y2='80' stroke='green' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arrow)'/>
  <line x1='20' y1='180' x2='20' y2='20' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/>
  <polygon points='15,20 25,20 20,10' fill='black'/>
  <text x='25' y='30' font-size='12'>N</text>
</svg>
questions.explanation: Convert the coordinates from millimetres to centimetres: P (4.5 cm, 8.0 cm), Q (12.5 cm, 14.0 cm). \(\Delta x = 12.5-4.5 = 8.0\) cm, \(\Delta y = 14.0-8.0 = 6.0\) cm. Map distance \(d_{map}=\sqrt{8^2+6^2}=10\) cm. With a scale of 1 cm : 250 m, ground distance \(d_{ground}=10\times250=2 500\) m. Bearing is measured clockwise from north: \(\theta = \arctan(\Delta x/\Delta y)=\arctan(8/6)\approx53.1^{\circ}\). Expressed as a three‑figure bearing this is 053°. Options B and D use the wrong bearing, C uses a different unit for distance. Hence option A is correct.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best explains why volcanic eruptions at continental rift zones tend to produce basaltic lava flows that are less explosive than those generated at convergent plate boundaries?

options.text: The mantle‑derived magma ascends through relatively thin continental crust, loses little dissolved water, and has low silica, resulting in low‑viscosity basaltic lava that erupts effusively.
Rift‑zone magma interacts with abundant groundwater, increasing volatile content and explosivity.
Continental rifts are characterised by high‑pressure magma chambers that suppress gas expansion.
The thicker crust at rifts causes magma to cool rapidly, producing highly explosive ash plumes.

questions.explanation: Continental rift zones involve extensional tectonics where mantle material rises through a relatively thin crust. The magma is typically basaltic, low in silica and water‑soluble volatiles, giving it a low viscosity. Consequently it can flow readily and erupts effusively with little explosive behaviour. In contrast, convergent margins generate magma that has assimilated crustal material and trapped volatiles, often yielding more silica‑rich, viscous magmas that erupt explosively. Options B, C and D describe conditions that would increase explosivity, not reduce it, so they are incorrect.

questions.question_text: At latitude 60° N, the summer solstice day length is about 18.5 hours while at latitude 20° N it is about 13.5 hours. Which of the following statements correctly accounts for this difference?

options.text: The axial tilt of the Earth causes the Sun’s declination to be larger relative to the horizon at higher latitudes during the solstice, extending the period the Sun stays above the horizon.
Atmospheric refraction is stronger at lower latitudes, shortening the apparent daylight period there.
Higher latitudes receive more solar radiation, which lengthens the day through increased atmospheric heating.
The Earth rotates faster at the equator, reducing the apparent day length at low latitudes.

questions.explanation: During the summer solstice the Sun’s declination equals the Earth’s axial tilt (≈23.5°). At 60° N the Sun’s path stays well above the horizon, giving a long arc and therefore a long daylight interval (≈18.5 h). At 20° N the Sun’s path is closer to the horizon, so the daylight interval is shorter (≈13.5 h). The difference is a direct consequence of the geometry of Earth’s tilt, not atmospheric refraction, solar heating, or variations in rotational speed. Hence option A correctly explains the observed disparity.

questions.question_text: A city’s population follows the logistic growth model \(P(t)=\frac{K}{1+ae^{-rt}}\) where \(K=2\,000\,000\) persons, \(r=0.04\) yr\(^{-1}\) and the initial population at \(t=0\) is 500 000. In which year will the population first exceed 1 500 000? Assume \(t\) is measured in years from 2020.
questions.explanation: First find \(a\) using the initial condition:\[500\,000 = \frac{2\,000\,000}{1+a}\]\[1+a = \frac{2\,000\,000}{500\,000}=4 \Rightarrow a=3.\]Set \(P(t)=1\,500\,000\):\[1\,500\,000 = \frac{2\,000\,000}{1+3e^{-0.04t}}\]\[1+3e^{-0.04t}=\frac{2\,000\,000}{1\,500\,000}=\frac{4}{3}\]\[3e^{-0.04t}=\frac{4}{3}-1=\frac{1}{3}\]\[e^{-0.04t}=\frac{1}{9}\]\[-0.04t=\ln\left(\frac{1}{9}\right)=-\ln9\]\[t=\frac{\ln9}{0.04}\approx\frac{2.1972}{0.04}=54.93\text{ yr}.\]Adding this to the base year 2020 gives about 2075. The first full year when the population exceeds 1.5 million is therefore 2075. Option C matches this result.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A multispectral sensor records reflectance values in the red (R) and near‑infrared (NIR) bands. The Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) is defined as \(\text{NDVI}=\frac{NIR-R}{NIR+R}\). Which of the following interpretations of an NDVI value of 0.75 is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: NDVI values range from -1 to +1. Values close to +1 (typically >0.6) correspond to vigorous, photosynthetically active vegetation because healthy plants strongly reflect NIR and absorb red light. A value of 0.75 therefore signals dense, healthy vegetation. Values near zero or negative represent bare soil, rocks, water, or non‑vegetated surfaces. Hence option A correctly interprets the NDVI measurement.

questions.question_text: A topographic map of a hilly region is drawn at a scale of 1 cm : 50 000 m. Using a map‑meter, the meandering course of a stream is measured to be 13.6 cm. What is the actual length of the stream on the ground, expressed in kilometres (km) to one decimal place?
questions.explanation: The map scale 1 cm : 50 000 m means that 1 cm on the map equals 50 000 m = 0.5 km on the ground.\nActual length = map length \(\times\) ground distance per centimetre = 13.6 cm \(\times\) 0.5 km/cm = 6.8 km.\nOption C matches this value. Options A, B and D are either too low or too high, arising from using an incorrect conversion factor.

questions.question_text: Which of the following volcanic landforms is most closely associated with the eruption of highly viscous, silica‑rich magma and typically displays a steep‑sided, conical shape with alternating layers of lava flows, ash, and tephra?
questions.explanation:
Silica‑rich (high‑viscosity) magma tends to trap gases, producing explosive eruptions that lay down alternating sequences of lava, ash and tephra. These build a steep, symmetrical cone known as a stratovolcano.
A shield volcano (A) is formed by low‑viscosity basaltic lava and has gentle slopes.
A cinder cone (B) is smaller and comprised mainly of fragmented pyroclasts.
A lava plateau (D) results from extensive flood basalt flows, not from steep conical building. Therefore, option C is correct.

questions.question_text:
A location records the following climate data (average monthly values):

- Mean temperature (°C): 12, 13, 15, 18, 22, 25, 27, 26, 23, 19, 15, 13
- Monthly precipitation (mm): 20, 25, 40, 80, 150, 220, 250, 240, 180, 100, 45, 30
Based on the Köppen climate classification, which type best describes this climate?
questions.explanation: All monthly mean temperatures are above 18 °C, satisfying the "tropical" requirement.\nTotal annual precipitation = 1500 mm. The Köppen dry‑month threshold is \(100 - \frac{P_{ann}}{25} = 100 - \frac{1500}{25} = 40\) mm. The driest month receives 20 mm, which is less than both 60 mm and the threshold, indicating a distinct dry season. Hence the climate is classified as tropical savanna, "Aw".\nOption A requires no month below 60 mm; option B requires a short dry season with the driest month ≥ 60 mm but still below the threshold; option D is a temperate climate with colder months. Therefore, option C is correct.

questions.question_text: In a country the populations of its cities follow the rank‑size rule \(P_n = \frac{P_1}{n}\). If the 5th‑largest city has a population of 800 000, what is the estimated population of the largest city?
questions.explanation: The rank‑size formula gives \(P_5 = \frac{P_1}{5}\). Rearranging, \(P_1 = 5 \times P_5 = 5 \times 800\,000 = 4\,000\,000\).\nThus the largest city is expected to have about 4 million inhabitants, which corresponds to option B. Options A, C and D result from using incorrect multiplication factors (e.g., 4×, 6×, or 7.5×).

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor orbits at an altitude of 700 km and has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.04 mrad in both the horizontal and vertical directions. What is the approximate ground‑resolution (size of one pixel) produced by the sensor, expressed in metres?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='80' y='60' width='160' height='80' fill='none' stroke='black'/><line x1='160' y1='0' x2='160' y2='200' stroke='gray' stroke-dasharray='4 2'/><line x1='0' y1='100' x2='320' y2='100' stroke='gray' stroke-dasharray='4 2'/><text x='165' y='20' font-size='12'>IFOV = 0.04 mrad</text><text x='10' y='115' font-size='12'>Altitude = 700 km</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
Ground resolution (size of one pixel) is obtained by multiplying the sensor altitude \(H\) by the IFOV angle \(	heta\) (in radians).
Convert the IFOV: 0.04 mrad = 0.04 × 10⁻³ rad = 4	imes10⁻⁵ rad.
Altitude \(H = 700\,	ext{km} = 700\,000\,	ext{m}\).
\[	ext{Ground size}=700\,000 	imes 4	imes10^{-5}=28\,	ext{m}\]
Because the IFOV is the same in both directions, the pixel is 28 m × 28 m, which is option A. The other options arise from using an incorrect conversion factor (e.g., treating 0.04 mrad as 0.04 rad) or mis‑placing a decimal point.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, two towns, Alpha and Beta, are plotted on a map. The map scale is 1 cm : 5 km. The coordinates of Alpha are (30 cm, 40 cm) and those of Beta are (80 cm, 90 cm) measured from the origin where the x‑axis points east and the y‑axis points north. Calculate the bearing from Alpha to Beta (to the nearest degree) and the ground distance between the towns.
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'> <circle cx='60' cy='80' r='5' fill='red'/> <text x='60' y='75' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Alpha</text> <circle cx='160' cy='180' r='5' fill='blue'/> <text x='160' y='175' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Beta</text> <line x1='60' y1='80' x2='160' y2='180' stroke='black' stroke-dasharray='4'/> </svg>
questions.explanation: Δx = 80 cm - 30 cm = 50 cm, Δy = 90 cm - 40 cm = 50 cm. Bearing = arctan(Δx/Δy) = arctan(1) = 45° clockwise from north. Map distance = √(Δx²+Δy²) = √(50²+50²) ≈ 70.71 cm. Ground distance = 70.71 cm × 5 km/cm ≈ 353.55 km ≈ 354 km. Options B and D use the wrong ground distance; C and D use the opposite bearing (135°). Hence option A is correct.

questions.question_text: Which volcanic landform is most typically associated with high‑silica (rhyolitic) magma erupting in a continental interior setting?
questions.explanation: Rhyolitic magma is highly viscous and gas‑rich, producing extremely explosive eruptions that often empty a magma chamber. The subsequent collapse of the overlying rock forms a large, circular depression called a caldera. Shield volcanoes and cinder cones are usually basaltic; stratovolcanoes are intermediate to felsic but the classic large‑scale collapse structure linked to rhyolite is a caldera.

questions.question_text: For a location at latitude \(\phi = 30^{\circ}\), on the day when the solar declination \(\delta = +23.5^{\circ}\), calculate the solar elevation angle at solar noon. Use the formula \(h = 90^{\circ} - |\phi - \delta|\).
questions.explanation: Compute the absolute difference: |30° - 23.5°| = 6.5°. Then \(h = 90° - 6.5° = 83.5°\), which rounds to 84°. Options A, C, and D result from incorrect subtraction or ignoring the absolute value.

questions.question_text: A city’s population follows the logistic growth model \(P(t)=\frac{K}{1+ae^{-rt}}\) where the carrying capacity \(K = 2{,}000{,}000\) persons and intrinsic growth rate \(r = 0.04\) yr\(^{-1}\). If the population at \(t=0\) is 500{,}000, what is the population after 10 years? (Round to the nearest thousand.)

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: Which sensor characteristic makes hyperspectral imagery particularly advantageous over multispectral imagery for detecting subtle variations in vegetation chlorophyll content?
questions.explanation: Hyperspectral sensors record many narrow, contiguous wavelength bands, allowing precise identification of narrow absorption features of chlorophyll. Multispectral sensors have few broad bands, which can miss subtle spectral nuances. Spatial resolution, swath width, and revisit time affect mapping efficiency but not the ability to discriminate fine spectral differences.

questions.question_text: On a topographic map having a scale of 1 cm : 5 km, two towns A and B are located at grid coordinates (12 cm, 8 cm) and (28 cm, 22 cm) respectively. What is the bearing from A to B measured clockwise from true north?
questions.explanation: First compute the differences in the east‑west (ΔE) and north‑south (ΔN) directions: ΔE = 28 cm - 12 cm = 16 cm (east), ΔN = 22 cm - 8 cm = 14 cm (north). The bearing θ measured clockwise from north is given by \( \theta = \tan^{-1}(\Delta E/\Delta N) \). Substituting, \( \theta = \tan^{-1}(16/14) = \tan^{-1}(1.1429) \approx 48.6^{\circ} \). Rounded to the nearest whole degree the bearing is 49°, which corresponds to option B. Options A, C and D are obtained by adding or subtracting roughly 10°‑20°, which do not match the calculated value.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, the divergent plate boundary shows upwelling mantle material that partially melts to form basaltic magma which intrudes the crust. Which process primarily drives the generation of this magma?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='80' y1='150' x2='240' y2='50' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='240,50 260,70 240,90' fill='orange'/><text x='250' y='80' font-size='12' fill='black'>Magma</text></svg>
questions.explanation: At a divergent (spreading) boundary the lithosphere is pulled apart, causing the underlying asthenosphere to rise. As the mantle material ascends it experiences a pressure drop while its temperature remains nearly constant. The reduction in pressure lowers the solidus of the peridotite, causing partial melting – this is decompression melting. Flux melting requires addition of volatiles (typical of subduction zones), heat transfer melting requires external heating (e.g., intrusion of hotter material), and crystallization melting is a reverse process. Therefore the correct mechanism is decompression melting (option A).

questions.question_text: During the summer months, the Indian subcontinent experiences a strong southwest monsoon. Which of the following mechanisms best explains the onset of this monsoon?

options.text: Differential heating creates a low pressure over land that draws moist air from the ocean.
Cold ocean currents cool the adjacent atmosphere, forcing air toward the continent.
Trade winds reverse direction due to seasonal changes in the Coriolis force.
Increased solar radiation over the ocean generates a high‑pressure system that pushes air inland.

questions.explanation: In summer the landmass heats up much faster than the surrounding ocean, producing a deep low‑pressure trough over the Indian subcontinent. The relatively higher pressure over the Indian Ocean forces moist maritime air to flow landward. As this air ascends over the heated terrain, it cools and precipitates, creating the characteristic monsoon rains. Options B, C and D describe processes that either cool the air or involve wind reversals unrelated to the primary land‑sea thermal contrast, so they are incorrect.

questions.question_text: A city had a population of 1,200,000 in 2015 and has been growing at an annual continuous rate of 2.5 %. In which year will its population first exceed 2,000,000?
questions.explanation:
The continuous growth model is \( P(t) = P_0 e^{rt} \) where \(P_0 = 1.2\times10^6\), \(r = 0.025\) yr⁻¹ and \(t\) is the number of years after 2015.

Set \(P(t) = 2.0\times10^6\) and solve for \(t\):
\[ 2.0\times10^6 = 1.2\times10^6 e^{0.025t} \]
\[ \frac{2.0}{1.2} = e^{0.025t} \]
\[ \ln\left(\frac{5}{3}\right) = 0.025t \]
\[ t = \frac{\ln(5/3)}{0.025} \approx \frac{0.5108}{0.025} \approx 20.43\text{ yr}. \]
Adding 20.43 yr to 2015 gives roughly mid‑2025. However, the population exceeds 2 million only after the full 20.43 yr have passed, i.e., sometime in 2035 (since the integer year must be after 2025). Checking the options, 2035 is the first year listed that satisfies the condition. Therefore, option C is correct. Options A and B are too early, and D is later than necessary.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A GIS analyst needs to map the width of a small river that is approximately 30 m across. Which sensor type provides the most appropriate spatial resolution to reliably detect the river width?

options.text: Panchromatic satellite sensor with 30 m ground sampling distance (GSD)
High‑resolution aerial photography with 0.5 m GSD
Thermal infrared sensor with 100 m GSD

questions.explanation: To detect a feature, the sensor's ground sampling distance should be at most one‑half of the feature size (Nyquist criterion). For a 30 m river, a GSD of ≤ 15 m would be the theoretical minimum. While the 10 m multispectral sensor (option B) meets this criterion, its 10 m pixels would only provide two to three pixels across the river, making width estimation imprecise. The 0.5 m aerial imagery (option C) yields about 60 pixels across the river, allowing accurate delineation and measurement. Options A (30 m) and D (100 m) are far coarser than the river width and would miss the river entirely. Hence, high‑resolution aerial photography is the best choice.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, two locations A and B are plotted on a map. The map scale is 1 cm : 4 km. The coordinates of A are (2 cm, 2 cm) and B are (10 cm, 8 cm) measured from the origin. What is the straight‑line distance between A and B in kilometres?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='50' cy='150' r='5' fill='red'/><circle cx='250' cy='50' r='5' fill='red'/><text x='45' y='165' font-size='12' fill='black'>A</text><text x='255' y='65' font-size='12' fill='black'>B</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
Difference in the x‑direction: \(\Delta x = 10\,	ext{cm} - 2\,	ext{cm} = 8\,	ext{cm}\).
ewline Difference in the y‑direction: \(\Delta y = 8\,	ext{cm} - 2\,	ext{cm} = 6\,	ext{cm}\).
ewline Using the Pythagorean theorem, the map distance is \[ d_{	ext{map}} = \sqrt{8^{2}+6^{2}} = \sqrt{64+36}=\sqrt{100}=10\,	ext{cm}. \]
ewline The scale states 1 cm = 4 km, therefore the real distance is \(10\,	ext{cm}	imes4\,	ext{km/cm}=40\,	ext{km}.
ewline Option B matches this result, while the other choices arise from incorrect multiplication or distance calculation.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes the compositional difference between oceanic and continental crust?

options.text: Oceanic crust is richer in silica and less dense than continental crust.
