Since 2.10×10^{6} > 2.8×10^{5}, the continental crust is supported by a much larger buoyant force. Hence option A is correct. Options B and C underestimate the effect of the larger thickness and density contrast; D is unnecessary because gravity cancels in a comparative statement.

questions.question_text:
The following table shows the long‑term monthly averages for City Z.

Month | Avg Temp (°C) | Avg Precip (mm)
--- | --- | ---
Jan | 22 | 78
Feb | 23 | 65
Mar | 25 | 70
Apr | 27 | 80
May | 28 | 120
Jun | 27 | 150
Jul | 26 | 140
Aug | 26 | 130
Sep | 25 | 110
Oct | 24 | 90
Nov | 23 | 85
Dec | 22 | 80

Based on the Köppen climate classification, which type best describes City Z?
questions.explanation: In the Köppen system, a tropical climate (type A) requires that all monthly mean temperatures exceed 18 °C, which is true for City Z. The tropical rainforest (Af) subtype further requires that each month receives at least 60 mm of precipitation. All months of City Z have precipitation ≥65 mm, satisfying the Af criterion. The monsoon (Am) subtype allows a short dry season with a month below 60 mm, which does not occur here. The savanna (Aw) subtype has a pronounced dry season with much lower rainfall, also absent. Therefore the correct classification is Af, option A. Options B, C, and D are ruled out by the temperature and rainfall thresholds.

questions.question_text:
In the diagram below, the concentric circles represent zones of a city according to the Burgess concentric zone model. Which zone is most likely to contain a mixture of residential and small‑scale commercial uses?

In the diagram, Zone 1 is the innermost circle, followed by Zone 2, Zone 3, Zone 4 and Zone 5 outward.
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='80' stroke='black' fill='none'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='50' stroke='black' fill='none'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='20' stroke='black' fill='none'/></svg>
questions.explanation: In the Burgess model, Zone 2 – the transition zone – surrounds the central business district and typically hosts a mix of low‑income residential housing and small‑scale commercial enterprises that serve the nearby population. Zone 1 is dominated by high‑intensity commercial activities; Zone 3 is primarily low‑income residential; Zone 5 is suburban residential with commuters. Hence option B is correct.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A satellite sensor is equipped with a detector array of 1024 × 1024 pixels and captures a swath that is 30 km wide at nadir. Assuming the instantaneous field of view is uniform across the array, calculate the ground sampling distance (GSD) in metres. Which of the following values is closest to the GSD?
questions.explanation:
The ground sampling distance is the ground distance covered by one pixel. For a swath width (SW) of 30 km = 30 000 m and N = 1024 pixels across the swath, 
GSD = 30 000 m ÷ 1024 ≈ 29.30 m. The closest answer is 29 m (option B). Option A underestimates the pixel size, while options C and D overestimate it.

questions.question_text: A topographic map has a scale of 1 cm : 250 km. On the map the straight‑line distance between Town A and Town B measures 9.6 cm. What is the actual ground distance between the two towns?
questions.explanation: The map scale indicates that 1 cm on the map equals 250 km on the ground. Multiply the measured map distance by the scale factor: 9.6 cm × 250 km / cm = 2,400 km. The other options arise from common mis‑multiplications (e.g., using 225 km or adding extra zeros). Hence option A is correct.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, point P is located at the origin. A line is drawn from P to Q. Using the shown north arrow, determine the bearing of line PQ measured clockwise from true north.
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='160' y1='180' x2='260' y2='80' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='155,180 165,180 160,170' fill='black'/><!-- north arrow --> <text x='160' y='165' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>N</text></svg>
questions.explanation: The line PQ slopes upward to the right, forming a 45° angle with the vertical north direction. Bearings are measured clockwise from north, so the bearing is 045°. Option B would be due east, C southeast, and D due south, none of which match the diagram.

questions.question_text: The lithostatic pressure at a depth of 15 km within the continental crust can be approximated by \(P = \rho g h\). Given an average crustal density \(\rho = 2.70 \;\text{g cm}^{-3}\) and \(g = 9.8 \;\text{m s}^{-2}\), calculate the pressure in megapascals (MPa). (1 Pa = 1 N m\(^{-2}\))
questions.explanation: First convert density to SI units: 2.70 g cm⁻³ = 2700 kg m⁻³. Depth \(h = 15\,\text{km} = 15,000\,\text{m}\). \(P = 2700 \times 9.8 \times 15,000 = 396,900,000\,\text{Pa}\). Converting to MPa (1 MPa = 10⁶ Pa) gives \(396.9\,\text{MPa}\), which rounds to 397 MPa. The other choices result from using incorrect density values or rounding errors.

questions.question_text: The population of City X increased from 2.05 million in 2015 to 2.30 million in 2020. Assuming exponential growth \(P = P_0 e^{rt}\), where \(t\) is in years, estimate the average annual growth rate \(r\) (expressed as a percentage to two decimal places).
questions.explanation: Set \(P/P_0 = 2.30/2.05 = 1.12195\). Taking natural logs: \(\ln(1.12195) = 0.1155\). With \(t = 5\) years, \(r = \ln(P/P_0)/t = 0.1155/5 = 0.0231\). Convert to a percentage: 0.0231 × 100 = 2.31 %. The other options arise from mis‑reading the ratio or failing to divide by the time interval.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: Which of the following statements correctly describes the difference between passive and active remote sensing instruments?

options.text: Passive sensors detect natural energy reflected or emitted by the Earth's surface, whereas active sensors emit their own energy and measure the returned signal.
Passive sensors emit microwave pulses and record the backscatter, while active sensors rely on solar radiation.
Both passive and active sensors require sunlight, but passive sensors have higher spatial resolution.
Active sensors can only operate in the infrared region, whereas passive sensors work in the microwave region.

questions.explanation: Passive instruments (e.g., radiometers) simply record radiation that originates from the Sun or the Earth itself. Active instruments (e.g., RADAR, LiDAR) generate their own illumination—microwave pulses or laser light—and sense the reflected energy. Options B, C, and D misstate the energy source or wavelength constraints, making them incorrect.

questions.question_text: On a topographic map the contour interval is 20 m. Points A and B are 3 cm apart on the map measured along a line bearing 045°. The map scale is 1 cm : 5 km. What are the straight‑line ground distance between A and B (in km) and the minimum possible elevation difference (in m) between the two points?
questions.explanation: Map distance = map measurement × scale factor = 3 cm × 5 km / cm = 15 km. The smallest elevation change occurs when the two points lie on adjacent contour lines, which differ by one contour interval = 20 m. Hence the correct pair is 15 km and 20 m.

questions.question_text: Which statement correctly explains why basaltic magma typically produces less explosive eruptions than rhyolitic magma?

options.text: Basaltic magma has lower silica content, resulting in lower viscosity \(\eta\); gases escape easily, reducing explosivity.
Basaltic magma is hotter, which increases viscosity and traps gases, leading to higher explosivity.
Basaltic magma contains more dissolved water, which raises viscosity and promotes explosive eruptions.
Basaltic magma solidifies at deeper levels, causing pressure buildup and violent explosions.

questions.explanation: Viscosity of magma increases strongly with silica (SiO₂) content. Basaltic magma is mafic (low SiO₂), so its viscosity \(\eta\) is low; volatile gases can escape without building high pressure, leading to effusive, less explosive eruptions. Rhyolitic magma is felsic (high SiO₂), very viscous, traps gases, and erupts explosively. Options B, C, D describe opposite or unrelated effects, so they are incorrect.

questions.question_text: A town’s population was 12.0 million in 2015 and grew to 14.4 million in 2021. Assuming exponential growth \(P(t)=P_0 e^{rt}\), what was the average annual growth rate (to two decimal places, %)?
questions.explanation: Using \(P(t)=P_0 e^{rt}\): \(r = \frac{1}{t}\ln\left(\frac{P_t}{P_0}\right)\). Here \(t = 2021-2015 = 6\) years, \(P_0 = 12.0\) million, \(P_t = 14.4\) million. \(\frac{P_t}{P_0}=1.20\). \(\ln(1.20)=0.182321\). \(r = 0.182321/6 = 0.030387\) yr⁻¹. Converting to percent: \(0.030387\times100 = 3.04\%\). Option B matches this value.

questions.question_text: According to the bid‑rent theory, which land‑use pattern best illustrates the typical decline of land value with increasing distance from the central business district (CBD) in a monocentric city?

options.text: High‑rise residential near the CBD, low‑rise residential far away.
Industrial zones nearest the CBD, residential zones farthest.
Commercial retail zones adjacent to the CBD, followed by progressively lower‑intensity residential zones outward.
Agricultural land interspersed within the urban core.

questions.explanation: Bid‑rent theory predicts that the highest land rents are closest to the CBD, where commercial retail uses pay the most. Moving outward, rent declines, so lower‑intensity residential uses become economically viable. Option C correctly reflects this concentric gradient. Options A and B misplace land uses relative to rent gradients, and D contradicts the urban‑core land‑use hierarchy.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor’s instantaneous field of view (IFOV) subtends an angle α = 0.05°. The sensor is at an altitude H = 800 km above the Earth's surface and points directly nadir. Using the relation \(\text{Ground Resolution}=2H\tan\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)\), calculate the approximate size of one pixel on the ground (in metres).
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="160" y1="180" x2="160" y2="20" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="160" y1="180" x2="260" y2="180" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M160,180 L260,180 L260,20" fill="none" stroke="grey" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4 2"/><text x="210" y="190" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">Altitude H</text><text x="170" y="100" font-size="12" transform="rotate(-90 170,100)">α</text></svg>
questions.explanation: Convert α to radians: \(\alpha=0.05^{\circ}=0.05\times\frac{\pi}{180}=8.7266\times10^{-4}\) rad. Half of it: \(\alpha/2=4.3633\times10^{-4}\) rad. For small angles, \(\tan(\alpha/2)\approx\alpha/2\). Thus \(\tan(\alpha/2)\approx4.3633\times10^{-4}\). Altitude H = 800 km = 800 000 m. Ground resolution = 2×800 000 m×4.3633×10^{-4} ≈ 698 m, which rounds to about 700 m. Option C is the closest value.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a topographic map has a scale of 1 cm : 2.5 km. Points A and B are 4.2 cm apart on the map and the line AB is drawn at a bearing of 120°. What is the true ground distance between A and B, and what is its azimuth measured clockwise from true north?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='60' y1='150' x2='260' y2='50' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='60' cy='150' r='5' fill='red'/><circle cx='260' cy='50' r='5' fill='red'/><text x='60' y='165' font-size='12' fill='black'>A</text><text x='260' y='45' font-size='12' fill='black'>B</text></svg>
questions.explanation: The map scale converts centimeters to kilometres directly: \(4.2\ \text{cm}\times2.5\ \text{km/cm}=10.5\ \text{km}\). The bearing shown on the map is already measured clockwise from true north, so the azimuth is 120°. Option A matches both the distance and bearing; the other options either use an incorrect conversion factor or alter the bearing.

questions.question_text: Which statement best explains why basaltic magma usually produces effusive lava flows rather than explosive eruptions?

options.text: It has low silica content, resulting in low viscosity and easy gas escape.
It contains a high percentage of dissolved water, raising the eruption temperature.
It originates from the continental crust, which suppresses explosive behavior.
It cools rapidly, forming a solid crust that traps gases.

questions.explanation: Basaltic magma is mafic, meaning it has low silica (SiO₂) and therefore low viscosity. Gases can escape readily, preventing pressure buildup and leading to gentle, effusive flows. Option B is incorrect because high water content actually promotes explosivity. Option C is wrong – basaltic magma typically derives from the mantle, not continental crust. Option D describes a process that would increase explosivity, not decrease it.

questions.question_text: A city at 35° N latitude records an average summer high of 32 °C and an average winter low of 8 °C. Assuming the annual temperature variation follows a simple sinusoidal curve, what is the mean annual temperature for the city?
questions.explanation: For a sinusoidal temperature model, the mean annual temperature is the average of the maximum and minimum yearly temperatures: \((32+8)/2 = 20\) °C. Option B gives this value. The other options are either too low or too high, reflecting common mis‑calculations such as using only the summer value or adding an extra seasonal offset.

questions.question_text: A region currently has a population of 5.0 million. Its ecological carrying capacity is estimated at 12 million and the intrinsic growth rate is 0.04 yr⁻¹. Using the logistic growth model \(P(t)=\frac{K}{1+\left(\frac{K-P_{0}}{P_{0}}\right)e^{-rt}}\), what is the projected population after 10 years (rounded to one decimal place)?
questions.explanation: First compute the ratio \((K-P_0)/P_0 = (12-5)/5 = 1.4\). Then \(e^{-rt}=e^{-0.04\times10}=e^{-0.4}\approx0.6703\). The denominator becomes \(1+1.4\times0.6703\approx1.9384\). Thus \(P(10)=12/1.9384\approx6.19\) million, which rounds to 6.2 million. Option B matches this result. Options A, C and D result from using the exponential model, ignoring the carrying capacity, or mis‑applying the formula.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: For a land‑cover mapping project that must reliably detect isolated features as small as 10 m, which of the following sensor spatial resolutions is the most appropriate according to the Nyquist sampling criterion?
questions.explanation: The Nyquist criterion states that to resolve a feature, the sensor’s pixel size must be at most half the size of the smallest object of interest. For a 10 m feature, the required pixel size is ≤ 5 m. Therefore a sensor with 5 m spatial resolution can detect the feature, while 10 m resolution would just meet the limit and could miss the object under real‑world conditions. Options C and D are far too coarse.

questions.question_text: A topographic map is drawn at a scale of 1 : 50 000. On the map the straight‑line distance between Village A and Village B measures 7.5 cm. The contour interval of the map is 10 m, and the two villages lie three contour lines apart. What is the average gradient between the villages expressed as a percentage?
questions.explanation: Map scale 1:50,000 means 1 cm = 50,000 cm = 0.5 km = 500 m. Real horizontal distance = 7.5 cm × 500 m/cm = 3,750 m. Three contour intervals correspond to a vertical rise of 3 × 10 m = 30 m. Gradient G = (Δh / Δd) × 100% = (30 m / 3,750 m) × 100% = 0.008 × 100% = 0.8%. Option A matches this value. Options B, C, and D are higher or lower and can be rejected.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements best explains why magma with a high silica (SiO₂) content tends to produce more explosive volcanic eruptions?

options.text: High silica magma has a lower temperature, which increases its ability to dissolve volatiles.
High silica magma is more viscous, trapping gases and allowing pressure to build up.
High silica magma contains less dissolved water, making eruptions less likely to be explosive.
High silica magma solidifies at higher altitudes, reducing explosivity.

questions.explanation: Silica forms polymerized chains that increase magma viscosity. A viscous magma resists gas escape, so volatiles become trapped, pressure rises, and when it finally releases the eruption is explosive. Option B directly states this mechanism. Option A is incorrect because lower temperature does not increase volatile solubility; it actually decreases it. Option C is opposite to reality; high‑silica magmas often contain more dissolved water. Option D confuses solidification altitude with explosivity, which is not a primary factor.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 1.2 million in the year 2000 and grew to 2.1 million by 2020. Assuming the population follows the logistic growth model \[ P(t)=\frac{K}{1+\left(\frac{K-P_0}{P_0}\right)e^{-rt}} \] with a carrying capacity \(K=5\) million, what is the intrinsic growth rate \(r\) (per year) rounded to two decimal places?
questions.explanation: Given: P₀ = 1.2 million, P(20) = 2.1 million, K = 5 million, t = 20 yr. Compute \(\frac{K-P_0}{P_0}=\frac{5-1.2}{1.2}=3.1667\). Set up the equation: \(2.1 = \frac{5}{1+3.1667e^{-20r}}\). Rearranged: \(1+3.1667e^{-20r}=\frac{5}{2.1}=2.3810\). Hence \(3.1667e^{-20r}=1.3810\) and \(e^{-20r}=0.4363\). Take natural logs: \(-20r=\ln(0.4363)=-0.8286\). Therefore \(r=0.8286/20=0.04143\) yr⁻¹ ≈ 4.1 %. Option B is correct. The other choices result from arithmetic errors such as neglecting the denominator or mis‑calculating the logarithm.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor is at an altitude \(H = 700\) km and has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of \(\theta = 0.02^{\circ}\). Using the relationship \[ G = H \times \tan(\theta) \] where \(G\) is the ground resolution, what is the approximate ground resolution in meters?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='60' y1='180' x2='260' y2='180' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='260' y1='180' x2='260' y2='40' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='180' x2='260' y2='40' stroke='red' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,5'/><text x='150' y='190' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>H</text><text x='270' y='110' font-size='12'>G</text><text x='160' y='120' font-size='12' fill='red'>θ</text></svg>
questions.explanation: Convert the angle to radians: \(0.02^{\circ}=0.02\times\frac{\pi}{180}=0.000349\) rad. For small angles, \(\tan(\theta)\approx\theta\). Thus \(G = 700\,\text{km}\times0.000349 = 0.2443\,\text{km}\). Converting to meters gives \(0.2443\times1000 \approx 244\,\text{m}\). Option B matches. Options A, C, and D correspond to incorrect conversion or neglect of the radian step.

questions.question_text: According to the Burgess concentric zone model, which zone is most likely to be inhabited primarily by low‑income households?
questions.explanation: In Burgess' model the innermost ring is the CBD (commercial). The next ring, the Zone of Transition, is where older housing, ethnic enclaves, and low‑income households are concentrated due to deteriorating conditions and proximity to the CBD. The Zone of Working‑Class Homes lies farther out and accommodates moderately higher‑income residents. The Zone of Commuters is the outermost ring, typically occupied by higher‑income families who commute into the city. Hence, low‑income households are most associated with the Zone of Transition (Option B).

questions.question_text: A topographic map is drawn at a scale of 1:50,000. The distance between Town A and Town B measures 7.5 cm on the map. What is the true ground distance between the two towns? Also, the bearing shown on the map from A to B is 135°. What is the true bearing?
questions.explanation: The scale 1:50,000 means 1 cm on the map represents 50,000 cm = 0.5 km on the ground. Ground distance = 7.5 cm × 0.5 km/cm = 3.75 km. Bearing is a direction and is not affected by scale, so the true bearing remains 135°. Option B is correct. Options A, C, and D use incorrect scale conversions.

questions.question_text: Which tectonic setting is most commonly associated with explosive, silica‑rich (rhyolitic) volcanic eruptions?

options.text: Subduction zones where oceanic crust melts and triggers crustal melting
Intraplate hotspots beneath thin oceanic lithosphere
Continental rift valleys with basaltic magmatism

questions.explanation: Explosive rhyolitic eruptions require high silica magma, which is viscous and traps gases. This magma is typically generated in subduction zones where water‑rich slab melts lead to melting of the overlying continental crust, producing silica‑rich magmas. Mid‑ocean ridges (A) produce basaltic magma, hotspots (C) usually basaltic, and rift valleys (D) also basaltic. Therefore B is the correct choice.

questions.question_text: At the beginning of 2022 a city had a population of 12.5 million. During the year there were 180,000 births, 110,000 deaths, 50,000 immigrants and 30,000 emigrants. What was the population at the end of the year?
questions.explanation: Natural increase = births – deaths = 180,000 – 110,000 = 70,000. Net migration = immigrants – emigrants = 50,000 – 30,000 = 20,000. Total increase = 70,000 + 20,000 = 90,000. Final population = 12,500,000 + 90,000 = 12,590,000 ≈ 12.59 million. Option B matches this result. Other options reflect arithmetic errors.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, the city is shown as a linear arrangement of three zones: the central business district (CBD), Zone A, and Zone B. The CBD occupies 10 % of the total city area, Zone A 30 %, and Zone B 60 %. Which statement best reflects the expected population density pattern?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='80' width='80' height='40' fill='lightgray' stroke='black'/><rect x='120' y='80' width='80' height='40' fill='lightgray' stroke='black'/><rect x='220' y='80' width='80' height='40' fill='lightgray' stroke='black'/><text x='60' y='105' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>CBD</text><text x='160' y='105' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Zone A</text><text x='260' y='105' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Zone B</text></svg>

options.text: The CBD has the highest population density.
Zone A has the highest population density.
Zone B has the highest population density.
All three zones have roughly equal density.

questions.explanation: In most urban models, the central business district contains commercial buildings, high‑rise apartments, and intensive land use, leading to the highest people per unit area despite its small spatial extent. Zone A, being residential, has lower density, and Zone B, often suburban or peri‑urban, has the lowest. Hence option A is correct. Options B, C, and D contradict typical urban density gradients.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A satellite orbits at an altitude of 900 km and carries a sensor with an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.02°. Approximate the ground‑sample distance (spatial resolution) of the sensor in metres. (Assume small‑angle approximation: \(R \approx H\times\theta\), where \(\theta\) is in radians.)
questions.explanation: Convert IFOV to radians: \(0.02° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.000349\) rad. Ground resolution \(R = H \times \theta = 900\,\text{km} \times 0.000349 = 0.314\,\text{km} = 314\,\text{m}\). Option C matches this value. Options A, B, and D result from using incorrect conversions or rounding errors.

questions.question_text: A map has a scale of 1 cm : 5 km. Points A and B are plotted so that point A is at (2.3 cm East, 1.5 cm North) from the origin and point B is at (7.8 cm East, 5.1 cm North). What is the bearing from A to B measured clockwise from true north, rounded to the nearest degree?
questions.explanation: First find the east‑west and north‑south differences (in cm):\\nΔE = 7.8 - 2.3 = 5.5 cm,\\nΔN = 5.1 - 1.5 = 3.6 cm.\\nThe bearing θ from north is given by the arctangent of the ratio of these differences:\\nθ = \\tan^{-1}(ΔE/ΔN) = \\tan^{-1}(5.5/3.6) ≈ 56.7°.\\nRounded to the nearest degree, the bearing is 57°, which corresponds to option B. \\nOption A (45°) would require equal ΔE and ΔN, which is not the case.\\nOption C (67°) and D (78°) are larger than the computed value, so they are eliminated.

questions.question_text: The average geothermal gradient in the continental crust is about 25°C per kilometre. Assuming a surface temperature of 15°C, at approximately what depth will the temperature reach the 600°C required for partial melting of granitic rocks?
questions.explanation: The temperature at depth z is given by T = T₀ + G·z, where T₀ = 15°C and G = 25°C/km. Setting T = 600°C gives:\\n600 = 15 + 25·z\\n⇒ 25·z = 585\\n⇒ z = 585 / 25 = 23.4 km.\\nRounded to the nearest kilometre, the depth is about 23 km, which is option B.\\nOptions A and D are far from the calculated value, and option C (24 km) is slightly higher than the precise result.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, the line graph shows the average monthly temperature (°C) for a tropical coastal city. What is the mean annual temperature for the city?
questions.explanation: Reading the graph gives the following monthly averages (°C): Jan 22, Feb 24, Mar 27, Apr 30, May 32, Jun 34, Jul 33, Aug 32, Sep 30, Oct 28, Nov 25, Dec 23.\\nSum = 22+24+27+30+32+34+33+32+30+28+25+23 = 340°C.\\nMean annual temperature = 340 / 12 = 28.33°C ≈ 28°C.\\nThus option B is correct. The other options are either below or above the calculated mean.

questions.question_text: City X had a population of 1.20 million in 2015. Between 2015 and 2020 the natural increase (births minus deaths) was +180 000 and net migration was –30 000. Assuming exponential growth, what was the average annual population growth rate (in % per year) over the 5‑year period?
questions.explanation: Final population after 5 years: P_f = 1.20 M + 180 000 – 30 000 = 1.35 M.\\nGrowth factor over 5 years: P_f / P_0 = 1.35 / 1.20 = 1.125.\\nFor exponential growth, the annual rate r satisfies (1 + r)^5 = 1.125.\\nHence r = 1.125^{1/5} – 1.\\nUsing logarithms: ln(1.125)=0.1173. Divide by 5 → 0.02346. Exponentiate: e^{0.02346}=1.0238.\\nThus r ≈ 0.0238 = 2.38% ≈ 2.4%. Option B is correct. Options A, C, and D are inconsistent with the computed rate.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A satellite sensor is positioned at an orbital altitude of 600 km above the Earth's surface. Its instantaneous field of view (IFOV) is 0.0001 radians. What is the ground sampling distance (GSD) – the linear size of one pixel on the ground – in metres?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='160' y1='180' x2='160' y2='20' stroke='gray' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='155,180 165,180 160,20' fill='rgba(0,0,255,0.3)'/><text x='170' y='100' font-size='12' fill='black'>IFOV</text></svg>
questions.explanation: Ground sampling distance is obtained by multiplying the sensor altitude (H) by the instantaneous field of view:\\nGSD = H \times IFOV.\\nHere H = 600 km = 600,000 m and IFOV = 0.0001 rad.\\nGSD = 600,000 m × 0.0001 = 60 m.\\nTherefore the correct answer is option B. The other options correspond to different IFOV values and are not consistent with the given data.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, two points A and B are plotted on a topographic map. Point A is located at (Easting 12.5 cm, Northing 8.0 cm) and point B at (Easting 3.0 cm, Northing 15.0 cm). The map scale is 1 cm : 2 km. What is the bearing of the line from A to B measured clockwise from true north?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='260' cy='80' r='5' fill='red'/><text x='260' y='70' font-size='12' fill='black'>A</text><circle cx='80' cy='150' r='5' fill='blue'/><text x='80' y='140' font-size='12' fill='black'>B</text><line x1='260' y1='80' x2='80' y2='150' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
questions.explanation: ΔE = 3.0 cm - 12.5 cm = -9.5 cm, ΔN = 15.0 cm - 8.0 cm = 7.0 cm. Bearing = atan2(ΔE, ΔN). \(\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-9.5}{7.0}\right) \approx -53^{\circ}\). Adding 360° gives a clockwise bearing of \(360°-53° = 307°\). Options A, B, and C correspond to other quadrants and are eliminated.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements best explains why basaltic magma is the most common magma type produced at divergent plate boundaries?

options.text: It is generated by the partial melting of continental crust, which is silica‑rich.
It originates from low‑degree partial melting of the upwelling asthenospheric mantle, producing low‑silica, low‑viscosity magma.
It forms from the crystallisation of felsic magma that has ascended from deep mantle plumes.
It is created by the dehydration of subducting oceanic slabs, which releases water‑rich fluids.

questions.explanation: At divergent boundaries the mantle rises as the plates pull apart. Decompression melting of the peridotite mantle produces basaltic magma that is low in silica (≈45 %) and therefore has low viscosity, allowing it to erupt readily. Option A describes continental crust melting, which yields felsic magma. Option C refers to felsic magma from plumes, not typical of mid‑ocean ridges. Option D describes arc volcanism at convergent boundaries.

questions.question_text: A city is located at latitude \(30^{\circ}\) N. Assuming the Earth's axial tilt is \(23.5^{\circ}\), what is the maximum solar altitude angle (the height of the Sun above the horizon) at solar noon on the summer solstice?
questions.explanation: Solar altitude at noon = \(90^{\circ} - |\text{latitude} - \text{declination}|\). On the summer solstice the declination \(\delta = +23.5^{\circ}\). Thus \(h = 90^{\circ} - |30^{\circ} - 23.5^{\circ}| = 90^{\circ} - 6.5^{\circ} = 83.5^{\circ}\). The other options result from using incorrect signs or forgetting the absolute value.

questions.question_text: A region currently has a population of 8 million. The ecological carrying capacity is estimated at 20 million and the intrinsic growth rate is \(r = 0.04\) yr⁻¹. Using the logistic growth model \(P(t)=\frac{K}{1+\left(\frac{K-P_{0}}{P_{0}}\right)e^{-rt}}\), what is the projected population after 15 years (rounded to one decimal place)?
questions.explanation:
Given \(P_{0}=8\), \(K=20\), \(r=0.04\), \(t=15\):
\( rac{K-P_{0}}{P_{0}} =  rac{20-8}{8}=1.5\).
\(e^{-rt}=e^{-0.04	imes15}=e^{-0.6}\approx0.5488\).
Denominator = \(1+1.5	imes0.5488=1+0.8232=1.8232\).
\(P(15)= rac{20}{1.8232}\approx10.97\) million ≈ 11.0 million. Options A, C, and D are either too low or too high, reflecting mis‑application of the formula or using exponential growth instead of logistic.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A remote‑sensing satellite orbits at an altitude of 700 km and its sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.05 mrad. What is the ground‑sampling distance (GSD) on the Earth's surface for this sensor? (Assume a nadir view).
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='140' y='20' width='40' height='20' fill='gray'/><text x='150' y='35' font-size='10' fill='white'>Sat</text><line x1='160' y1='40' x2='160' y2='150' stroke='black' stroke-dasharray='5,5'/><rect x='150' y='150' width='20' height='20' fill='green'/><text x='152' y='165' font-size='10' fill='white'>GSD</text></svg>
questions.explanation: Convert IFOV to radians: 0.05 mrad = 0.05 × 10⁻³ rad = 5×10⁻⁵ rad. Altitude \(H = 700\) km = 700,000 m.\nGSD = H × IFOV = 700,000 m × 5×10⁻⁵ rad = 35 m. Therefore the correct answer is 35 m. Option A would result from using a IFOV ten times smaller; C and D correspond to mis‑placement of decimal points.

questions.question_text: On a topographic map the scale is 1 cm : 3 km. The straight‑line distance measured between Town A and Town B is 5.8 cm and the bearing from A to B is 70° measured clockwise from true north. What is the true ground distance and bearing from Town A to Town B?
questions.explanation:
The map scale tells us that 1 cm on the map equals 3 km on the ground. Multiply the measured map distance by this factor:
\[ d_{	ext{ground}} = 5.8\,	ext{cm}	imes 3\, rac{	ext{km}}{	ext{cm}} = 17.4\,	ext{km}. \]
The bearing is given directly as 70° measured clockwise from true north, so the true bearing is also 70°.

Option A matches both the calculated distance and the given bearing. Options B and D use an incorrect distance (14.5 km, which would result from a scale of 2.5 km / cm). Options C and D reverse the bearing to 250°, which is the opposite direction and therefore wrong.

questions.question_text: Which volcanic landform is most commonly associated with the eruption of low‑viscosity basaltic magma that can travel great distances as fluid lava flows?
questions.explanation: Basaltic magma is hot and has low silica content, giving it low viscosity. Such magma spreads out in thin sheets and can travel many kilometres before solidifying. The volcanic structure that results from the accumulation of these extensive, fluid lava flows is a shield volcano, characterised by broad, gently sloping sides.\n\nOption B correctly identifies a shield volcano. A composite (stratovolcano) forms from alternating layers of viscous lava and ash, requiring higher‑silica magma. Cinder cones are built from pyroclastic fragments, not from long‑range fluid flows. Lava domes are produced by very viscous, silica‑rich magma that piles up near the vent.

questions.question_text: At latitude \(45^{\circ}\text{N}\) on the summer solstice (solar declination \(\delta = +23.5^{\circ}\)), the approximate daylight duration can be estimated by the formula \[ D = \frac{24}{\pi}\arccos\!\left(-\tan\phi\,\tan\delta\right) \] where \(\phi\) is latitude. Using this formula, which of the following is closest to the length of daylight (in hours) on that day?
questions.explanation:
Insert \(\phi = 45^{\circ}\) and \(\delta = 23.5^{\circ}\) into the expression:
\[ -	an\phi\,	an\delta = -	an45^{\circ}\,	an23.5^{\circ} = -(1)(0.433) = -0.433. \]

Compute the arccosine: \(\arccos(-0.433) \approx 115.7^{\circ}\). Convert to radians: \(115.7^{\circ}	imes rac{\pi}{180}=2.020\,	ext{rad}.\)

Now calculate daylight hours:
\[ D =  rac{24}{\pi}	imes 2.020 \approx 7.639	imes2.020 \approx 15.44\,	ext{h}. \]
Rounded to the nearest tenth, this is about 15.5 h, which corresponds to option B.

Option A (14 h) underestimates the effect of the high declination; options C and D are too large for a latitude of 45° N.

questions.question_text: A country recorded a natural increase of 120 000 (births minus deaths) and a net migration of –30 000 during 2022. Its mid‑year population was 15 million. What was the annual population growth rate (percent) for 2022? Use the formula \(\text{Growth rate (\%)} = \frac{\text{Natural increase}+\text{Net migration}}{\text{Mid‑year population}}\times100\).
questions.explanation:
First combine natural increase and net migration: \(120\,000 + (-30\,000) = 90\,000\).

Apply the growth‑rate formula:
\[ 	ext{Growth rate} =  rac{90\,000}{15\,000\,000}	imes100 = 0.006	imes100 = 0.60\%. \]
Thus the correct answer is 0.60 % (option A).

Option B assumes the net migration was zero, giving \(120\,000/15\,000\,000 = 0.80\%\). Options C and D are progressively larger and do not reflect the given figures.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor flies at an altitude \(H = 800\) km above the Earth's surface and has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) that subtends an angle \(\alpha = 0.025^{\circ}\). The ground‑sample distance (GSD) can be approximated by \[ \text{GSD}=H\tan\alpha. \] What is the GSD in kilometres?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='160' y1='20' x2='160' y2='180' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='160,20 155,30 165,30' fill='black'/><text x='170' y='50' font-size='12'>H = 800 km</text><line x1='160' y1='180' x2='200' y2='180' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M160,180 L165,175 L170,180 L165,185 Z' fill='gray'/><text x='170' y='195' font-size='12'>α = 0.025°</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
Convert the IFOV angle to radians: \[ \alpha = 0.025^{\circ}	imes rac{\pi}{180}=0.00043633	ext{ rad}. \]
For such a small angle, \(	an\alpha \approx \alpha\).
Now compute GSD: \[ 	ext{GSD}=800\,	ext{km}	imes	an(0.00043633)\approx800	imes0.00043634=0.349\,	ext{km}. \]
Rounded to two decimal places, this is about 0.35 km, which matches option B.

Option A is about half the correct value, option C is 60 % larger, and option D is more than double; they result from using incorrect conversion factors or neglecting the tangent function.

questions.question_text: On a map with a scale of 1 cm : 2 km, point A is located at (2.5 cm East, 1.5 cm North) and point B at (7.0 cm East, 5.0 cm North). What is the bearing from point A to point B measured clockwise from true north? (Give the nearest whole degree.)
questions.explanation:
First find the differences in the Easting and Northing coordinates: \(\Delta E = 7.0	ext{ cm} - 2.5	ext{ cm} = 4.5	ext{ cm}\) and \(\Delta N = 5.0	ext{ cm} - 1.5	ext{ cm} = 3.5	ext{ cm}\). The bearing \(	heta\) from A to B is given by \(	heta = 	an^{-1}(\Delta E / \Delta N)\). Substituting the values, \(	heta = 	an^{-1}(4.5/3.5) \approx 	an^{-1}(1.2857) \approx 52.5\degree\). Rounded to the nearest degree the bearing is 53\degree, which corresponds to option C.

Distractor analysis:
- 30\degree and 45\degree are too small because the eastward component is larger than the northward component.
- 67\degree would require \(\Delta E / \Delta N\) > 2.4, which is not the case here.

questions.question_text: Which volcanic landform is most characteristically associated with intraplate hotspot volcanism?
questions.explanation: Hotspot volcanism occurs away from plate boundaries where a mantle plume rises beneath the lithosphere. The magma is typically low‑viscosity, basaltic, and erupts effusively, building broad, gently sloping edifices known as shield volcanoes (e.g., the Hawaiian Islands). Composite volcanoes form at convergent margins with more silica‑rich magma, cinder cones are small explosive cones, and lava domes involve viscous rhyolitic lava. Therefore the correct answer is A, shield volcano.

questions.question_text: In 2022 a country had a mid‑year population of 20 million. During that year there were 350,000 births, 250,000 deaths, and a net migration of –30,000. What was the annual population growth rate expressed as the number of persons per 1,000 population?
questions.explanation:
Natural increase = births – deaths = 350,000 – 250,000 = 100,000. Adding net migration gives total change = 100,000 + (‑30,000) = 70,000. The growth rate per 1,000 is \(r =  rac{70,000}{20,000,000}	imes 1000 = 3.5\). Hence the correct answer is B.

Distractor analysis:
- 2.5 per 1,000 would correspond to a total change of 50,000, which understates the actual increase.
- 4.5 and 5.5 per 1,000 imply changes of 90,000 and 110,000 respectively, which are higher than the computed 70,000.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text:
In the diagram below, a satellite sensor orbits at an altitude of \(H = 700\) km and has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of \(0.025^{\circ}\). What is the approximate ground‑resolved size (pixel size) on the Earth's surface?

In the diagram below, ...
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="160" cy="100" r="40" fill="none" stroke="black"/><line x1="160" y1="0" x2="160" y2="100" stroke="red"/></svg>
questions.explanation:
Convert IFOV to radians: \(	heta_{	ext{rad}} = 0.025^{\circ}	imes rac{\pi}{180}=0.000436\,	ext{rad}\). Ground resolution \(G = H	imes	heta_{	ext{rad}} = 700\,	ext{km}	imes0.000436 = 0.305\,	ext{km}\approx 300\,	ext{m}\). Option C matches this value.

Distractor analysis:
- 150 m and 250 m are too small because they would require a much narrower IFOV.
- 400 m is larger than the calculated value, implying a broader IFOV than given.

questions.question_text:
In the diagram below, the Earth is shown with a tilt of \(23.5^{\circ}\) relative to its orbital plane. At a latitude of \(55^{\circ}\) N on the summer solstice (solar declination \(\delta = +23.5^{\circ}\)), what is the approximate length of daylight in hours?

In the diagram below, ...
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="160" cy="100" r="80" fill="none" stroke="black"/><line x1="160" y1="20" x2="160" y2="180" stroke="blue"/><line x1="160" y1="100" x2="240" y2="130" stroke="red"/></svg>
questions.explanation:
First compute the hour angle at sunrise/sunset: \(H_0 = \arccos igl(-	an55^{\circ}\,	an23.5^{\circ} igr)\). \(	an55^{\circ}=1.428\) and \(	an23.5^{\circ}=0.434\); their product is 0.619, so \(-	an\phi\,	an\delta = -0.619\). \(\arccos(-0.619)\approx 128.3^{\circ}\). Day length = \(2	imes128.3^{\circ}/15^{\circ}\) per hour = \(256.6/15 \approx 17.1\) hours. Hence option C is correct.

Distractor analysis:
- 14.5 h and 15.8 h correspond to lower latitudes or winter solstice conditions.
- 18.3 h would be expected at even higher latitudes (e.g., 65°N) on the solstice.

questions.question_text: A topographic map is drawn at a scale of 1 : 75 000. On the map the straight‑line distance between Town A and Town B measures 9.6 cm and the bearing from A to B is given as N 30° E. What is the true ground distance between the towns in kilometres and how should the bearing be expressed as a compass direction?
questions.explanation:
The scale 1 : 75 000 means 1 cm on the map equals 75 000 cm on the ground. \(75 000\,	ext{cm}=0.75\,	ext{km}\).
Ground distance = 9.6 cm × 0.75 km / cm = 7.2 km.
The bearing N 30° E is already a compass bearing meaning 30° east of true north, so it is written as N 30° E.
Option A gives both the correct distance and the correct bearing; the other options either have the wrong distance or an incorrect expression of the bearing.

questions.question_text: Partial melting of mantle peridotite at depths greater than 100 km beneath a continental lithosphere most commonly produces which type of volcanic rock?
questions.explanation: At depths > 100 km the pressure is high and the degree of partial melting of the mantle is relatively low, yielding a melt that is mafic in composition (high in Mg and Fe, low in SiO₂). This magma crystallises to basalt when it reaches the surface. Andesite and rhyolite are more silica‑rich and typically derive from higher‑degree melting or crustal assimilation, while diorite is an intrusive rock. Therefore the correct answer is basalt.

questions.question_text: A city had a population of 5 million in 2015 and has been growing at a constant annual rate of 2.3 % (compounded yearly). What is the projected population in 2020?
questions.explanation:
Using the compound growth formula \(P = P_0(1+r)^t\):
\(P_0 = 5,000,000\), \(r = 0.023\), \(t = 5\) years.
\( (1+0.023)^5 = 1.023^5 \approx 1.1204 \) (calculated step‑by‑step in the analysis).
Thus \(P = 5,000,000 	imes 1.1204 \approx 5,602,000\), i.e. about 5.60 million. Option A matches this result; the other options are off by more than the rounding error.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a remote‑sensing satellite sensor is at an orbital altitude \(H = 700\) km and has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of \(0.05^{\circ}\). What is the ground‑sample distance (GSD) in metres captured by a single detector element? (Use \(\text{GSD}=2H\tan\frac{\text{IFOV}}{2}\)).
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='160' y1='180' x2='160' y2='20' stroke='black'/><path d='M160 180 L170 170 L150 170 Z' fill='black'/><text x='165' y='100' font-size='12'>H=700 km</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
First convert IFOV to radians: \(0.05^{\circ}=0.05	imes rac{\pi}{180}=8.7266	imes10^{-4}\) rad. Half of this is \(4.3633	imes10^{-4}\) rad. For small angles, \(	an	heta\approx	heta\).
\(	ext{GSD}=2	imes700\,	ext{km}	imes4.3633	imes10^{-4}=1 400\,	ext{km}	imes4.3633	imes10^{-4}\).
Convert km to m: 1 400 km = 1 400 000 m.
\(	ext{GSD}=1 400 000	imes4.3633	imes10^{-4}\approx610.9\) m, rounded to about 610 m. Option A is the closest value. The other options correspond to errors such as forgetting the factor 2 or using degrees instead of radians.

questions.question_text: According to the rank‑size rule, if the largest city in a country has a population of 8 million, what is the expected population of the fifth‑largest city?
questions.explanation: The rank‑size rule states \(P_r = \frac{P_1}{r}\), where \(P_r\) is the population of the city of rank \(r\) and \(P_1\) is the population of the largest city.\nFor the 5th‑largest city: \(P_5 = \frac{8\,\text{million}}{5} = 1.6\,\text{million}\).\nOption A matches this calculation. The other options would correspond to different rank‑size relationships or incorrect arithmetic.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, two towns, A and B, are plotted on a topographic map. The map scale is 1 cm : 2 km. Town A is located at coordinates (Easting 4 cm, Northing 3 cm) and Town B at (Easting 9 cm, Northing 7 cm). What is the ground distance between the towns in kilometres, and what is the bearing from A to B measured clockwise from north? Assume the map grid is oriented true north.
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox=\"0 0 320 200\" xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\"><circle cx=\"80\" cy=\"140\" r=\"5\" fill=\"red\"/><text x=\"80\" y=\"135\" font-size=\"12\" fill=\"black\">A</text><circle cx=\"240\" cy=\"80\" r=\"5\" fill=\"blue\"/><text x=\"240\" y=\"75\" font-size=\"12\" fill=\"black\">B</text><line x1=\"80\" y1=\"140\" x2=\"240\" y2=\"80\" stroke=\"black\" stroke-width=\"2\"/></svg>
questions.explanation: Difference in eastings = 9 cm - 4 cm = 5 cm; difference in northings = 7 cm - 3 cm = 4 cm. Map distance = \(\sqrt{5^2+4^2}=\sqrt{41}\) cm \(\approx 6.40\) cm. Ground distance = 6.40 cm × 2 km/cm = 12.8 km. Bearing from north = \(\arctan\frac{\text{Easting diff}}{\text{Northing diff}} = \arctan\frac{5}{4} \approx 51.3^{\circ}\). Option A matches both values; the other options either mis‑state the distance or bearing.

questions.question_text: A volcanic plateau is formed when successive lava flows spread over a region. If the plateau covers an area of 15 000 km² and the average thickness of the accumulated lava is 120 m, estimate the total erupted lava volume in cubic kilometres. Choose the closest value.
questions.explanation: Convert thickness to kilometres: 120 m = 0.12 km. Volume = area × thickness = 15 000 km² × 0.12 km = 1 800 km³ = 1.8 × 10³ km³. Option A is the closest estimate; the other options differ by at least 20 %.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, the Earth is shown with its axial tilt of 23.5° and a latitude line at 55° N. Assuming the solar declination on the summer solstice is +23.5°, calculate the length of daylight (in hours) at latitude 55° N on that day.
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox=\"0 0 320 200\" xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\"><circle cx=\"160\" cy=\"100\" r=\"80\" fill=\"lightblue\" stroke=\"black\"/><line x1=\"160\" y1=\"100\" x2=\"160\" y2=\"20\" stroke=\"black\" stroke-width=\"2\"/><text x=\"165\" y=\"30\" font-size=\"12\" fill=\"black\">North Pole</text><line x1=\"160\" y1=\"100\" x2=\"240\" y2=\"100\" stroke=\"gray\" stroke-width=\"2\" stroke-dasharray=\"4 2\"/><text x=\"250\" y=\"105\" font-size=\"12\" fill=\"black\">55° N</text><line x1=\"120\" y1=\"100\" x2=\"200\" y2=\"100\" stroke=\"orange\" stroke-width=\"4\"/><text x=\"210\" y=\"95\" font-size=\"12\" fill=\"black\">Sun rays</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
For latitude \(\phi = 55^{\circ}\) and declination \(\delta = 23.5^{\circ}\):
\[ H_0 = \arccos igl(-	an55^{\circ}\,	an23.5^{\circ} igr) \]
\(	an55^{\circ}=1.428\), \(	an23.5^{\circ}=0.433\); product = 0.619, so \(-	an\phi	an\delta = -0.619\).
\(H_0 = \arccos(-0.619) \approx 128.3^{\circ}\).
Day length = \( rac{2}{15} H_0\) hours = \( rac{2}{15}	imes128.3 \approx 17.1\) h. Option C matches; the other values are too low or high.

questions.question_text: A country currently has a population of 8 million. Its ecological carrying capacity is 20 million and the intrinsic growth rate is 0.04 yr⁻¹. Using the logistic growth model \(P(t)=\frac{K}{1+A e^{-rt}}\) where \(A = \frac{K-P_0}{P_0}\), after how many whole years will the population reach 15 million?
questions.explanation:
Given \(K=20\) million, \(P_0=8\) million, \(r=0.04\).
\(A =  rac{20-8}{8}=1.5\). Set \(P(t)=15\):
\[15 =  rac{20}{1+1.5 e^{-0.04 t}}\]
Invert: \(1+1.5 e^{-0.04 t}= rac{20}{15}= rac{4}{3}\).
Thus \(1.5 e^{-0.04 t}= rac{4}{3}-1= rac{1}{3}\).
\(e^{-0.04 t}= rac{1}{3}\div1.5 =  rac{2}{9}=0.2222\).
Taking natural logs: \(-0.04 t = \ln 0.2222 = -1.5041\).
\(t =  rac{1.5041}{0.04} \approx 37.6\) years, which rounds to 38 years. Option B is correct; the other options are far from the computed value.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A UAV flies at an altitude of 120 m carrying a digital camera with a sensor width of 13.2 mm, focal length 8 mm, and an image width of 4000 pixels. What is the ground sampling distance (GSD) in centimetres per pixel?
questions.explanation: Convert dimensions to metres: \(S_w = 13.2\,\text{mm}=0.0132\,\text{m}\), \(f = 8\,\text{mm}=0.008\,\text{m}\).\n\[ \text{GSD}=\frac{120\,\text{m}\times0.0132\,\text{m}}{0.008\,\text{m}\times4000}=\frac{1.584}{32}=0.0495\,\text{m}=4.95\,\text{cm} \]\nRounded to one decimal place, the GSD is about 5.0 cm per pixel. Option B matches this value; the other options are too low or too high.

questions.question_text: Points P and Q are plotted on a topographic map. The map scale is 1 cm = 2 km. Point P is located 3.2 cm east and 4.5 cm north of the map origin, while point Q is 7.8 cm east and 1.2 cm north of the origin. What is the true bearing from P to Q (rounded to the nearest degree)?
questions.explanation:
First convert the map distances to real distances using the scale (1 cm = 2 km).
\(\Delta E = (7.8-3.2)\,	ext{cm}=4.6\,	ext{cm}=4.6	imes2=9.2\,	ext{km}\) east.
\(\Delta N = (1.2-4.5)\,	ext{cm}= -3.3\,	ext{cm}= -3.3	imes2 = -6.6\,	ext{km}\) (south).
The bearing \(	heta\) is given by the atan2 function: \[ 	heta = \operatorname{atan2}(\Delta E,\Delta N) \]
Plugging the values, \[ 	heta = \operatorname{atan2}(9.2,-6.6) \approx 125.4^{\circ}. \] Rounded to the nearest degree the bearing is 125°.

Eliminating the distractors: 54° would be the angle if the line were in the NE quadrant; 305° points to the NW quadrant; 70° is a smaller acute angle not matching the computed value. Hence option A is correct.

questions.question_text: Which type of volcanic eruption is most characteristically associated with high‑silica (rhyolitic) magma because of its high viscosity and gas‑rich nature?
questions.explanation: Rhyolitic magma contains > 70 % silica, making it highly viscous. The trapped volatiles are released explosively, producing towering eruption columns and widespread ash fall – the hallmark of a Plinian eruption (e.g., the 79 AD eruption of Mount Vesuvius). Hawaiian eruptions involve low‑viscosity basalt; Strombolian eruptions are moderate‑explosive basaltic to andesitic; Surtseyan eruptions occur when magma interacts explosively with water. Therefore the correct answer is B, Plinian.

questions.question_text: A region has an average temperature of 24 °C in its hottest month and 12 °C in its coldest month. Annual precipitation is 800 mm, with a distinct dry summer in which the driest summer month receives less than 30 mm. According to the Köppen climate classification, which climate type best fits this region?
questions.explanation:
Köppen criteria:
- Temperate (C) climates have the coldest month > 0 °C and < 18 °C (12 °C satisfies).
- Summer‑dry (s) requires the driest summer month < 30 mm and the driest summer month < (1/3) of the wettest winter month – the data meet this condition.
- Hot‑summer (a) requires the warmest month ≥ 22 °C, which is true (24 °C).
Thus the region falls under C s a – a Mediterranean climate with hot summers. Options A, C, and D do not satisfy the temperature and precipitation pattern. Hence option B is correct.

questions.question_text: In 2020 a country had a mid‑year population of 15 million. During that year there were 200 000 births, 150 000 deaths and a net migration of –40 000 (more people left than arrived). What was the annual population growth rate expressed as a percentage (rounded to two decimal places)?
questions.explanation:
Natural increase = births – deaths = 200 000 – 150 000 = 50 000.
Total population change = natural increase + net migration = 50 000 + (‑40 000) = 10 000.
Initial population \(P_0 = 15 000 000\).
Growth rate \(r = (10 000 / 15 000 000) 	imes 100\% \approx 0.0667\%\). Rounded to two decimal places, this is 0.07 %.
Option A matches this value; the other options are considerably higher and can be discarded.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor is at an altitude of 700 km above the Earth's surface and has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.0001 rad. What is the ground‑resolution (size of one pixel) produced by this sensor? (Assume a nadir viewing geometry.)
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="160" y1="20" x2="160" y2="180" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="140" y="180" width="40" height="20" fill="lightblue" stroke="black"/></svg>
questions.explanation: Ground resolution (pixel size) for a nadir‑looking sensor is obtained by multiplying the altitude by the IFOV (in radians):\n\[ \text{Resolution}= H\times \text{IFOV}= 700\,\text{km}\times 0.0001 = 0.07\,\text{km}=70\,\text{m}. \]\nThus the sensor records each pixel as a 70 m ground area. Option A gives this value. Options B and D are off by factors of 10, and option C misinterprets the unit as kilometres.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, points P and Q are plotted on a scaled map. The map scale is 1 cm : 5 km. The distance measured between P and Q on the map is 12 cm. Using the coordinates shown, determine (a) the true ground distance between the two locations, and (b) the bearing from P to Q measured clockwise from true north. (Round the bearing to the nearest degree.)
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='200' y2='50' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='50' cy='150' r='4' fill='red'/><text x='40' y='165' font-size='12'>P</text><circle cx='200' cy='50' r='4' fill='blue'/><text x='190' y='45' font-size='12'>Q</text></svg>
questions.explanation: Map distance 12 cm × 5 km / cm = 60 km, so the true ground distance is 60 km. From the SVG, ΔE = 200−50 = 150 units and ΔN = 150−50 = 100 units (northward). Bearing \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(150/100) \approx 56.3^{\circ}\). Rounded to the nearest degree gives 56°. Option A matches both values.

questions.question_text: Which type of plate boundary is most commonly associated with the generation of intermediate‑composition (andesitic) magma and the eruption of composite (stratovolcano) volcanoes?
questions.explanation: Subduction zones force oceanic crust to melt partially, producing water‑rich, intermediate‑composition magmas that commonly form andesitic rocks. The resulting viscous magma drives explosive eruptions typical of composite volcanoes. Divergent and transform settings produce basaltic magmas, while hotspots generate mainly basaltic or alkaline rocks.

questions.question_text: At latitude \(60^{\circ}\text{N}\) on the summer solstice (solar declination \(\delta = +23.5^{\circ}\)), what is the approximate length of daylight? Use the formula \[ D = \frac{2}{15}\,\arccos\bigl(-\tan\phi\,\tan\delta\bigr) \] where \(D\) is in hours and \(\phi\) is latitude.
questions.explanation: Compute \(\tan\phi = \tan60^{\circ}=1.732\) and \(\tan\delta = \tan23.5^{\circ}=0.434\). The product is \(1.732 \times 0.434 = 0.751\). Then \(\arccos(-0.751) \approx 138.6^{\circ}\). Day length \(D = \frac{2}{15} \times 138.6 \approx 18.48\) h, i.e., about 18.5 hours. Option A is correct.

questions.question_text: A region has an initial population of 12 million, an ecological carrying capacity of 30 million, and a intrinsic growth rate of \(r = 0.03\) yr\(^{-1}\). Using the logistic growth model \[ P(t)=\frac{K}{1+\left(\frac{K-P_0}{P_0}\right)e^{-rt}} \], what is the expected population after 5 years? (Round to one decimal place in million.)

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A remote‑sensing satellite orbits at an altitude of 800 km above the Earth's surface. Its sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.0001 rad. What is the approximate ground sampling distance (GSD) on the surface directly beneath the satellite?
questions.explanation: Convert altitude to metres: \(H = 800\,\text{km}=800,000\,\text{m}\). GSD = \(H \times \text{IFOV}=800,000\,\text{m}\times0.0001 = 80\,\text{m}\). Therefore the ground resolution is about 80 m. Option A is correct.

questions.question_text: A topographic map has a scale of 1 cm : 5 km. On the map the straight‑line distance between two towns measured with a ruler is 3.2 cm. What is the true ground distance between the towns?
questions.explanation: The map scale tells us that 1 cm on the map equals 5 km on the ground. Multiply the measured map distance by the scale factor: \(3.2\text{ cm} \times 5\text{ km/cm}=16\text{ km}\). Therefore the true distance is 16 km. Options A‑C are obtained by using incorrect scale factors (e.g., 1 cm = 3 km, 1 cm = 4 km, etc.) and are eliminated.

questions.question_text: Which type of volcanic rock is most characteristically produced at a divergent plate boundary where mantle upwelling generates low‑viscosity magma?
questions.explanation: Divergent boundaries are sites of mantle upwelling and partial melting that produce basaltic magma, which is low in silica and therefore low in viscosity. Basalt erupts as fluid lava flows typical of mid‑ocean ridges. Andesite, rhyolite, and dacite are higher‑silica rocks associated with convergent margins or continental volcanic arcs, so they are eliminated.

questions.question_text: A country had a population of 12 million in 2015 and has been growing at a constant annual rate of 2.5 % (compounded yearly). What is the expected population at the beginning of 2020?
questions.explanation:
Using the compound growth formula \(P = P_{0}(1+r)^{n}\), where \(P_{0}=12\text{ million}\), \(r=0.025\) and \(n=5\) years:
\[P = 12 \times (1.025)^{5} \approx 12 \times 1.1314 \approx 13.58\text{ million}\]\
Rounded to one decimal place, the population is 13.6 million. Options A, C, and D result from using incorrect rates or rounding errors and are therefore excluded.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, which concentric zone of the Burgess model typically contains middle‑class families?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='20' fill='none' stroke='black'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='50' fill='none' stroke='black'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='80' fill='none' stroke='black'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='110' fill='none' stroke='black'/><text x='160' y='95' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>CBD</text><text x='160' y='125' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Zone of Transition</text><text x='160' y='155' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Zone of Independent Workers</text><text x='160' y='185' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Zone of Better Residences</text></svg>
questions.explanation: In the Burgess concentric zone model, the innermost ring is the CBD (commercial). The next ring is the zone of transition (mixed residential‑commercial, often low‑income). The third ring houses independent workers (working‑class). The fourth ring, the "zone of better residences," is where middle‑class families typically live, offering better housing and amenities. The outermost commuter zone is for suburban commuters. Therefore option D is correct; all other zones represent lower‑income or commercial functions.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor flies at an altitude of 700 km and has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.05 milliradian. What is the ground sampling distance (GSD) for a single pixel on the Earth's surface?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='160' y1='200' x2='160' y2='0' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='150' y1='200' x2='150' y2='0' stroke='red' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='165' y='190' font-size='10'>Sensor</text><text x='155' y='10' font-size='10'>Ground</text><text x='120' y='120' font-size='10'>IFOV = 0.05 mrad</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
The ground sampling distance is obtained by multiplying the sensor altitude (H) by the instantaneous field of view (IFOV):\[GSD = H \times IFOV\]\
Convert units: altitude = 700 km = 700,000 m; IFOV = 0.05 milliradian = 0.05 × 10⁻³ rad = 0.00005 rad.\
Thus, \[GSD = 700,000 \text{ m} \times 0.00005 = 35 \text{ m}\].\
Option A matches this calculation. Options B–D arise from using incorrect unit conversions or rounding errors, so they are discarded.

questions.question_text: On a topographic map with a scale of 1 cm : 5 km, point P is plotted 3 cm east and 4 cm north of the origin, and point Q is plotted 8 cm east and 1 cm north of the origin. What is the bearing from P to Q, measured clockwise from north, to the nearest degree?
questions.explanation: ΔE = 8 cm - 3 cm = 5 cm, ΔN = 1 cm - 4 cm = -3 cm (southward). Bearing is found with \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(\Delta E/\Delta N)\). \(\tan^{-1}(5/(-3)) = -59°\). Because ΔN is negative, add 180°: \(180°-59° = 121°\). Therefore the bearing is 121° (option A). The other options result from omitting the 180° correction or swapping ΔE and ΔN.

questions.question_text: A volcanic rock sample contains 48 % SiO₂ by weight. According to the standard silica classification, which magma type does this composition represent?
questions.explanation: Mafic (basaltic) magmas contain 45–52 % SiO₂. The sample’s 48 % places it firmly in the basaltic range. Andesitic rocks have 52–63 % SiO₂, rhyolitic >63 %, and dacitic 63–68 %. Hence option A is correct; the other options describe higher silica contents.

questions.question_text: At a latitude of 30° N on the day of the summer solstice (solar declination \(\delta = +23.5^{\circ}\)), what is the solar altitude angle at local solar noon?
questions.explanation: Using \(\alpha = 90^{\circ} - |\phi - \delta|\): \(\phi = 30^{\circ}\), \(\delta = 23.5^{\circ}\). \(|30 - 23.5| = 6.5^{\circ}\). Thus \(\alpha = 90^{\circ} - 6.5^{\circ} = 83.5^{\circ}\). Option A matches. Options B–D arise from incorrect subtraction or ignoring the absolute value.

questions.question_text: A city had a population of 5 million in 2015. Its growth follows the logistic model \(P(t)=\frac{K}{1+\left(\frac{K-P_{0}}{P_{0}}\right)e^{-rt}}\) with carrying capacity \(K=20\) million, intrinsic rate \(r=0.05\) yr⁻¹, and \(P_{0}=5\) million at \(t=0\). In which year will the population first exceed 15 million?
questions.explanation:
Set \(P(t)=15\) million:
\[15 = \frac{20}{1+3e^{-0.05t}}\]
Invert: \(1+3e^{-0.05t}=\frac{20}{15}=\frac{4}{3}\).
Thus \(3e^{-0.05t}=\frac{4}{3}-1=\frac{1}{3}\) → \(e^{-0.05t}=\frac{1}{9}=0.1111\).
Take natural logs: \(-0.05t=\ln 0.1111\approx-2.1972\).
Solve: \(t=\frac{2.1972}{0.05}\approx43.94\) years.
Starting from 2015, add 44 years → 2059. Option C is correct. The other years correspond to mis‑calculated t values.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite orbits 700 km above the Earth's surface. The sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.05 milliradian. What is the ground‑sample distance (GSD) – i.e., the size of one pixel on the surface – produced by this sensor?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='160' y1='20' x2='100' y2='150' stroke='black'/><line x1='160' y1='20' x2='220' y2='150' stroke='black'/><rect x='100' y='150' width='120' height='10' fill='none' stroke='red'/><text x='160' y='10' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Satellite</text><text x='160' y='180' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Ground footprint</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
Convert IFOV to radians: 0.05 milliradian = 0.05×10⁻³ rad = 5×10⁻⁵ rad.
Ground‑sample distance (GSD) = altitude × IFOV:
\[GSD = 700\,\text{km} \times 5\times10^{-5} = 700\,000\,\text{m} \times 5\times10^{-5}=35\,\text{m}.\]
Thus each detector element records a 35 m square on the ground (option A). The other choices arise from using incorrect unit conversions or forgetting the milliradian factor.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, points A and B are plotted on a topographic map that uses a scale of 1 cm : 5 km. The straight‑line distance measured between A and B on the map is 14 cm. What is the actual ground distance between the two settlements?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="80" cy="150" r="5" fill="red"/><text x="80" y="145" font-size="12" fill="black">A</text><circle cx="240" cy="50" r="5" fill="blue"/><text x="240" y="45" font-size="12" fill="black">B</text><line x1="80" y1="150" x2="240" y2="50" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/></svg>
questions.explanation: The map scale states that 1 cm on the map equals 5 km on the ground. Multiply the measured map distance by the scale factor: 14 cm × 5 km / cm = 70 km. Options B, C and D correspond to using incorrect scale factors (4 km, 3 km, 2 km per cm) and are therefore eliminated.

questions.question_text: At latitude 35° N on the summer solstice (solar declination \(\delta = +23.5^{\circ}\)), what is the solar altitude angle at local solar noon?
questions.explanation: Solar altitude at noon is given by \(h = 90^{\circ} - |\phi - \delta|\). Substituting \(\phi = 35^{\circ}\) and \(\delta = 23.5^{\circ}\): \(|35 - 23.5| = 11.5^{\circ}\). Thus \(h = 90^{\circ} - 11.5^{\circ} = 78.5^{\circ}\). The other options result from using wrong signs or omitting the absolute‑value operation.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 120,000 in the year 2010. If it experiences an average annual exponential growth rate of 2.8 % per year, what will be its projected population in the year 2025?
questions.explanation: Use the exponential growth formula \(P = P_{0} e^{rt}\): \(P_{0}=120{,}000\), \(r = 0.028\) yr\(^{-1}\), \(t = 2025-2010 = 15\) yr. \(rt = 0.028 \times 15 = 0.42\). \(e^{0.42} \approx 1.521\). Therefore \(P \approx 120{,}000 \times 1.521 \approx 182{,}520\). Rounded to the nearest hundred gives 182,500 (option A). The other choices use either linear growth or incorrect rounding.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A remote‑sensing satellite orbits at a height of 800 km above the Earth's surface. Its imaging sensor has a focal length of 20 mm and each detector element (pixel) measures 10 µm. Using the relationship \[ \text{GSD} = \frac{H \times p}{f} \], where \(H\) is the satellite altitude, \(p\) is the pixel size, and \(f\) is the focal length, what is the ground sampling distance (GSD) in metres?
questions.explanation: Convert all quantities to metres: \(H = 800\,\text{km} = 800{,}000\,\text{m}\); \(p = 10\,\mu\text{m} = 10 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m} = 1\times10^{-5}\,\text{m}\); \(f = 20\,\text{mm} = 0.02\,\text{m}\). Plug into the formula: \(\text{GSD} = \frac{800{,}000 \times 1\times10^{-5}}{0.02} = \frac{8}{0.02} = 400\,\text{m}\). Option A matches; the others result from arithmetic or unit‑conversion errors.

questions.question_text: According to the Burgess concentric zone model of urban land use, which zone is most likely to be occupied predominantly by middle‑class families?
questions.explanation: In Burgess' concentric zone model, Zone IV is the outer residential zone where middle‑class families typically locate because it offers larger housing plots and a quieter environment while still being relatively close to the city centre. Zone I is commercial, Zone II is a mixed‑use transition area, and Zone III is dominated by working‑class housing.

questions.question_text: A topographic map has a scale of 1 cm : 2500 m. On the map the straight‑line distance between two villages is measured as 6.4 cm. What is the actual ground distance between the villages in kilometres?
questions.explanation: The map scale states that 1 cm on the map equals 2500 m on the ground, i.e., 2.5 km. Multiply the map distance by this factor: 6.4 cm × 2.5 km / cm = 16 km. Therefore option C is correct. Options A and B underestimate the conversion factor, while D overestimates it.

questions.question_text: Which type of volcanic eruption is most commonly associated with basaltic magma of low viscosity?
questions.explanation: Basaltic magma is hot and has low silica content, giving it low viscosity. Such magma tends to produce gentle, effusive eruptions that emit fluid lava flows, characteristic of Hawaiian eruptions. Plinian and Vulcanian eruptions involve highly viscous, silica‑rich magmas, while Strombolian eruptions are of intermediate viscosity. Hence option C is the correct choice.

questions.question_text: At a weather station situated at sea level, the temperature on a clear day at 12:00 LT is 30 °C. Assuming a constant dry adiabatic lapse rate of \(9.8^{\circ}\text{C km}^{-1}\), what temperature would be expected at the summit of a nearby mountain that is 2.3 km above sea level?
questions.explanation:
The dry adiabatic lapse rate \(\Gamma\) tells us that temperature decreases by 9.8 °C for each kilometre of ascent.

Drop = \(\Gamma 	imes h = 9.8^{\circ}	ext{C km}^{-1} 	imes 2.3\,	ext{km} = 22.54^{\circ}	ext{C}\).

Temperature at summit = \(30^{\circ}	ext{C} - 22.54^{\circ}	ext{C} \approx 7.46^{\circ}	ext{C}\), which rounds to 7.5 °C. Option B matches this result. The other options either use an incorrect lapse rate or ignore the height altogether.

questions.question_text: A town’s population was 85,000 in 2010 and 112,500 in 2015. Assuming exponential growth, estimate the population in 2020. Round to the nearest thousand.
questions.explanation:
Exponential growth follows \(P(t)=P_{0}e^{rt}\).
First find the annual growth rate \(r\):
\[r= rac{1}{5}\ln\left( rac{112,500}{85,000}ight)= rac{1}{5}\ln(1.3235)=0.05612\,	ext{yr}^{-1}\]
Project another 5 years to 2020: \[P_{2020}=112,500	imes e^{0.05612	imes5}=112,500	imes e^{0.2806}=112,500	imes1.3235\approx148,500\]
Rounded to the nearest thousand gives 149,000, which is option B. Options A and C are too low/high because they use incorrect rates, while D is far beyond the projected value.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A remote sensing satellite is in a circular orbit at an altitude of 700 km above the Earth’s surface. Its sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.03°. Using the diagram below, calculate the width of the ground swath (in km) that the sensor captures at nadir. (Assume a flat‑Earth approximation for the calculation.)
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='160' y1='180' x2='160' y2='20' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='20' x2='100' y2='80' stroke='red' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='20' x2='220' y2='80' stroke='red' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='160,20 165,30 155,30' fill='red'/><text x='165' y='50' font-size='12' fill='black'>0.03°</text><text x='150' y='190' font-size='12' fill='black'>Satellite</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
The ground swath width at nadir is given by \(W = 2H	an(	heta/2)\).
Convert the IFOV to radians: \(	heta = 0.03^{\circ} 	imes  rac{\pi}{180}=0.0005236\,	ext{rad}\).
Half‑angle: \(	heta/2 = 0.0002618\,	ext{rad}\).
For small angles, \(	an(	heta/2) \approx 	heta/2 \).
Thus \(W \approx 2 	imes 700\,	ext{km} 	imes 0.0002618 = 0.3665\,	ext{km}\).
Rounded to two decimals this is 0.37 km, matching option B. Options A and C result from using incorrect conversion or forgetting the factor of 2, while D overestimates the swath width.

questions.question_text: A topographic map of a region uses a scale of 1 cm : 4 km. On the map, two settlements A and B are located 6.0 cm apart. What is the ground distance between the settlements?
questions.explanation: The map scale tells us that 1 cm on the map represents 4 km on the ground. Multiply the map distance by the scale factor: 6.0 cm × 4 km/cm = 24 km. Therefore option C is correct. Options A (12 km) and B (20 km) result from using a smaller scale factor, while D (30 km) would require a factor of 5 km/cm, which does not match the given scale.

questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes the difference in thickness between continental and oceanic crust?

options.text: Continental crust averages about 35 km thick, while oceanic crust averages about 5 km.
Continental crust averages about 5 km thick, while oceanic crust averages about 35 km.
Both continental and oceanic crust have similar thickness, around 10 km.
Continental crust is thinner than oceanic crust, averaging 2 km compared to 7 km.

questions.explanation: Continental crust is typically 30–45 km thick, averaging around 35 km, because it is composed mainly of less dense granitic rocks. Oceanic crust is much thinner, 5–10 km thick, averaging about 5 km, being mainly basaltic. Option A states this correctly. Options B, C, and D invert or misrepresent the established thickness values.

questions.question_text: The climate data for the town of Riverton show an average annual temperature of 22 °C and total annual precipitation of 850 mm, with a pronounced dry season from November to March where monthly precipitation is less than 20 mm. Which Köppen climate classification best fits Riverton?
questions.explanation: An Aw (tropical savanna) climate is characterized by a high mean temperature year‑round, total precipitation sufficient to avoid desert conditions, and a distinct dry season where monthly rainfall falls below 60 mm (often <20 mm). Riverton’s climate matches these criteria: 22 °C mean temperature, 850 mm annual rainfall, and a five‑month dry period. Cfa and Csb have cooler winters, while BSh is drier overall, making them unsuitable.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 80,000 in 2015. During 2015–2020 the town experienced 4,800 births, 2,400 deaths, 3,200 immigrants, and 1,600 emigrants. What was the town’s population at the end of 2020?
questions.explanation: Use the demographic balancing equation: \(P_{2020}=P_{2015}+ (B-D)+(I-E)\). \(B-D = 4,800-2,400 = 2,400\). \(I-E = 3,200-1,600 = 1,600\). Total change = 2,400 + 1,600 = 4,000. Adding to the 2015 base: 80,000 + 4,000 = 84,000. Hence option B is correct. Options A, C, and D correspond to under‑ or over‑estimating the net change.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a nadir‑pointing satellite sensor captures a rectangular ground footprint that is 10 km wide (across‑track) and 15 km long (along‑track). If the sensor records a digital number of 180 for a pixel covering the entire footprint, what is the average radiance per square kilometre represented by that pixel, assuming the sensor’s calibration constant is 0.5 W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹ per digital unit?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="60" y="50" width="200" height="100" fill="none" stroke="black"/><text x="160" y="40" font-size="14" text-anchor="middle">Sensor footprint</text></svg>
questions.explanation: First convert the digital number to total radiance: 180 DN × 0.5 W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹/DN = 90 W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹. The ground area of the pixel is 10 km × 15 km = 150 km². Average radiance per km² = 90 / 150 = 0.6 W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹ km⁻². Therefore option B is correct. Options A, C, and D arise from mis‑calculating either the total radiance or the area.

questions.question_text: A map uses a scale of 1 cm : 2 km. Two towns are plotted with a north‑south separation of 3.5 cm and an east‑west separation of 2.0 cm. What is the bearing from Town X to Town Y measured clockwise from north?
questions.explanation:
First convert the map separations to real distances using the scale 1 cm = 2 km.
North‑south distance: 3.5 cm × 2 km/cm = 7 km (north).
East‑west distance: 2.0 cm × 2 km/cm = 4 km (east).
The bearing \(\theta\) from X to Y is given by \(\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\text{east}}{\text{north}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{7}\right) \approx 29.7^{\circ}\). Rounded to the nearest whole degree, the bearing is 030°, which corresponds to option A. Option B (045°) would require equal east and north components. Option C (060°) implies a larger east component. Option D (075°) would mean the east component is much larger than the north component, which is not the case.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements best explains why continental crust generally stands higher than oceanic crust?

options.text: Continental crust is thicker and less dense than oceanic crust, giving it greater buoyancy.
Continental crust is younger and therefore hotter, causing it to expand upward.
Oceanic crust contains more silica, making it lighter than continental crust.
Continental crust is supported by underlying mantle plumes that push it upward.

questions.explanation: Isostasy states that a lithospheric block will float at an elevation where its weight is balanced by the buoyant force of the underlying mantle. Continental crust has an average thickness of about 35–40 km and a mean density of ~2.7 g cm⁻³, whereas oceanic crust is thinner (≈7 km) and denser (~2.9 g cm⁻³). The greater thickness and lower density of continental crust make it more buoyant, causing it to sit higher than oceanic crust. Option B is incorrect because continental crust is generally older, not younger. Option C reverses the composition: oceanic crust is mafic (basaltic) and richer in iron‑magnesium, not silica. Option D mentions mantle plumes, which are localized and do not explain the widespread elevation difference.

questions.question_text: During the boreal summer, the Indian subcontinent experiences a strong monsoon. Which mechanism primarily drives the onset of this monsoon system?

options.text: Differential heating creates a low‑pressure area over the land, drawing moist air from the ocean.
