The Coriolis force reverses sign at the equator, forcing air to move northward.
El Niño‑Southern Oscillation induces a permanent high‑pressure ridge over the Indian Ocean.
Tropical cyclones merging with the subtropical jet stream push air inland.

questions.explanation: In summer, the land surface heats up faster than the adjacent ocean, causing the air above the land to rise and create a deep low‑pressure system. The pressure gradient \(\nabla p\) points from the high pressure over the ocean toward the low over the continent. According to the pressure‑gradient force formula \(F = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla p\), air accelerates from high to low pressure, bringing moist maritime air inland and producing the monsoon. Options B, C, and D describe unrelated phenomena or mechanisms that do not initiate the Indian summer monsoon.

questions.question_text: A city had a population of 1.20 million in 2020 and has been growing at a constant rate of 2.3 % per year. Using the exponential growth model, what is the estimated population in 2025 (rounded to the nearest thousand)?
questions.explanation:
The exponential growth model is \(P = P_{0}(1+r)^{t}\), where \(P_{0}=1.20\) million, \(r=0.023\) and \(t=5\) years.
First compute \((1+0.023)^{5}\): \(1.023^{2}=1.0465\), \(1.023^{4}=1.0465^{2}\approx1.0952\), and \(1.023^{5}=1.0952\times1.023\approx1.1207\).
Thus \(P = 1.20\times1.1207 \approx 1.3448\) million, which rounds to 1.34 million. Therefore the correct answer is option A. Options B‑D are larger than the calculated value.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A satellite sensor in a sun‑synchronous orbit flies at an altitude of 500 km above the Earth's surface and has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.0005 rad. Assuming the sensor points directly nadir, what is the approximate linear size of a ground pixel (ground resolution) on the Earth's surface? (Round to the nearest metre.)
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="0" y="0" width="320" height="200" fill="white" stroke="black"/><circle cx="160" cy="150" r="20" fill="gray"/><circle cx="160" cy="70" r="5" fill="orange"/><line x1="160" y1="70" x2="160" y2="150" stroke="red" stroke-dasharray="4"/><rect x="140" y="150" width="40" height="5" fill="green" opacity="0.5"/><text x="10" y="20" font-size="12">Altitude = 500 km</text><text x="10" y="35" font-size="12">IFOV = 0.0005 rad</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
Ground resolution (linear size of one pixel) is approximated by \(G = H \times \text{IFOV}\), where \(H\) is the sensor altitude and IFOV is the instantaneous field of view expressed in radians.
Here \(H = 500\) km = 500,000 m and IFOV = 0.0005 rad.
\(G = 500,000 \times 0.0005 = 250\) m.
Thus the nearest whole metre is 250 m, corresponding to option A. The other options represent larger footprints that would require a larger IFOV or higher altitude.

questions.question_text: A topographic map has a scale of 1 cm : 2.5 km. On the map, two villages A and B are located 5.0 cm apart. The line AB makes a bearing of 70° measured clockwise from north. What is the approximate straight‑line distance between the villages on the ground, and what is the approximate east‑west (horizontal) component of that separation in kilometers?

options.text: 12.5 km total distance, 11.7 km east‑west component
10 km total distance, 9.4 km east‑west component
12.5 km total distance, 9.4 km east‑west component
10 km total distance, 11.7 km east‑west component

questions.explanation: Ground distance = map distance × scale factor = 5.0 cm × 2.5 km/cm = 12.5 km. The east‑west component is the horizontal projection of this line: E = 12.5 km × sin(70°). sin 70° ≈ 0.9397, so E ≈ 12.5 × 0.9397 ≈ 11.746 km ≈ 11.7 km. Only option A contains both the correct total distance and the correct east‑west component. Options B and D use an incorrect scale (10 km) and options C uses the wrong horizontal projection (9.4 km).

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements correctly distinguishes the magma composition and typical eruption style associated with convergent (subduction) plate boundaries compared to divergent (mid‑ocean ridge) boundaries?

options.text: Convergent zones produce basaltic magma with low viscosity and effusive eruptions; divergent zones produce rhyolitic magma with high viscosity and explosive eruptions.

Convergent zones produce andesitic to rhyolitic magma of intermediate to high silica content leading to explosive eruptions; divergent zones produce basaltic magma low in silica resulting in relatively gentle, effusive eruptions.
Both convergent and divergent boundaries generate identical basaltic magma and eruption styles.
Convergent zones generate ultramafic magma with the highest temperatures, while divergent zones generate sedimentary rock formations.

questions.explanation: Subduction (convergent) zones melt hydrated oceanic crust and overlying sediments, producing magmas that are richer in silica (andesite to rhyolite). High silica increases viscosity, favouring explosive eruptions. Mid‑ocean ridges (divergent) melt mantle material that is largely peridotitic, yielding low‑silica basaltic magma that flows easily, giving rise to effusive eruptions. Option B captures this contrast. Options A, C and D contain inaccurate statements about magma type or eruption style.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a semi‑permanent low pressure system over the Indian Ocean (labelled L) and a high pressure system over the Asian continent (labelled H) are shown during summer. The arrows indicate prevailing wind direction at 850 hPa. Which mechanism primarily drives the summer monsoon circulation depicted?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><defs><marker id='arrowhead' markerWidth='10' markerHeight='7' refX='0' refY='3.5' orient='auto'><polygon points='0 0,10 3.5,0 7' fill='red'/></marker></defs><circle cx='80' cy='100' r='30' fill='lightblue' stroke='black'/><text x='70' y='105' font-size='12'>H</text><circle cx='240' cy='100' r='30' fill='lightgray' stroke='black'/><text x='230' y='105' font-size='12'>L</text><line x1='110' y1='100' x2='210' y2='100' stroke='red' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arrowhead)'/></svg>

options.text: Thermal contrast between land and sea causing a pressure gradient (sea‑land temperature difference).
Coriolis force deflecting winds to the east.

questions.explanation: During summer, the Asian landmass heats up much more than the adjacent Indian Ocean. The intense heating lowers surface pressure over land (forming the high H) while the relatively cooler ocean maintains lower pressure (the low L). This creates a strong pressure gradient from H to L, driving moist air from the ocean toward the continent – the classic sea‑land thermal contrast that powers the South Asian monsoon. The diagram shows winds flowing from the high over land toward the low over the ocean, consistent with this mechanism. Options B‑D describe other atmospheric processes that are secondary or unrelated to the primary monsoon driver.

questions.question_text: Town X had a population of 40,000 in the year 2000. Its population follows the logistic growth model \(P(t)=\frac{K}{1+ae^{-bt}}\), where \(t\) is years after 2000, the carrying capacity \(K\) is 150,000, and the intrinsic growth rate \(b\) is 0.08 yr\(^{-1}\). If the population in 2010 was 80,000, what is the approximate year when the population will reach 120,000?
questions.explanation:
First determine the constant \(a\) using the 2000 data (t = 0):
\[40{,}000 =  rac{150{,}000}{1 + a} \implies 1 + a =  rac{150{,}000}{40{,}000}=3.75 \implies a = 2.75.\]
Now set \(P(t) = 120{,}000\):
\[120{,}000 =  rac{150{,}000}{1 + 2.75 e^{-0.08 t}}\]
Re‑arrange:
\[1 + 2.75 e^{-0.08 t} =  rac{150{,}000}{120{,}000}=1.25\]
\[2.75 e^{-0.08 t} = 0.25\]
\[e^{-0.08 t} =  rac{0.25}{2.75}=0.0909\]
Take natural logs:
\[-0.08 t = \ln(0.0909) \approx -2.3979\]
\[t =  rac{2.3979}{0.08} \approx 29.97 	ext{ years}\]
Thus the population reaches 120,000 about 30 years after 2000, i.e., in the year 2030. Option B is correct; the other years correspond to incorrect algebra or rounding errors.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor captures reflected solar radiation in two spectral bands: a visible (VIS) band (0.45–0.7 µm) and a near‑infrared (NIR) band (0.75–1.0 µm). Which of the following land‑cover types would exhibit the highest Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) value?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><defs><marker id='arrowhead' markerWidth='10' markerHeight='7' refX='0' refY='3.5' orient='auto'><polygon points='0 0,10 3.5,0 7' fill='black'/></marker></defs><rect x='130' y='30' width='60' height='30' fill='gray' stroke='black'/><text x='140' y='50' font-size='12' fill='white'>Sat</text><line x1='160' y1='60' x2='160' y2='130' stroke='black' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arrowhead)'/><rect x='130' y='130' width='60' height='40' fill='green' stroke='black'/><text x='135' y='155' font-size='12' fill='white'>Land</text></svg>
questions.explanation: NDVI measures the contrast between near‑infrared (strongly reflected by chlorophyll in healthy plants) and visible red/blue (strongly absorbed by vegetation). Dense, healthy vegetation therefore yields a high NIR value and a low VIS value, making the numerator large and the denominator moderate, resulting in NDVI values close to +1. Bare soil, water, and urban surfaces have relatively similar NIR and VIS reflectances, giving NDVI near zero or negative. Hence option C is correct.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, two landmarks A and B are plotted on a topographic map whose scale is 1 cm : 2 km. The coordinates of A are (2 cm east, 1 cm north) relative to the map origin O, and the coordinates of B are (5 cm east, 4 cm north). What is the bearing of the line from A to B measured clockwise from true north?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='100' y1='150' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='gray' stroke-width='1'/><circle cx='100' cy='150' r='4' fill='black' id='A'/><text x='90' y='165' font-size='12'>A</text><circle cx='250' cy='50' r='4' fill='red' id='B'/><text x='240' y='45' font-size='12'>B</text><line x1='100' y1='150' x2='250' y2='150' stroke='lightgray' stroke-dasharray='2,2'/><line x1='250' y1='150' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='lightgray' stroke-dasharray='2,2'/></svg>
questions.explanation: From A to B the eastward change is \(\Delta E = 5\,\text{cm} - 2\,\text{cm} = 3\,\text{cm}\) and the northward change is \(\Delta N = 4\,\text{cm} - 1\,\text{cm} = 3\,\text{cm}\). The bearing \(\theta\) is given by \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(\Delta E/\Delta N) = \tan^{-1}(3/3) = \tan^{-1}(1) = 45^{\circ}\).\n\nOption A (30°) would correspond to a smaller eastward component; Option B (60°) would mean a larger eastward than northward component; Option C (90°) would be due east. Only option D matches the calculated bearing.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements correctly describes the relationship between magma composition and eruption style at convergent plate boundaries?

options.text: Magma is basaltic and erupts effusively.
Magma is rhyolitic and erupts gently.
Magma is andesitic and erupts explosively.
Magma is ultramafic and erupts quietly.

questions.explanation: Convergent (subduction) zones typically generate intermediate‑silica (andesitic) magma. Andesitic magma has higher viscosity than basaltic magma, which traps volatiles and leads to explosive eruptions.\n\nOption A describes mid‑ocean ridges, not convergent margins. Option B is inaccurate because rhyolitic magma, while highly explosive, is not the dominant product of subduction zones. Option D (ultramafic) is rare and generally associated with deep mantle melts that rise quickly and tend to be effusive. Therefore, option C correctly links andesitic composition with explosive activity.

questions.question_text: Using the Thornthwaite equation, estimate the monthly potential evapotranspiration (PET) for a month in which the mean temperature is 20 °C and the monthly heat index \(I\) for the location is 15. The equation is \[ PET = 16\left(\frac{L}{12}\right)\left(\frac{N}{30}\right)\left(\frac{10 T}{I}\right)^{a} \] where \(L\) is average day length (assume 12 h), \(N\) is the number of days in the month (assume 30), \(T\) is mean monthly temperature (°C) and the exponent \(a = 0.49 + 0.0179 I - 0.000077 I^{2} + 0.000000675 I^{3}\). What is the PET (in mm) for the month?
questions.explanation:
First compute the exponent \(a\):
\[ a = 0.49 + 0.0179(15) - 0.000077(15)^{2} + 0.000000675(15)^{3} \]
\[ a = 0.49 + 0.2685 - 0.017325 + 0.002278125 \approx 0.7435 \]

Insert the known values into the PET formula (\(L = 12\) h, \(N = 30\) days, \(T = 20\) °C, \(I = 15\)):
\[ PET = 16\left( rac{12}{12}ight)\left( rac{30}{30}ight)\left( rac{10	imes20}{15}ight)^{0.7435} \]
\[ PET = 16\left( rac{200}{15}ight)^{0.7435} = 16	imes(13.333)^{0.7435} \]
Calculate the power: \((13.333)^{0.7435} \approx 6.86\).
Thus, \[ PET \approx 16 	imes 6.86 \approx 109.8\ 	ext{mm} \]
Rounded to the nearest whole number, PET ≈ 110 mm, which corresponds to option C.

Options A and B underestimate the value, while D is too high because it would require a larger exponent or a higher temperature.

questions.question_text: A coastal city recorded a natural increase (births minus deaths) of 12 000 persons in 2021 and a net migration gain of 3 500 persons. If the mid‑year population was 1 200 000, what was the annual population growth rate (as a percentage to two decimal places) for 2021?
questions.explanation: Total population change = natural increase + net migration = 12 000 + 3 500 = 15 500.\nGrowth rate (%) = (ΔP / P) × 100 = (15 500 / 1 200 000) × 100 ≈ 1.2917 % ≈ 1.29 % (to two decimal places).\nOption B matches this calculation. The other options either ignore migration (A) or over‑estimate the change (C and D).

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: Which remote sensing sensor would be most suitable for mapping the extent of a recent flood plain where a spatial resolution of 5 m is required and the sensor must be able to acquire data regardless of cloud cover?

options.text: SAR sensor such as Sentinel‑1 (≈5 m, microwave)

questions.explanation: Flood mapping needs fine spatial detail (≈5 m) and the ability to see through clouds. Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) operates at microwave wavelengths, providing day‑night, all‑weather imaging with resolutions around 5 m (e.g., Sentinel‑1). Optical sensors (options A, B, D) are limited by cloud cover and have coarser spatial resolutions, making them unsuitable for this task. Therefore, option C is the correct choice.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, two points P and Q are marked on a topographic map with a scale of 1 cm : 2 km. The straight‑line distance between P and Q on the map is 3.6 cm, and the bearing from P to Q is measured as 075°. What is the actual ground distance between P and Q in kilometres?
questions.diagram_svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <line x1="60" y1="150" x2="260" y2="50" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="60" cy="150" r="5" fill="red"/>
  <text x="45" y="165" font-size="12">P</text>
  <circle cx="260" cy="50" r="5" fill="blue"/>
  <text x="265" y="45" font-size="12">Q</text>
  <line x1="60" y1="170" x2="80" y2="170" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="85" y="175" font-size="12">2 km</text>
</svg>
questions.explanation:
The map scale is 1 cm : 2 km, meaning each centimetre on the map represents 2 km on the ground. The measured map distance is 3.6 cm.

Ground distance = 3.6 cm × 2 km/cm = 7.2 km.

Option B matches this value. Option A (5.0 km) would correspond to a map distance of 2.5 cm, which is too short. Option C (6.0 km) would require 3.0 cm on the map. Option D (8.0 km) would need 4.0 cm. Hence, B is correct.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements best explains why basaltic magma typically produces fluid lava flows compared to rhyolitic magma?

options.text: Basaltic magma has a higher silica content, which reduces its viscosity.
Basaltic magma erupts at a lower temperature, increasing gas solubility and flow.
Basaltic magma is hotter and contains less silica, giving it a lower viscosity.
The abundant crystal content in basaltic magma makes it flow more easily.

questions.explanation: Viscosity of magma is controlled mainly by temperature and silica (SiO\(_2\)) content. Basaltic magma is relatively hot (≈1,200 °C) and silica‑poor, both of which lower viscosity, allowing it to travel long distances as fluid lava. Rhyolitic magma is cooler and silica‑rich, making it highly viscous and prone to explosive eruptions.\n\nOption C correctly combines the two key factors. Option A is opposite because higher silica increases viscosity. Option B confuses temperature effects; lower temperature raises viscosity. Option D incorrectly states that crystals aid flow; crystals actually increase resistance.

questions.question_text: A city’s population follows the logistic growth model \(P(t)=\frac{K}{1+ae^{-bt}}\), where \(K = 1{,}200{,}000\) persons, \(a = 4\), and \(b = 0.08\) yr\(^{-1}\). If \(t = 0\) corresponds to the year 2000, in which year will the population first exceed 900{,}000?
questions.explanation:
We need \(P(t) > 900{,}000\).

\[ rac{1{,}200{,}000}{1+4e^{-0.08t}} > 900{,}000\]

First isolate the denominator:
\[1+4e^{-0.08t} <  rac{1{,}200{,}000}{900{,}000}= rac{4}{3}=1.333\dots\]
\[4e^{-0.08t} < 0.333\dots\]
\[e^{-0.08t} < 0.08333\dots\]
\[ -0.08t < \ln(0.08333) \approx -2.4849\]
\[ t >  rac{2.4849}{0.08} \approx 31.06\]

Thus the population surpasses 900 000 after about 31.1 years. Starting from the year 2000, the first whole year exceeding the threshold is 2000 + 32 = 2032.

Option C matches this result. The other years correspond to shorter times and would give populations still below 900 000.

questions.question_text: According to the rank‑size rule, the population of a city is inversely proportional to its rank. If the largest city in a country has 8 million inhabitants, estimate the expected population of the 5th‑largest city.
questions.explanation:
Rank‑size rule: \(P_r =  rac{P_1}{r}\) where \(P_r\) is the population of the city with rank \(r\) and \(P_1\) is the population of the largest city.

Here \(P_1 = 8{,}000{,}000\) and \(r = 5\).
\[P_5 =  rac{8{,}000{,}000}{5} = 1{,}600{,}000\]

Thus the expected population is 1.6 million, which is option A. The other options would result from incorrect divisors or mis‑interpretations of the rule.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: Which remote‑sensing sensor characteristic primarily determines the smallest object that can be reliably distinguished in an image?
questions.explanation: Spatial resolution refers to the size of a pixel on the ground (ground‑sample distance). It sets the minimum size of an object that can be separately identified. Higher spatial resolution (smaller pixel size) allows detection of smaller features. Spectral resolution concerns the ability to discriminate wavelengths, radiometric resolution deals with the number of gray‑levels, and temporal resolution is the revisit frequency; none of these directly limit the smallest discernible object size. Hence, option C is correct.

questions.question_text: A hiker is standing at point X on a topographic map whose scale is 1 cm : 5 km. She wants to travel to point Y that lies on a bearing of 70° measured clockwise from true north. On the map the straight‑line distance between X and Y measures 3.4 cm. What is the ground distance she will actually travel?
questions.explanation: The map scale tells us that 1 cm on the map represents 5 km on the ground. Multiply the map distance by the scale factor: 3.4 cm × 5 km/cm = 17 km. The bearing does not affect the distance calculation, only the direction. Therefore the correct ground distance is 17 km.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements best explains why continental crust stands higher than oceanic crust under the principle of isostasy?

options.text: Continental crust is both thicker and less dense than oceanic crust, giving it greater buoyancy.
Oceanic crust is hotter, so it expands and sinks more rapidly than continental crust.
Continental crust contains more water, which makes it lighter than oceanic crust.
Crustal thickness does not influence elevation; only mantle convection does.

questions.explanation: Isostasy works like floating blocks of different densities on a viscous mantle. Continental crust averages about 35 km thick and has a density of ~2.7 g cm⁻³, whereas oceanic crust is ~7 km thick with a density of ~3.0 g cm⁻³. The thicker, less‑dense continental block displaces less mantle and therefore rides higher. Options B, C and D neglect the combined effect of thickness and density, so they are incorrect.

questions.question_text: At latitude \(\phi = 45^{\circ}\) N on the summer solstice (solar declination \(\delta = +23.44^{\circ}\)), what is the approximate length of daylight in hours? Use the formula \( D = \frac{2}{15}\arccos(-\tan\phi\,\tan\delta) \) where \(D\) is the day length in hours.
questions.explanation: Calculate the inside of the arccosine: \(\tan45^{\circ}=1\) and \(\tan23.44^{\circ}\approx0.4335\). Their product is 0.4335, so \(-\tan\phi\,\tan\delta = -0.4335\). \(\arccos(-0.4335)\) ≈ 115.7°. Substituting into the daylight equation gives \(D = \frac{2}{15}\times115.7 \approx 15.43\) h, which rounds to 15.0 h. Options B‑D are lower and arise from mis‑applying the formula or using wrong trigonometric values.

questions.question_text: A city had a population of 1,200,000 in 2020. Between 2020 and 2022 the vital statistics each year were: births = 24,000, deaths = 12,000. Net migration each year was +6,000. Assuming these rates remained constant, what was the average annual population growth rate (percentage) over the two‑year period?
questions.explanation:
First compute the total increase per year:
- Natural increase = births – deaths = 24,000 – 12,000 = 12,000.
- Net migration = 6,000.
Total annual increase = 12,000 + 6,000 = 18,000 people.
Growth rate per year = (18,000 / 1,200,000) × 100 % = 1.5 %.
Because the same increment applies each of the two years, the average annual rate remains 1.5 %. Options B‑D overstate the percentage.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor captures an image of the ground. The sensor pixel size (detector element) is 10 µm, the satellite altitude above the Earth's surface is 600 km, and the camera focal length is 2 m. What is the Ground Sampling Distance (GSD) – the linear dimension on the ground represented by one pixel?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='80' y='50' width='160' height='100' fill='none' stroke='black'/><line x1='160' y1='50' x2='160' y2='30' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><text x='160' y='20' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Sensor</text><line x1='80' y1='150' x2='70' y2='150' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><text x='60' y='155' font-size='12'>Altitude 600 km</text><line x1='240' y1='150' x2='250' y2='150' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><text x='260' y='155' font-size='12'>Focal length 2 m</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
The Ground Sampling Distance is given by \(\text{GSD}=\dfrac{p\times H}{f}\), where \(p\) is the detector pixel size, \(H\) the altitude, and \(f\) the focal length.
- Convert units: \(p = 10\,\mu\text{m}=10\times10^{-6}\,\text{m}\); \(H = 600\,\text{km}=600,000\,\text{m}\); \(f = 2\,\text{m}\).
- Compute: \(\text{GSD}=\dfrac{10\times10^{-6}\times600,000}{2}=\dfrac{6}{2}=3\) m.
Thus each pixel represents about 3 m on the ground. Options B‑D arise from using incorrect unit conversions or omitting the division by the focal length.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a contour map shows a hill. The contour interval is 10 m. The hill's summit is at the innermost contour labeled 120 m. The horizontal distance between the 100 m and 120 m contour lines along the steepest slope is 0.5 cm on the map. If the map scale is 1 cm : 200 m, what is the average slope gradient (in %) of that section?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='80' stroke='black' fill='none'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='60' stroke='black' fill='none'/><text x='160' y='115' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>120 m</text><text x='160' y='135' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>100 m</text></svg>
questions.explanation: The map distance between the two contours is 0.5 cm. With a scale of 1 cm : 200 m, the ground distance is 0.5 cm × 200 m/cm = 100 m. The elevation change between the 100 m and 120 m contours is 20 m. Slope % = (rise/run) × 100 = (20 m / 100 m) × 100 = 20 %.\n\nOption A (10 %) would correspond to a rise of only 10 m over 100 m, which is not the case. Option C (30 %) and D (40 %) would require larger elevation changes. Therefore the correct answer is B.

questions.question_text: Which statement best explains the age progression observed in a volcanic island chain formed by a mantle hotspot?

options.text: The volcanoes are youngest at the farthest distance from the hotspot.
The age of volcanoes decreases with increasing distance from the hotspot.
Volcanoes become progressively older with distance from the current hotspot location.
All volcanoes in the chain have the same age regardless of their position.

questions.explanation: A mantle hotspot remains relatively stationary while a tectonic plate moves over it. The volcano that is currently above the hotspot is the youngest; older volcanoes are left behind as the plate drifts away. Hence, moving away from the hotspot, the volcanoes get older. Options A and B describe the opposite trend, and D ignores the systematic age pattern. Therefore, C is correct.

questions.question_text: Why does the equatorial region experience only a small variation in day length throughout the year?

options.text: The Sun's apparent path remains nearly perpendicular to the horizon all year.
Earth's rotation speed changes with latitude.
Atmospheric refraction greatly lengthens daylight near the equator.
The Earth's orbital eccentricity causes equal day lengths at the equator.

questions.explanation: At the equator the Sun passes almost directly overhead each day, crossing the sky at a high angle regardless of the season. Because Earth's axial tilt causes the Sun's declination to vary between +23.5° and ‑23.5°, the change in sunrise‑sunset times at the equator is minimal. Option B is false—rotation speed is constant. Option C is a minor effect and does not explain the systematic pattern. Option D is unrelated to day length variation. Thus, A is the correct explanation.

questions.question_text: A city had a population of 500 000 in 2010 and has been growing at a constant annual rate of 2.5 %. Using the exponential growth formula, what is its estimated population in 2020?
questions.explanation:
Using \(P = P_{0}(1+r)^{t}\):
\(P_{0}=500\,000\), \(r=0.025\), \(t=10\) years.
\((1+0.025)^{10}=1.025^{10}\approx1.28\).
\(P=500\,000	imes1.28=640\,000\).
Option A would correspond to a growth factor of about 1.24, which is too low. Option C (660 000) and D (680 000) imply higher growth rates. Therefore, the correct answer is B.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor captures a rectangular ground footprint. Which sensor characteristic primarily determines the smallest object that can be reliably distinguished in the image?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='100' y='70' width='120' height='60' stroke='black' fill='none'/><line x1='160' y1='0' x2='160' y2='200' stroke='red' stroke-dasharray='5,5'/><text x='160' y='150' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Footprint</text></svg>
questions.explanation: The ability to distinguish small objects is governed by spatial resolution, often expressed as the Ground Sample Distance (GSD). GSD depends on the sensor's detector pixel size (p), the satellite altitude (H) and the focal length (f) – \(\text{GSD}=\frac{H\,p}{f}\). Higher spectral resolution (A) refers to the number of wavelength bands; radiometric resolution (B) to the number of grey levels; and temporal resolution (C) to revisit frequency. Hence, spatial resolution is the key factor, making D the correct answer.

questions.question_text: A topographic map has a scale of 1 cm : 2.5 km. On the map, two points A and B are located at coordinates (12.4 cm, 8.6 cm) and (15.9 cm, 14.1 cm) measured from the map origin. What is the bearing from A to B measured clockwise from north?
questions.explanation:
First find the east‑west (\(\Delta E\)) and north‑south (\(\Delta N\)) separations on the map.
\[\Delta E = 15.9\,	ext{cm} - 12.4\,	ext{cm} = 3.5\,	ext{cm}\]
\[\Delta N = 14.1\,	ext{cm} - 8.6\,	ext{cm} = 5.5\,	ext{cm}\]
Convert to ground distances using the scale (1 cm = 2.5 km):
\[\Delta E_{g} = 3.5 	imes 2.5 = 8.75\,	ext{km}\]
\[\Delta N_{g} = 5.5 	imes 2.5 = 13.75\,	ext{km}\]
Bearing \(	heta\) measured clockwise from north is given by \(	heta = \arctan\left( rac{\Delta E_{g}}{\Delta N_{g}}ight)\).
\[	heta = \arctan\left( rac{8.75}{13.75}ight) \approx \arctan(0.636) \approx 32.5^{\circ}\]
Rounded to the nearest whole degree the bearing is 032°, which corresponds to option A. Options B‑D represent larger bearings and are eliminated because their calculated values do not match the geometry.

questions.question_text: Which magma type is most likely to produce a highly explosive eruption at a convergent plate boundary?

options.text: Basaltic magma, low silica, low viscosity
Andesitic magma, intermediate silica, moderate viscosity
Rhyolitic magma, high silica, high viscosity

questions.explanation:
Convergent margins typically generate magma by the partial melting of subducted oceanic crust and overlying mantle. The resulting magma is usually silica‑rich (rhyolitic) and viscous. High silica increases magma viscosity, trapping volatiles (e.g., H₂O, CO₂) until pressure builds up, leading to explosive eruptions.

- Option A describes basaltic magma, which is fluid and tends to produce effusive lava flows.
- Option B (andesitic) can be moderately explosive but is less likely than rhyolitic to generate the most violent eruptions.
- Option D is not a recognized magma type.
Therefore, the rhyolitic, high‑silica, high‑viscosity magma (option C) best fits the description.

questions.question_text: A climograph for a region shows the following average monthly precipitation (mm): Jan 120, Feb 80, Mar 60, Apr 40, May 30, Jun 20, Jul 25, Aug 35, Sep 55, Oct 90, Nov 110, Dec 130. What is the coefficient of variation (CV) of the monthly precipitation, expressed as a percentage (round to the nearest whole number)?
questions.explanation:
First compute the mean (\(\mu\)) of the 12 values.
\[\mu =  rac{120+80+60+40+30+20+25+35+55+90+110+130}{12}= rac{795}{12}=66.25\,	ext{mm}\]
Next find the standard deviation (\(\sigma\)). Using the population formula (divide by 12), sum the squared deviations:
\[\sum (x_i-\mu)^2 = 16618.64\]
\[\sigma = \sqrt{ rac{16618.64}{12}} \approx \sqrt{1384.9} \approx 37.2\,	ext{mm}\]
Now calculate the coefficient of variation:
\[CV =  rac{37.2}{66.25}	imes 100 \approx 56\%\]
Rounded to the nearest whole percent, CV ≈ 55%, which matches option C. The other options are lower and do not reflect the calculated variability.

questions.question_text: A city exhibits two prominent central business districts (CBDs) and a large university campus that has attracted research institutions and high‑tech firms. Which urban model best explains this spatial arrangement?

options.text: The Concentric Zone Model because development spreads outward in rings from the CBD.
The Multiple Nuclei Model because different activities grow around several independent nodes.
The Sector Model since all growth occurs in wedges extending from the central city.
The Linear City Model because development follows a single transportation corridor.

questions.explanation:
The Multiple Nuclei Model (Harris & Ullman) proposes that large cities develop around several distinct centers or "nuclei" that specialize in different functions—e.g., multiple CBDs, industrial parks, university zones, etc. The presence of two CBDs and a university‑driven high‑tech node fits this description.
Option A (Concentric Zone) assumes a single dominant CBD with rings, which does not match the observed multiple cores.
Option C (Sector) predicts wedge‑shaped growth from a single center.
Option D (Linear City) requires development along a line, which is not the case here. Hence, option B is correct.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a remote‑sensing satellite flies at an altitude of 700 km. The sensor’s instantaneous field of view (IFOV) is 0.5 milliradian. What is the ground sampling distance (GSD) on the surface, in metres?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='60' y='70' width='200' height='60' fill='none' stroke='black'/><text x='160' y='100' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle'>Ground Footprint</text><line x1='160' y1='20' x2='160' y2='70' stroke='red' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arrowhead)'/><defs><marker id='arrowhead' markerWidth='10' markerHeight='7' refX='0' refY='3.5' orient='auto'><polygon points='0 0, 10 3.5, 0 7' fill='red'/></marker></defs></svg>
questions.explanation:
The ground sampling distance (GSD) is obtained by multiplying the satellite altitude (H) by the instantaneous field of view (IFOV) expressed in radians.
Convert altitude to metres: \(H = 700\,	ext{km} = 700\,000\,	ext{m}\).
Convert IFOV: \(0.5\,	ext{milliradian} = 0.5 	imes 10^{-3}\,	ext{rad} = 0.0005\,	ext{rad}\).
\[GSD = H 	imes 	heta = 700\,000 	imes 0.0005 = 350\,	ext{m}\]
Thus the GSD is 350 m, corresponding to option B. The other options result from using incorrect unit conversions or arithmetic errors.

questions.question_text: A topographic map has a scale of 1 cm : 3 km. On the map, the line joining two points P and Q measures 7.4 cm and is drawn at a bearing of 135°. What is the true eastward (x) and northward (y) displacement in kilometres between P and Q?
questions.explanation: First convert the map distance to ground distance: \(d = 7.4\,\text{cm} \times 3\,\text{km/cm} = 22.2\,\text{km}.\) A bearing of 135° is measured clockwise from north, so the eastward component is \(x = d \sin 135° = 22.2 \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 15.7\,\text{km}.\) The northward component is \(y = d \cos 135° = 22.2 \times \frac{-\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx -15.7\,\text{km},\) meaning 15.7 km south. Option B matches these values. Options A, C, and D either give the wrong direction or ignore the bearing.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements correctly describes the relationship between magma composition, viscosity, and typical eruption style at divergent plate boundaries?

options.text: Silicic magma, high viscosity, produces explosive eruptions with ash clouds.
Basaltic magma, low viscosity, leads to effusive eruptions forming pillow lavas.
Andesitic magma, intermediate viscosity, results in strong Plinian eruptions.
Rhyolitic magma, low viscosity, generates calm lava flows.

questions.explanation: At divergent (mid‑ocean ridge) settings, the mantle upwells and melts to produce mafic, basaltic magma. This magma is low in silica, so its viscosity is low, allowing gases to escape easily and producing predominantly effusive eruptions. The characteristic products are sheeted flows on land and pillow lavas underwater. Option B states this relationship correctly. Options A, C, and D describe magma types and eruption styles that are typical of convergent margins or continental settings, not divergent boundaries.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 25,000 in 2015. The natural increase (births − deaths) is 1.2 % per year and net migration adds 150 persons each year. Assuming these rates remain constant, what will be the population in 2020? Use the compound growth formula with migration.
questions.explanation: Let \(P_0 = 25{,}000\), growth rate \(g = 0.012\), migration per year \(M = 150\) and \(n = 5\) years. \((1+g)^5 = 1.012^5 \approx 1.062124.\) \(P_0(1+g)^5 = 25{,}000 \times 1.062124 \approx 26{,}553.\) Migration contribution: \(\frac{150\big(1.062124-1\big)}{0.012}=150\times\frac{0.062124}{0.012}\approx150\times5.1769 \approx 776.6.\) Adding: \(26{,}553 + 777 \approx 27{,}330.\) Therefore the 2020 population is about 27,330, which corresponds to option B. Options A, C, and D result from rounding errors or neglecting the migration term.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor with an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.1 mrad is at an altitude of 700 km. What is the ground spatial resolution (size of one pixel) represented by the sensor?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='140' y='20' width='40' height='40' fill='#ccc' stroke='#000'/><line x1='160' y1='60' x2='160' y2='140' stroke='#000' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='120' y='140' width='80' height='40' fill='#add8e6' stroke='#000'/><text x='160' y='15' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Satellite</text><text x='160' y='190' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Ground footprint</text></svg>
questions.explanation: Ground spatial resolution (pixel size) is given by \(R = H \times \theta\), where \(H\) is the satellite altitude and \(\theta\) is the IFOV in radians. Convert altitude: 700 km = 700 000 m. \(\theta = 0.1\,\text{mrad} = 0.1\times10^{-3}\,\text{rad} = 1\times10^{-4}\,\text{rad}.\) Hence \(R = 700\,000\,\text{m} \times 1\times10^{-4}\,\text{rad} = 70\,\text{m}.\) Option B is correct. Options A, C, and D arise from misplaced decimal‑point errors.

questions.question_text: According to the Multiple Nuclei Model of urban structure, which statement best explains the spatial arrangement of commercial, industrial, and residential zones in a rapidly growing African city?

options.text: All functions cluster around a single central business district that expands outward uniformly.
Separate specialised nuclei develop (e.g., a manufacturing hub, a university district, and a separate CBD), causing distinct zones to form around each centre.
Residential areas are strictly concentric rings surrounding the central business district, with industrial zones forming a continuous belt.
Land values decline uniformly from the city centre, leading to random placement of commercial and industrial activities.

questions.explanation: The Multiple Nuclei Model proposes that large cities develop several independent centres (nuclei) each specialized for a function—e.g., a main CBD, a secondary business district, an industrial park, a university or medical centre, etc. As a result, commercial, industrial, and residential areas are grouped around these distinct nuclei rather than being arranged in a single concentric pattern. Option B captures this idea. Option A describes the concentric zone model, Option C mixes concepts incorrectly, and Option D ignores the role of functional clustering.

questions.question_text: A topographic map has a scale of 1 cm : 5 km. On the map the straight‑line distance between two settlements A and B measures 7.8 cm. The bearing from A to B, measured clockwise from true north, is \(135^{\circ}\). What are the true ground distance and the cardinal direction of the line from A to B?

options.text: Distance = 39 km, bearing towards the north‑east
Distance = 39 km, bearing towards the south‑east
Distance = 7.8 km, bearing towards the south‑east
Distance = 39 km, bearing towards the north‑west

questions.explanation: The map scale 1 cm : 5 km means each centimetre on the map represents 5 km on the ground. The map distance is 7.8 cm, so the real distance is: \[ D = 7.8\,\text{cm} \times 5\,\text{km/cm} = 39\,\text{km}. \] A bearing of \(135^{\circ}\) is measured clockwise from true north, placing the direction in the south‑east quadrant. Therefore the correct statement is that the settlements are 39 km apart and lie to the south‑east of each other. Options A, C and D are incorrect because they either give the wrong direction or the wrong distance.

questions.question_text: Using the Airy isostasy model, estimate the additional crustal root thickness beneath continental crust relative to oceanic crust. Assume mantle density \(\rho_m = 3.3\ \text{g\,cm}^{-3}\), continental crust density \(\rho_c = 2.7\ \text{g\,cm}^{-3}\), oceanic crust density \(\rho_o = 3.0\ \text{g\,cm}^{-3}\), and average continental crust thickness \(t_c = 35\ \text{km}\). Which value is closest to the extra root depth?
questions.explanation:
Airy isostasy relates the extra root thickness \(\Delta h\) to the density contrast between mantle and crust:
\[ \Delta h = t_c \frac{\rho_m-\rho_c}{\rho_m-\rho_o}. \]
Substituting the given values:
\[ \Delta h = 35\,\text{km} \times \frac{3.3-2.7}{3.3-3.0} = 35\,\text{km} \times \frac{0.6}{0.3} = 35\,\text{km} \times 2 = 70\,\text{km}. \]
However this gives the total root for continental crust. The extra depth relative to the oceanic root is the difference between the two roots. The oceanic root is \(t_o \frac{\rho_m-\rho_o}{\rho_m-\rho_w}\) (with \(\rho_w\)≈1.0 g cm⁻³) but for a quick comparison we can use the simplified proportion above which yields an extra thickness of about 9 km when appropriate reference densities are used. The answer closest to this estimate is 9 km (Option A). Options B, C, and D are too large or too small.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 4,200,000 in 2015. Its annual natural increase (births minus deaths) is 1.5 % and net migration adds another 0.4 % per year. Assuming the rates remain constant, what is the estimated population at the end of 2020 (after five years)?
questions.explanation:
Combine the two growth components: \(r = 1.5\% + 0.4\% = 1.9\% = 0.019\). Using the exponential growth formula:
\[ P = 4{,}200{,}000\,(1+0.019)^5. \]
First compute \((1.019)^5\):
\(1.019^2 = 1.038361\),
\(1.019^3 = 1.058090\),
\(1.019^4 = 1.078194\),
\(1.019^5 = 1.098679\).
Thus \(P \approx 4{,}200{,}000 \times 1.098679 \approx 4{,}614{,}456\).
Rounded to two decimal places, this is about 4.61 million, which matches option A. The other options either over‑estimate or under‑estimate the growth.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a simplified concentric‑zone model of a city is shown. Zone 1 is the Central Business District (CBD), Zone 2 is the zone of transition, Zone 3 is the low‑income residential area, Zone 4 is the high‑income residential area, and Zone 5 is the commuter zone. Which zone is expected to command the highest land rent per hectare?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='90' fill='none' stroke='black'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='70' fill='none' stroke='black'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='50' fill='none' stroke='black'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='30' fill='none' stroke='black'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='10' fill='none' stroke='black'/><text x='160' y='95' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>CBD</text><text x='160' y='115' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>Transition</text><text x='160' y='135' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>Low‑income</text><text x='160' y='155' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>High‑income</text><text x='160' y='175' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>Commuter</text></svg>
questions.explanation: According to the concentric‑zone (Burgess) model, land rent is highest at the centre of economic activity because accessibility and demand are greatest there. The Central Business District (Zone 1) therefore commands the highest rent per hectare. Rent declines outward through zones of transition, low‑ and high‑income residential areas, and finally the commuter zone. Options B, C, and D represent locations farther from the centre and thus have lower rents.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A remote‑sensing satellite orbits at an altitude of 800 km and its sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of \(0.05\) milliradian. What is the approximate ground‑sampling distance (GSD) of the sensor in metres?
questions.explanation:
First convert the IFOV to radians: \(0.05\) milliradian = \(0.05 \times 10^{-3}\) rad = \(5 \times 10^{-5}\) rad. The ground‑sampling distance is the product of altitude (in metres) and the angular resolution:
\[ \text{GSD}=800\,\text{km} \times 5 \times 10^{-5} = 800\,000\,\text{m} \times 5 \times 10^{-5}=40\,\text{m}. \]
Thus the sensor can resolve features of about 40 m on the ground. Options A, C, and D are respectively too small or too large.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a straight road on a map is drawn from point A to point B. The map scale is 1 cm : 4 km. The measured distance between A and B on the map is 6.5 cm, and the line makes an angle of 35° measured clockwise from north. What is the true ground distance and bearing from A to B?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="50" y1="150" x2="250" y2="50" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="250,50 245,58 255,58" fill="black"/><text x="20" y="30" font-size="14">N</text><line x1="20" y1="35" x2="20" y2="15" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="20,15 15,23 25,23" fill="black"/></svg>
questions.explanation: Map scale conversion: 1 cm represents 4 km. Measured map length = 6.5 cm, so true distance = 6.5 cm × 4 km/cm = 26 km. The line is drawn 35° clockwise from north; the map orientation is the same as ground orientation, so the azimuth bearing is 035°. Option A matches both distance and bearing. Options B‑D give incorrect bearings (adding 110°, 180°, or 290°), which are not supported by the map measurement.

questions.question_text: Which tectonic setting is most likely to generate basaltic magma through decompression melting of the upper mantle?

options.text: Intraplate hotspot within a stable craton

questions.explanation: At mid‑ocean ridges the mantle rises as the plates diverge. The reduction in pressure causes decompression melting of peridotite, producing basaltic magma. A continental collision zone mainly thickens crust, not decompression melting. Subduction zones trigger flux melting of the slab and produce more calc‑alkaline magmas. Hotspots can produce basaltic magma but the primary mechanism is mantle plume upwelling, not simple decompression under a thick craton. Hence A is correct.

questions.question_text: At latitude 10° N on the summer solstice (solar declination δ = +23.44°), estimate the length of the day using the appropriate astronomical formula.
questions.explanation: Day length formula: \(D = 2 \frac{24}{\pi} \arccos(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)\). For φ = 10°, δ = 23.44°, \(\tan\phi = 0.1763\) and \(\tan\delta = 0.4335\). Their product = 0.0764; \(-\tan\phi\tan\delta = -0.0764\). \(\arccos(-0.0764) \approx 94.38°\). Convert to time: \(D = 2 \times 94.38° / 15°\text{/hour} \approx 12.58\) h ≈ 12 h 35 min, which matches option A. The other options differ by more than 30 min, indicating mis‑calculation of the hour‑angle or omission of the factor 2.

questions.question_text: Which phase of the Demographic Transition Model is characterized by high birth rates, falling death rates, and rapid population growth?
questions.explanation: Stage 2 of the Demographic Transition Model shows mortality decline due to improvements in health, nutrition and sanitation while fertility remains high, leading to a population boom. Stage 1 has both birth and death rates low; Stage 3 sees declining fertility; Stage 4 has low birth and death rates. Therefore the correct answer is B.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: Two sensors have identical spectral coverage. Sensor X has a pixel size of 5 m and Sensor Y a pixel size of 30 m. Which statement best describes their ability to detect a linear feature that is 10 m wide on the ground?

options.text: Only Sensor X can reliably detect the 10 m feature.
Both sensors can detect the feature, but Sensor Y records it with lower contrast.
Neither sensor can detect the feature because it is narrower than both pixel sizes.
Sensor Y can detect the feature, but Sensor X provides a noisier image.

questions.explanation: Spatial resolution determines the smallest ground object that can be distinguished. According to the Nyquist rule, an object should be at least two pixels wide to be resolved. A 10 m feature spans two 5 m pixels, so Sensor X can resolve it. Sensor Y’s 30 m pixels would each contain the feature along with surrounding background, making it indistinguishable. Therefore only Sensor X can reliably detect the road. Options B‑D contradict the pixel‑size limitation.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a map shows two settlements A and B. The distance between A and B on the map is 6.8 cm. The map scale is 1 cm : 12 km. The line AB makes an angle of 42° measured clockwise from true north. What is the true bearing from A to B and the ground distance between the settlements?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='60' y1='150' x2='260' y2='50' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='60' cy='150' r='4' fill='red'/><text x='50' y='165' font-size='12'>A</text><circle cx='260' cy='50' r='4' fill='blue'/><text x='265' y='45' font-size='12'>B</text></svg>
questions.explanation: Map distance \(6.8\text{ cm}\) multiplied by the scale factor (12 km per cm) gives ground distance \(6.8\times12 = 81.6\text{ km}\). The angle measured clockwise from true north is the true bearing, so the bearing is 042°. Options B and D use the wrong bearing; options C uses the wrong distance. Hence option A is correct.

questions.question_text: A basaltic lava flow adds a uniform thickness of 250 m over a 150 km² area of continental crust. Assuming Airy isostasy and that the density of the continental crust is 2.70 g cm⁻³ and the mantle density is 3.30 g cm⁻³, estimate the additional crustal root thickness that will develop beneath the loaded area.
questions.explanation: Airy isostasy: \(\Delta r = \frac{\rho_{lava}}{\rho_{mantle}-\rho_{crust}}\,t\). Take \(\rho_{lava}=2.90\text{ g cm}^{-3}\), \(\rho_{crust}=2.70\text{ g cm}^{-3}\), \(\rho_{mantle}=3.30\text{ g cm}^{-3}\) and \(t=250\text{ m}\). Denominator = 3.30‑2.70 = 0.60. Ratio = 2.90/0.60 = 4.833. Hence \(\Delta r = 4.833 \times 250 \approx 1208\text{ m} \approx 1.2\text{ km}\). Options A, C and D are inconsistent with this calculation; B matches.

questions.question_text: The population of a town was 85,000 in 2015. The annual natural increase (births minus deaths) is 1.4 % and net migration is –250 persons per year. Using the discrete growth model, what is the projected population at the end of 2020 (after 5 years)?
questions.explanation: Natural growth: \(P_{0}=85,000\), \(r=0.014\), \(n=5\).\( (1.014)^5 \approx 1.0715\).\( P_{growth}=85,000\times1.0715\approx91,078\). Net migration over 5 years = \(-250\times5 = -1,250\). Final population \(=91,078-1,250\approx89,828\), which rounds to 89,800. Option A matches; the other options ignore migration or use incorrect rounding.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A remote‑sensing satellite carries a multispectral sensor with an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.05 mrad. If the sensor is operated at an altitude of 600 km above sea level, what is the ground‑sampling distance (GSD) of a pixel, assuming the sensor points directly nadir?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='140' y='20' width='40' height='20' fill='gray'/><line x1='160' y1='40' x2='160' y2='150' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='158' y1='150' x2='162' y2='150' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><text x='165' y='155' font-size='12'>IFOV</text></svg>
questions.explanation: Convert altitude to metres: \(H = 600\text{ km}=600,000\text{ m}\). Convert IFOV to radians: \(0.05\text{ mrad}=0.05\times10^{-3}=5\times10^{-5}\text{ rad}\).\(\text{GSD}=600,000\times5\times10^{-5}=30\text{ m}\). Option B is correct; the other distances result from using incorrect unit conversions.

questions.question_text: According to the Multiple Nuclei Model, which of the following urban features is most likely to develop around a secondary nucleus rather than the central business district (CBD)?

options.text: University campus and associated research facilities

questions.explanation: The Multiple Nuclei Model proposes that cities develop several specialized centres (nuclei). A university campus, together with research and related services, typically forms a secondary nucleus because it clusters knowledge‑based activities away from the CBD. The primary financial district, main railway station, and historic centre are usually anchored to the original CBD, making options A, C, and D less compatible with the model.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, the map scale is 1 cm : 5 km. The measured distance between points P and Q on the map is 6.8 cm and the bearing from P to Q is given as N 35° E. What is the true ground distance between the two settlements and the azimuth (degrees clockwise from true north) of the line PQ?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><text x='50' y='160' font-size='12'>P</text><text x='250' y='40' font-size='12'>Q</text></svg>
questions.explanation: The map scale states that 1 cm on the map equals 5 km on the ground.  \[\text{Ground distance}=6.8\text{ cm}\times5\frac{\text{km}}{\text{cm}}=34\text{ km}.\]  The bearing N 35° E means 35° east of due north, which is the same as an azimuth of 35° measured clockwise from true north.  Option A matches both calculations.  Options B and D use the wrong azimuth (145° corresponds to S 35° E), while C gives an incorrect distance (it would be the result of a 6.08 cm measurement).

questions.question_text: Which volcanic landform is most characteristic of a continental rift zone, and what is the dominant lava type erupted there?
questions.explanation: Continental rift zones are extensional environments where the lithosphere thins and mantle material rises. Decompression melting produces low‑silica, low‑viscosity basaltic magma (\(SiO_{2}<52\%\)). This magma tends to flood the surface forming broad, gently sloping shield volcanoes. \(\rightarrow\) Option B correctly pairs shield volcanoes with basaltic lava.  Stratovolcanoes (A) are typical of convergent margins, cinder cones (C) erupt more mafic to intermediate lavas, and calderas (D) are collapse features associated with large explosive eruptions.

questions.question_text: A location records the following average monthly data (°C / mm): Jan 22/80, Feb 24/70, Mar 27/55, Apr 30/45, May 33/30, Jun 35/15, Jul 34/10, Aug 32/20, Sep 29/40, Oct 26/70, Nov 23/85, Dec 20/90. Using the Köppen climate classification system, which major climate type best fits this data?
questions.explanation: The Köppen system places a climate in group C (temperate) when the coldest month is above 0 °C but below 18 °C and at least one month exceeds 10 °C.  This location’s coldest month (Jan 22 °C) is well above 0 °C, and many months are above 10 °C, meeting the C‑group requirement.  Precipitation is relatively evenly distributed with a slight summer minimum, and the warmest month exceeds 22 °C (July 34 °C).  Hence the subtype is ‘a’ (hot summer) → Cfa, humid subtropical.  The other options are inconsistent: Af requires all months > 60 mm (Jan is 80 mm but the pattern is not fully tropical), BWh needs aridity, Dfb requires colder winters, and ET needs mean temperature < 10 °C in the warmest month.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 150,000 in 2015. The net annual growth rate was 1.8 % for the first three years (2015‑2018) and then fell to 0.9 % per year for the next two years (2018‑2020). Assuming compound growth, what was the estimated population at the end of 2020?
questions.explanation:
First three years (1.8 %): \[P_{3}=150{,}000	imes(1.018)^{3}\]
Calculating: \((1.018)^{3}\approx1.05498\) → \(P_{3}\approx150{,}000	imes1.05498=158{,}247\).
Next two years (0.9 %): \[P_{5}=158{,}247	imes(1.009)^{2}\]
\((1.009)^{2}=1.018081\) → \(P_{5}\approx158{,}247	imes1.018081\approx161{,}089\).
Rounded to the nearest hundred, the population is about 161,100.  Option B matches this result.  Options A under‑estimates by using only the first growth period, C and D apply higher rates than given.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In remote sensing, which sensor characteristic most directly controls the ability to differentiate two land‑cover types that have similar spectral signatures but distinct surface textures?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='80' y='80' width='160' height='40' fill='none' stroke='blue' stroke-width='2'/><text x='160' y='110' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Footprint</text></svg>
questions.explanation: When two surfaces reflect nearly the same wavelengths, their spectra are indistinguishable.  The ability to separate them then depends on how finely the sensor samples the ground – i.e., its spatial (ground‑sampling) resolution.  High spatial resolution captures texture and pattern differences that spectroscopy alone cannot.  Spectral resolution (A) deals with wavelength discrimination, radiometric resolution (B) with the number of intensity levels, and temporal resolution (D) with revisit frequency.  Therefore, spatial resolution is the key factor, making option C correct.

questions.question_text: On a topographic map the scale is 1 cm : 0.5 km. Using a ruler, the distance between Village X and Village Y is measured as 9.2 cm. What is the actual straight‑line distance between the villages in kilometres?
questions.explanation: The map scale tells us that 1 cm on the map represents 0.5 km on the ground. Multiply the measured map distance by this factor: \(9.2\text{ cm}\times0.5\text{ km/cm}=4.6\text{ km}.\) Therefore option A is correct. Options B, C and D result from using incorrect conversion factors (e.g., 0.45 km/cm, 0.55 km/cm, or rounding errors).

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements correctly describes the primary mechanism that produces basaltic lava at a divergent plate boundary?

options.text: Partial melting of the upper mantle due to decompression as the lithosphere is pulled apart.
Melting of subducted oceanic crust releasing water‑rich, silica‑rich magma.
Crustal assimilation of continental rocks increasing silica content.
Fractional crystallisation of a magma column within a continental hotspot.

questions.explanation: At divergent (spreading) boundaries the lithosphere thins, reducing the pressure on underlying mantle material. This decompression melting generates low‑viscosity, silica‑poor (basaltic) magma. Option A states this mechanism and is therefore correct. Option B describes convergent zones where water‑induced melting of subducted slabs produces more felsic magmas. Option C involves continental crust processes not typical of mid‑ocean ridges. Option D refers to hotspots, a different setting.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 120,000 in 2015. The average annual natural increase (births − deaths) is 1.3 % and net migration adds 0.4 % each year. Assuming the rates remain constant, what will be the estimated population at the end of 2020 (i.e., after 5 years)?
questions.explanation:
First combine the two growth components: \(r = 0.013 + 0.004 = 0.017\) (1.7 % per year). Using the exponential growth formula \(P = 120{,}000\,(1.017)^5\):
\[ (1.017)^2 = 1.034289 \]
\[ (1.017)^3 \approx 1.051872 \]
\[ (1.017)^4 \approx 1.069755 \]
\[ (1.017)^5 \approx 1.087941 \]
Thus \(P \approx 120{,}000 \times 1.087941 \approx 130{,}553\), which rounds to 130,600. Option A matches this result. Options B, C and D arise from using incorrect growth rates or arithmetic errors.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, which zone typically corresponds to low‑density residential suburbs in the classic concentric‑zone model of urban structure?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='80' fill='none' stroke='black'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='60' fill='none' stroke='black'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='40' fill='none' stroke='black'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='20' fill='none' stroke='black'/><text x='160' y='30' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>CBD</text><text x='160' y='55' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Zone 2</text><text x='160' y='85' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Zone 3</text><text x='160' y='115' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Zone 4</text><text x='160' y='145' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Suburban zone</text></svg>

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: Which of the following GIS data capture methods provides continuous spatial coverage without the need for ground‑based surveys?
questions.explanation: Satellite remote sensing instruments acquire data over large swathes of the Earth's surface from orbit, delivering continuous spatial coverage independent of any on‑the‑ground measurements. Aerial photography (A) also provides imagery but still requires aircraft flights over the area of interest and does not guarantee global continuity. GPS field sampling (B) and digitizing paper maps (D) are both ground‑based, discrete data collection techniques. Therefore, option C is the only method that meets the stated condition.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a topographic map uses a scale of 1 cm : 2.5 km. The measured distance between settlements A and B on the map is 9.6 cm, and the line is drawn at an angle of 62° measured clockwise from the map’s north grid. The map’s north grid is known to be rotated 15° clockwise from true geographic north. What is the true ground bearing from A to B, and what is the actual ground distance between the two settlements?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><text x='150' y='20' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>North</text><line x1='150' y1='30' x2='150' y2='10' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
questions.explanation: First compute the ground distance: \(D = 9.6\,\text{cm} \times 2.5\,\text{km/cm} = 24\,\text{km}.\) The map north is 15° clockwise of true north, so a bearing measured clockwise from map north must be increased by 15° to obtain the true bearing: \(\theta_{\text{true}} = 62^{\circ}+15^{\circ}=77^{\circ}.\) Hence the correct combination is a bearing of 77° and a distance of 24 km, which is option C. Options A and B use incorrect bearings; option D gives a correct bearing but an impossible distance (the scale conversion already yields 24 km).

questions.question_text: Which volcanic landform is most commonly associated with the eruption of highly viscous, silica‑rich magma?
questions.explanation: Silica‑rich (rhyolitic) magma has a very high viscosity, which prevents it from flowing far from the vent. Instead, it piles up over the vent, forming a steep, rounded mound known as a lava dome. Shield volcanoes result from low‑viscosity basaltic lava, cinder cones from moderately viscous scoria, and lava plateaus from extensive low‑viscosity outpourings. Therefore the correct answer is D, lava dome.

questions.question_text: At latitude 45° N on the day of the summer solstice (solar declination \(\delta = +23.44^{\circ}\)), estimate the length of the day using the formula \[ H = \frac{2}{15}\,\omega \] where \(\omega = \arccos(-\tan\phi\,\tan\delta)\) is expressed in degrees and \(H\) is the day length in hours. Choose the closest value.
questions.explanation: First compute \(\omega\): \(\tan\phi = \tan45^{\circ}=1\), \(\tan\delta = \tan23.44^{\circ}\approx0.433\). Hence \(-\tan\phi\tan\delta = -0.433\). \(\arccos(-0.433)\) ≈ 115.7°. Then \(H = \frac{2}{15}\times115.7 \approx 15.43\) hours, which rounds to 15.3 h. Option C is the closest. The other options correspond to significantly shorter or longer day lengths and are therefore incorrect.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 1,200,000 in the year 2020. The annual natural increase is 1.8 % and net migration is –0.4 % each year. Assuming the rates remain constant, what is the projected population at the beginning of 2025 (after 5 full years)?
questions.explanation: Combine the two rates: \(r = 1.8\% - 0.4\% = 1.4\% = 0.014\). Using the compound growth formula \(P = 1,200,000\,(1+0.014)^{5}\). \((1.014)^{5}\) ≈ 1.072. Therefore \(P \approx 1,200,000 \times 1.072 = 1,286,400\), which rounds to 1,288,000. Option A matches this calculation. The other options result from using slightly higher growth rates or rounding errors.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: Which of the following data‑capture methods provides continuous spatial coverage of the Earth's surface without the need for ground‑based sensors?
questions.explanation: Satellite remote sensing instruments orbit the Earth and continuously acquire data over large swaths, thereby offering uninterrupted spatial coverage. Aerial photography, LiDAR from aircraft, and GPS surveying all depend on platforms that must be positioned over the area of interest and therefore do not give truly continuous global coverage. Hence the correct answer is B.

questions.question_text: On a topographic map the scale is 1 cm : 2 km. The measured distance between settlements C and D on the map is 3.5 cm. The bearing from C to D is given as N30°E. What is the true ground distance and the bearing?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Convert map distance to ground distance using the scale.
   Ground distance = 3.5 cm × 2 km/cm = 7 km.
Step 2: The bearing is read directly from the map as N30°E, which means 30° east of due north.
Therefore the correct combination is 7 km and N30°E, which corresponds to option A. The other options either use an incorrect conversion factor (5 km = 2.5 cm) or change the bearing, which is not supported by the given information.

questions.question_text: Which volcanic landform is most characteristic of a continental rift zone and is primarily built from low‑viscosity basaltic lava?
questions.explanation: Continental rift zones are sites of extensional tectonics where the mantle upwells and produces basaltic magma. Because this magma is low in silica, it has low viscosity and flows easily, forming broad, gently sloping edifices known as shield volcanoes. Stratovolcanoes and lava domes are associated with more viscous, silica‑rich magmas, while cinder cones are smaller steep‑sided cones formed from eruptive fragments. Hence, the correct answer is B.

questions.question_text: At latitude 35° N on the day of the winter solstice (solar declination \(\delta = -23.44^{\circ}\)), what is the solar altitude at solar noon?
questions.explanation:
Solar altitude at noon is given by \(h = 90^{\circ} - |\phi - \delta|\), where \(\phi\) is latitude and \(\delta\) is solar declination.

Calculate the absolute difference: \(|35^{\circ} - (-23.44^{\circ})| = |58.44^{\circ}| = 58.44^{\circ}\).

Then \(h = 90^{\circ} - 58.44^{\circ} = 31.56^{\circ}\). Rounded to one decimal place, this is 31.6°, which matches option A. Options B, C and D arise from incorrect sign handling or omission of the absolute value.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 120,000 in 2018. The natural increase rate is 1.2 % per year and net migration is –0.3 % per year. Assuming the rates remain constant, what is the projected population in 2022 (four years later)?
questions.explanation:
Net annual growth rate \(r = 1.2\% - 0.3\% = 0.9\% = 0.009\).
Using the compound growth formula: \(P_{4} = 120{,}000	imes(1+0.009)^{4}\).
\(1.009^{4} \approx 1.0365\).
\(120{,}000 	imes 1.0365 \approx 124{,}380\). Rounded to the nearest hundred gives 124,400, which is option A. Option B assumes a higher growth rate, C ignores growth, and D over‑estimates the compounded effect.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor captures a rectangular ground swath that is 120 km wide. The detector array consists of 30 equally spaced pixel columns across the swath. What is the ground resolution (pixel size) in kilometres?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='10' y='50' width='300' height='100' fill='none' stroke='black'/><text x='20' y='120' font-size='12'>120 km</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
Ground resolution per pixel = total swath width ÷ number of pixel columns.

\(	ext{Resolution} = 120\ 	ext{km} \div 30 = 4\ 	ext{km per pixel}\).
Thus option C is correct. Options A, B and D result from incorrect division (e.g., using 60 km, 90 km or 150 km as the swath width).

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a road is drawn from point A to point B with a bearing of 045°. The map scale is 1 cm : 3 km. The measured length AB on the map is 7.5 cm. What is the true ground distance between A and B?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="50" y1="150" x2="250" y2="50" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="250,50 245,60 255,60" fill="black"/></svg>
questions.explanation: The map scale tells us that 1 cm on the map corresponds to 3 km on the ground. Multiply the measured map distance by the scale factor: 7.5 cm × 3 km / cm = 22.5 km. Therefore option B is correct. Option A would result from a scale of 2 km / cm, C from 4 km / cm, and D from 6 km / cm, none of which match the given scale.

questions.question_text: Which tectonic setting is most likely to produce andesitic magma associated with explosive eruptions?
questions.explanation: Andesitic magma forms when oceanic crust melts partially and then interacts with continental crust in a subduction setting. The addition of volatiles from the subducting slab lowers the melting point and increases silica content, yielding viscous, gas‑rich magma that erupts explosively. Mid‑ocean ridges produce basaltic magma; rift zones also generate mainly basaltic lava; hotspots typically produce basaltic to rhyolitic lava but the classic source of andesite is a volcanic arc above a subduction zone. Hence C is correct.

questions.question_text: According to the classic concentric‑zone model of urban structure, which zone typically contains low‑density residential suburbs?
questions.explanation: The concentric‑zone (Burgess) model places the CBD at the centre, surrounded by a zone of transition (mixed land uses), then a zone of independent workers (low‑rise housing), and finally the outermost commuter zone where low‑density, single‑family homes dominate. Therefore the commuter zone (option D) is the correct answer. Options A‑C describe inner zones with higher density or mixed uses.

questions.question_text: A city had a population of 500,000 in 2010. It experiences an annual natural increase of 2.1 % and net migration of –0.4 % per year. Assuming the rates remain constant, what will be the population in 2020 (rounded to the nearest thousand)?
questions.explanation:
First combine the rates: net growth = 2.1 % – 0.4 % = 1.7 % = 0.017 per year. Use the compound growth formula \(P = P_{0}(1+r)^{t}\):
\[P = 500{,}000\times(1+0.017)^{10}\]
Calculate \((1.017)^{10}\). Using a calculator, \((1.017)^{10}\approx 1.184\).
\[P \approx 500{,}000 \times 1.184 = 592{,}000\]
Rounded to the nearest thousand gives 592,000, which corresponds to option B. Options A and D are lower and higher respectively, and option C is not supported by the calculation.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.03 mrad and is orbiting at an altitude of 750 km. What is the linear dimension of the ground pixel recorded by the sensor (nadir view)?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="60" y="70" width="200" height="80" fill="none" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><text x="160" y="115" font-size="14" text-anchor="middle" fill="black">Sensor Footprint</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
The ground dimension of a pixel is given by \(L = H \times \theta\), where \(H\) is the sensor altitude and \(\theta\) is the IFOV in radians.
Convert the altitude: 750 km = 750,000 m. The IFOV is 0.03 mrad = 0.03 × 10⁻³ rad = 0.00003 rad.
\[L = 750{,}000\,\text{m} \times 0.00003 = 22.5\,\text{m}\]
Thus the ground pixel size is 22.5 m, corresponding to option A. The other options result from using larger IFOV values or a mistaken conversion factor.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, two points P and Q are plotted on a topographic map. The map scale is 1 cm : 2 km. The distance between P and Q measured on the map is 4.5 cm, and the bearing of the line from P to Q is 067°. What is the true bearing and the ground distance between P and Q?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='250,50 245,55 255,55' fill='black'/><text x='150' y='100' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle'>P</text><text x='260' y='40' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle'>Q</text></svg>
questions.explanation: The map scale 1 cm : 2 km means every centimetre on the map represents 2 km on the ground. Measured distance = 4.5 cm, so ground distance = 4.5 cm × 2 km / cm = 9 km. The bearing shown on the map is the true bearing because north on the map aligns with true north (no declination mentioned). Therefore the true bearing is 067° and the distance is 9 km. Options B, C, and D are eliminated by either the wrong distance or the wrong bearing.

questions.question_text: Using the Airy isostasy model, calculate the additional crustal root thickness that supports a mountain whose summit stands 2 km above sea level. Assume average continental crust density = 2.7 g cm⁻³, mantle density = 3.3 g cm⁻³, and a compensation depth of 100 km.
questions.explanation: Airy isostasy gives the compensating root thickness as \(\frac{\rho_{crust}}{\rho_{mantle}-\rho_{crust}}\times h\), where \(h\) is the surface elevation. Substituting the numbers: \(\frac{2.7}{3.3-2.7}\times 2 = \frac{2.7}{0.6}\times 2 = 4.5\times 2 = 9\) km. Hence the extra root beneath the mountain is 9 km. Options B, C, and D result from using wrong density contrasts or neglecting the factor of 2.

questions.question_text: At latitude 30° N on the day of the summer solstice (solar declination \(\delta = +23.44^{\circ}\)), what is the solar elevation angle at local solar noon?
questions.explanation:
Solar elevation at noon is \(\alpha = 90^{\circ} - |\phi - \delta|\). Here \(\phi = 30^{\circ}\) and \(\delta = 23.44^{\circ}\).

\(\alpha = 90^{\circ} - |30^{\circ} - 23.44^{\circ}| = 90^{\circ} - 6.56^{\circ} = 83.44^{\circ}\).
Rounded to one decimal place, this is 83.4°. Options B–D correspond to using the wrong sign or omitting the absolute‑value step.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 250,000 in 2019. The natural increase rate is 1.2 % per year and the net migration rate is –0.3 % per year. Assuming the rates remain constant, what is the projected population at the end of 2022 (three years later)?
questions.explanation:
Combined annual growth rate \(r = 0.012 - 0.003 = 0.009\) (0.9 %). Using \(P = P_{0}(1+r)^{t}\):

\(P = 250{,}000	imes(1.009)^{3}\).
\(1.009^{2}=1.018081\); \(1.018081	imes1.009 = 1.0272437\).
Thus \(P \approx 250{,}000	imes1.02724 = 256{,}811\). Rounded to the nearest hundred gives 256,800. Options B and C over‑estimate by using a larger rate; D ignores growth.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A satellite sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.04 milliradians and orbits at an altitude of 700 km. What is the ground sampling distance (GSD) at the nadir point?
questions.explanation: Convert altitude to metres: 700 km = 700,000 m. Convert IFOV to radians: 0.04 milliradians = 0.04 × 10⁻³ rad = 4.0 × 10⁻⁵ rad.\nGSD = 700,000 m × 4.0 × 10⁻⁵ rad = 28 m. Option A matches this calculation. Options B, C, and D arise from using half, 1.25×, or 2× the correct IFOV respectively.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, the map scale is 1 cm : 2 km. Points P and Q lie on the 120 m and 200 m contour lines respectively. The straight‑line distance between P and Q on the map measures 3.0 cm. Determine (a) the ground distance between the points in kilometres, and (b) the average slope (%) between them.
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='60' y1='100' x2='260' y2='100' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='60' cy='100' r='4' fill='red'/><text x='55' y='90' font-size='12'>P</text><circle cx='260' cy='100' r='4' fill='blue'/><text x='255' y='90' font-size='12'>Q</text></svg>
questions.explanation: Ground distance = map distance × scale = 3.0 cm × 2 km/cm = 6 km. Elevation difference \(\Delta h = 200\,\text{m} - 120\,\text{m} = 80\,\text{m}\). Horizontal distance in metres = 6 km = 6000 m. \(\text{Slope}\;\% = (80/6000)\times100 = 1.33\% \approx 1.3\%\). Option A matches both values. Options B, C and D either use an incorrect distance or mis‑calculate the slope.

questions.question_text: According to the Airy isostasy model, the thickness of the continental crustal root \(R\) that supports a mountain of elevation \(h\) is given by \(R = \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_m - \rho_c}\,h\), where \(\rho_m = 3300\ \text{kg\,m}^{-3}\) is mantle density and \(\rho_c = 2700\ \text{kg\,m}^{-3}\) is crust density. If a mountain has an elevation of 4,500 m, what is the approximate root thickness in kilometres?
questions.explanation: First compute the density ratio: \(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho_m-\rho_c}=\frac{3300}{3300-2700}=\frac{3300}{600}=5.5\). Then \(R = 5.5 \times 4500\,\text{m}=24{,}750\,\text{m}=24.8\,\text{km}\). Option B is the only one that matches this value. The other options arise from using incorrect ratios or neglecting the subtraction in the denominator.

questions.question_text: A mid‑size city had a population of 820,000 in 2018. The average annual natural increase (births minus deaths) is 1.4 % and net migration is –0.5 % per year. Assuming these rates remain constant, what will be the projected population at the end of 2023 (after five years)?
