\((1+0.027)^5 = 1.027^5 \approx 1.141 \) (calculated step‑wise: 1.027²≈1.0547, ^4≈1.112, ×1.027≈1.141).
\(P = 8 	imes 1.141 \approx 9.128\) million, i.e., about 9.1 million.
Option A is the only one close to this result; the others are far off.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.0002 rad and is orbiting at an altitude of 600 km. What is the ground sample distance (GSD) for a nadir view?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="160" cy="100" r="5" fill="red"/><line x1="160" y1="100" x2="160" y2="0" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><text x="165" y="10" font-size="12">IFOV</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
For a nadir view, ground sample distance is given by \(	ext{GSD}=H	imes	ext{IFOV}\).
Altitude \(H = 600\) km = 600,000 m.
IFOV = 0.0002 rad.
\(	ext{GSD}=600,000	imes0.0002 = 120\) m.
Option A matches this calculation; the other values arise from incorrect multiplication or unit conversion errors.

questions.question_text: A topographic map shows Town A and Town B separated by a measured distance of 3.5 cm. The map scale is 1 cm : 250 m. On the map the line from A to B makes an angle of 60° measured clockwise from the north direction. What are the true ground distance (in kilometres) and the true bearing from Town A to Town B?
questions.explanation: The ground distance is obtained by multiplying the map distance by the scale factor: \(D = 3.5\text{ cm}\times250\text{ m/cm}=875\text{ m}=0.875\text{ km}.\) The bearing on the map is measured clockwise from north, so the true bearing is the same as the map bearing, 60°. Option A gives both the correct distance and bearing; all other options contain either an incorrect distance or an incorrect bearing.

questions.question_text: Continental crust is composed of 70 % granite (density 2.67 g cm⁻³) and 30 % basalt (density 2.92 g cm⁻³). Oceanic crust consists of 100 % basalt. Calculate the average density of each crust type and state which type is thicker under isostatic equilibrium.

options.text: Continental 2.75 g cm⁻³, Oceanic 2.92 g cm⁻³; continental crust is thicker.
Continental 2.80 g cm⁻³, Oceanic 2.85 g cm⁻³; oceanic crust is thicker.
Continental 2.70 g cm⁻³, Oceanic 2.80 g cm⁻³; both are equally thick.
Continental 2.60 g cm⁻³, Oceanic 2.92 g cm⁻³; continental crust is thinner.

questions.explanation: Average density of continental crust: \(\rho_{c}=0.70\times2.67+0.30\times2.92=2.745\text{ g\,cm}^{-3}\) (≈2.75). Oceanic crust is pure basalt, so \(\rho_{o}=2.92\text{ g\,cm}^{-3}.\) Because continental crust is less dense, isostatic balance requires it to have a greater thickness to support the same load. Hence the continental crust is thicker. Option A correctly states both densities and the thickness relationship; the other options contain incorrect values or conclusions.

questions.question_text: A region had a population of 4.2 million at the beginning of 2020. During 2020 the crude birth rate was 18 per 1 000, the crude death rate was 7 per 1 000 and net migration was –3 per 1 000. Assuming these rates remained constant throughout the year, what was the population at the end of 2020 (rounded to the nearest thousand)?
questions.explanation:
Net growth per 1 000: birth – death + migration = 18 – 7 – 3 = 8 per 1 000, i.e. a growth rate of 0.008.

\(P_{t}=4.2	imes10^{6}	imes(1+0.008)=4.2	imes10^{6}	imes1.008=4,233,600\).
Rounded to the nearest thousand gives 4,234,000. Option A matches this result; the other options are off by tens of thousands.

questions.question_text: In a rapidly growing metropolis a wide river runs north‑south through the city centre and a major highway parallels the river on its eastern bank. High‑income residential neighborhoods are found mainly on the western bank of the river, while industrial zones line the eastern bank adjacent to the highway. Which urban spatial model best explains this pattern of land‑use segregation?
questions.explanation: The sector model (Hoyt, 1939) proposes that cities develop in sectors radiating outward from the central business district along major transportation routes. In the described city, the river and the highway act as axes that channel development. High‑income areas occupy a sector on the western side of the river, away from the industrial sector that follows the highway on the east. This pattern fits the sector model, whereas the concentric zone model assumes uniform rings, the multiple‑nuclei model predicts several independent centres, and central‑place theory addresses settlement hierarchy rather than intra‑urban land‑use.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor is positioned 700 km above the Earth's surface and has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.0002 rad. The sensor points directly toward the nadir. What is the linear size of the ground footprint (assuming a rectangular footprint) captured by the sensor on the Earth's surface? Choose the closest value.
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='160' cy='150' r='80' fill='#cce5ff'/><rect x='150' y='20' width='20' height='30' fill='#999'/><line x1='160' y1='50' x2='160' y2='150' stroke='#000' stroke-width='2'/><text x='165' y='45' font-size='12'>IFOV</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
Ground footprint size is given by \(L = h 	imes 	heta\) where \(h\) is the sensor altitude and \(	heta\) is the IFOV in radians. \(h = 700\,	ext{km}=700,000\,	ext{m}\) and \(	heta = 0.0002\,	ext{rad}\).

\(L = 700,000\,	ext{m} 	imes 0.0002 = 140\,	ext{m}\).
Thus the footprint is about 140 m across. Option A is the closest value; the other options are either half, double, or an order of magnitude larger.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, two towns X and Y are located 5.2 cm apart on a topographic map whose scale is 1 cm : 150 m. The bearing from X to Y is measured as 68°. What is the true distance between the towns in kilometres, and what is the azimuth (bearing) of the line from Y to X?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='60' y1='150' x2='260' y2='50' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='60' cy='150' r='5' fill='red'/><text x='45' y='165' font-size='12'>X</text><circle cx='260' cy='50' r='5' fill='blue'/><text x='245' y='45' font-size='12'>Y</text></svg>
questions.explanation: Map distance = 5.2 cm. Scale = 1 cm : 150 m, so 1 cm on the map = 150 m in reality.\n\[ d_{\text{true}} = 5.2\ \text{cm} \times 150\ \text{m/cm} = 780\ \text{m} = 0.78\ \text{km}. \]\nThe bearing from X to Y is 68°. The reverse bearing (azimuth from Y to X) is obtained by adding 180°: 68° + 180° = 248°. Therefore the correct pair is 0.78 km and 248°, which is option A. Options B and C keep the forward bearing, while D uses an incorrect reverse bearing.

questions.question_text: Continental rift zones often generate basaltic magma even though mantle temperatures are lower than those beneath oceanic hot‑spots. Which of the following explanations best accounts for this observation?

options.text: Lower crustal thinning reduces pressure, allowing mantle peridotite to partially melt at relatively low temperatures.
Water released from subducting slabs raises the solidus of the mantle, producing basaltic melts.
Higher concentrations of radioactive elements in the crust provide additional heat for basalt generation.
Accumulation of felsic crustal material mixes with mantle melt, lowering its viscosity.

questions.explanation: In a continental rift the lithosphere is stretched and the crust thins. This thinning leads to a reduction in lithostatic pressure on the underlying mantle. Decompression melting of peridotite can therefore occur at temperatures that would not melt mantle under higher pressure. Option A correctly describes this process. Option B refers to subduction zones, not rifts. Option C concerns crustal heat production, which is insufficient to melt mantle peridotite. Option D describes magma differentiation rather than the primary generation of basaltic magma.

questions.question_text: A country had a population of 12 million in 2010. Over the next ten years it experienced an average annual natural increase rate of 1.4 % and a net migration rate of –0.3 % per year. Assuming the rates remain constant and compound annually, what is the projected population in 2020 (rounded to one decimal place in millions)?
questions.explanation:
Net annual growth rate = natural increase + net migration = 1.4 % – 0.3 % = 1.1 % = 0.011 as a decimal.
\[ P_{2020}=12\,	ext{million}	imes(1+0.011)^{10}. \]
Compute the compound factor: (1.011)^10 ≈ e^{10\ln(1.011)}. 
\(\ln(1.011)≈0.01094\), so 10·0.01094 = 0.1094 and e^{0.1094} ≈ 1.1155.
\[ P_{2020}≈12	imes1.1155≈13.386	ext{ million}. \]
Rounded to one decimal place gives 13.4 million, which corresponds to option A. Options B and C underestimate the effect of the positive net growth, while D over‑estimates it.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: Which spectral index is most widely used for estimating vegetation vigor when only near‑infrared (NIR) and red‑band reflectance values are available?
questions.explanation: NDVI is defined as \( (\rho_{NIR}-\rho_{red})/(\rho_{NIR}+\rho_{red}) \) and requires only NIR and red reflectance, making it the standard choice for quick vegetation health assessment. SAVI adds a soil‑brightness correction factor, EVI includes the blue band, and GCI uses the green band; therefore they are not the most suitable when only NIR and red are available.

questions.question_text: Which urban model best explains a polycentric metropolitan region in which several sub‑centers have developed around a dominant central business district, each serving distinct economic functions?
questions.explanation: The Multiple Nuclei Model (Harris and Ullman) posits that large cities develop several independent centres (nuclei) such as industrial parks, university districts, and retail hubs, all linked to a central business district. This explains the polycentric layout described. The Concentric Zone Model assumes a single centre with surrounding rings, the Sector Model suggests wedges extending outward, and the Peripheral Model focuses on suburban growth at the edge, none of which capture multiple distinct sub‑centers.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, two points A and B are plotted on a topographic map. The map scale is 1 cm : 500 m. Point A is located at (30 cm, 40 cm) and point B at (70 cm, 80 cm). What is the bearing from point A to point B measured clockwise from true north?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="50" y1="150" x2="250" y2="50" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/></svg>
questions.explanation: First convert the map coordinates to real‑world distances using the scale 1 cm = 500 m. The east‑west (ΔE) difference is (70 cm‑30 cm) × 500 m / cm = 20 000 m and the north‑south (ΔN) difference is (80 cm‑40 cm) × 500 m / cm = 20 000 m. The bearing θ is given by \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(\Delta E/\Delta N) = \tan^{-1}(1) = 45°\). Option B matches this result. Options A, C, and D correspond to incorrect arctangent values.

questions.question_text: Continental crust has an average density of 2.7 g cm⁻³ while oceanic crust averages 3.0 g cm⁻³. Which of the following statements best explains why continental crust typically forms higher topography than oceanic crust?

options.text: Continental crust is thicker, providing greater lithostatic pressure that lifts it upward.
Continental crust has a lower density, giving it greater buoyancy according to \(F_{b}= (\rho_{m}-\rho_{c})Vg\).
Oceanic crust contains more basalt, which cools faster and contracts, lowering its elevation.
Continental crust is rich in iron‑bearing minerals that expand under pressure, raising the surface.

questions.explanation: Buoyancy of a lithospheric block is governed by the density contrast between the mantle (\(\rho_{m}\)) and the crust (\(\rho_{c}\)). The upward buoyant force \(F_{b}\) increases as \(\rho_{c}\) decreases. Because continental crust (2.7 g cm⁻³) is less dense than oceanic crust (3.0 g cm⁻³), it experiences a larger net buoyant force, causing it to sit higher on the mantle. Option B directly states this principle. Option A confuses thickness with buoyancy; thickness alone does not guarantee higher elevation. Option C describes cooling but does not explain the primary density‑driven buoyancy. Option D is incorrect because iron‑rich minerals increase density, not elevation.

questions.question_text: A region had a population of 12 million in 2020. Its vital statistics for each year are a birth rate of 12 per 1 000, a death rate of 4 per 1 000, and a net migration rate of +4 per 1 000. Assuming these rates remain constant, what is the projected population at the end of 2025?
questions.explanation:
First determine the overall annual growth rate. Natural increase = birth rate – death rate = (12‑4) per 1 000 = 8 ‰ = 0.8 %. Adding net migration (+4 ‰ = 0.4 %) gives a total growth rate \(r = 0.8\% + 0.4\% = 1.2\%\) = 0.012. However the problem states net migration of +4 per 1 000, not 0.4 %; thus total increase = 8 ‰ + 4 ‰ = 12 ‰ = 1.2 % = 0.012. The projection for \(t = 5\) years is:
\[P = 12\,\text{million}\times(1+0.012)^{5}\]
\((1.012)^{5} \approx 1.0615\) (using a calculator or binomial approximation). Multiplying: \(12\times1.0615 \approx 12.74\) million. Rounding to the nearest tenth gives 12.8 million, which matches option B. Wait – re‑evaluate: the net migration rate was +4 per 1 000, giving total growth 12 ‰ = 1.2 % = 0.012. 12 million × (1.012)^5 = 12 million × 1.0615 ≈ 12.738 million ≈ 12.8 million. Therefore the correct answer is **B**.  
*Correction*: The earlier step incorrectly used 1.6 % growth. The correct total increase is 12 ‰ (1.2 %). Hence option B is correct.
Option A underestimates, option C and D overestimate the compounded growth.

questions.question_text: Which urban model best describes a city whose land‑use pattern consists of a central business district surrounded by successive concentric rings of similar functions such as residential, industrial, and commuter zones?
questions.explanation: The Burgess Concentric Zone Model (1909) proposes a series of rings radiating from the central business district (CBD). Each ring represents a distinct land‑use zone (CBD, transition, working‑class residential, better‑residential, commuter zone). This description matches the question. The Sector Model (Hoyt) predicts wedge‑shaped sectors, not concentric rings. The Multiple Nuclei Model suggests several independent centres. The Bid‑Rent Theory explains rent gradients but does not outline a full ring pattern.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In a GIS, which data model is most appropriate for accurately representing linear features such as roads, pipelines, and rivers while preserving topological relationships?
questions.explanation: The vector data model stores geographic features as points, lines (polylines), and polygons. Polylines are used for linear features and can store topology (e.g., connectivity, adjacency). Raster grids represent continuous phenomena and are unsuitable for precise linear representation. TINs model surfaces using interconnected triangles, not linear networks. A raster mosaic is also a raster format. Therefore, option B is correct.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a line AB measured on a topographic map is 6.2 cm long and has a bearing of 070°. The map scale is 1 cm : 0.5 km. Calculate (a) the true ground distance between A and B, and (b) the eastward and northward components of this distance in kilometres.
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="50" y1="150" x2="250" y2="50" stroke="black" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="0" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L0,6 L9,3 z" fill="black"/></marker></defs></svg>

options.text: Distance = 3.1 km; East ≈ 2.9 km; North ≈ 1.1 km
Distance = 3.1 km; East ≈ 1.1 km; North ≈ 2.9 km
Distance = 6.2 km; East ≈ 5.8 km; North ≈ 2.2 km
Distance = 3.1 km; East ≈ 2.0 km; North ≈ 2.5 km

questions.explanation:
Step 1: Convert the map length to ground distance using the scale 1 cm = 0.5 km.
\[ D = 6.2\ \text{cm}\times0.5\ \frac{\text{km}}{\text{cm}} = 3.1\ \text{km} \]
Step 2: Resolve the distance into components. Bearing 070° is measured clockwise from north, so the angle east of north is 70°.
\[ D_N = D\cos 70^{\circ}=3.1\times0.3420\approx1.06\ \text{km} \]
\[ D_E = D\sin 70^{\circ}=3.1\times0.9397\approx2.91\ \text{km} \]
Step 3: Compare with the options. Option A matches the calculated values (rounded to one decimal place). Therefore, A is correct.
Elimination: B swaps the components, C uses an incorrect scale, D gives wrong component magnitudes.

questions.question_text: Which statement best explains why basaltic lavas erupted at mid‑ocean ridges contain lower silica concentrations than the andesitic lavas commonly associated with continental volcanic arcs?

options.text: Mid‑ocean ridges experience higher degrees of partial melting of mantle peridotite, producing silica‑rich melt.
Continental arcs involve low‑degree melting of the subducted slab, which preferentially extracts silica‑rich components.
Basalts derive from low‑degree partial melting of mantle peridotite, yielding melt low in silica, whereas arcs involve higher‑degree melting of crustal material that enriches silica.
Water released from the subducting slab suppresses silica enrichment in arc magmas, making them more mafic than ridge basalts.

questions.explanation: Mid‑ocean ridge basalts are generated when upwelling mantle peridotite undergoes a relatively low degree of partial melting (≈5‑10 %). This melt extracts primarily the mafic components (Fe, Mg) and is poor in silica, giving basaltic composition. In contrast, continental volcanic arcs are fed by higher‑degree melting of both the hydrated subducting slab and the overlying crust, processes that add silica‑rich minerals such as quartz and feldspar to the magma, producing andesitic compositions. Option C correctly captures this contrast. Options A and B invert the degree of melting, and D misstates the effect of water on silica content.

questions.question_text: A region had a population of 8.3 million in 2019. The crude birth rate was 18 per 1 000, the crude death rate 7 per 1 000, and the net migration rate –2 per 1 000 for that year. Assuming these rates remain constant for a single year, what is the projected population for 2020?
questions.explanation:
First compute the annual natural increase and migration using the given rates.
\[\text{Births}=\frac{18}{1000}\times8.3\,\text{million}=0.018\times8.3=0.1494\,\text{million}=149,400\]
\[\text{Deaths}=\frac{7}{1000}\times8.3\,\text{million}=0.007\times8.3=0.0581\,\text{million}=58,100\]
\[\text{Net migration}=\frac{-2}{1000}\times8.3\,\text{million}= -0.002\times8.3= -0.0166\,\text{million}= -16,600\]
Net change \(\Delta P\) = 149,400 – 58,100 – 16,600 = 74,700 persons.
Add to the base population:
\[8.3\,\text{million}+0.0747\,\text{million}=8.3747\,\text{million}\]
Rounded to three decimal places the 2020 population is 8.375 million, which corresponds to option A.

questions.question_text: A city exhibits a densely built central business district surrounded by a concentric ring of high‑density residential neighbourhoods, followed by a zone of lower‑density suburbs. Major highways radiate outward from the centre. Which urban land‑use model best describes this pattern?
questions.explanation: The described arrangement matches the classic Burgess Concentric Zone Model: (1) a central business district at the core, (2) an inner ring of high‑density residential zones (zone II), (3) a subsequent ring of lower‑density residential suburbs (zone III), and (4) transportation corridors that radiate outward. The Sector Model predicts development along wedges extending from the CBD, which is not indicated here. The Multiple Nuclei Model involves several independent centres, and the Urban Realms Model focuses on multiple sub‑urban realms linked by commuter flows. Hence, option B is correct.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A satellite sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.0003 rad and flies at an altitude of 700 km above the Earth’s surface. What is the ground sampling distance (GSD) produced by this sensor?
questions.explanation:
The ground sampling distance is obtained by multiplying the sensor altitude by the instantaneous field of view:
\[\text{GSD}=700\,\text{km}\times0.0003\,\text{rad}=0.21\,\text{km}=210\,\text{m}\]
Option A matches this calculation. Options B, C and D result from misplaced decimal placement.
Thus, the correct answer is A.

questions.question_text: A topographic map is drawn at a scale of 1:250,000. The straight‑line distance measured between Town X and Town Y on the map is 4.5 cm. What is the actual ground distance between the two towns in kilometres?
questions.explanation: The scale 1:250,000 means 1 cm on the map equals 250,000 cm on the ground. Convert to kilometres: 250,000 cm = 2.5 km. Therefore 4.5 cm × 2.5 km cm⁻¹ = 11.25 km. Option B is correct. The other choices result from incorrect conversion factors (e.g., forgetting to convert centimetres to kilometres).

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements best explains why basaltic lavas erupted at oceanic hotspots tend to have higher silica content than those erupted at mid‑ocean ridges?

options.text: Hotspots melt a larger proportion of the overlying crust, which is silica‑rich, while ridge magmas originate almost exclusively from mantle peridotite.
Mid‑ocean ridge magmas experience higher degrees of fractional crystallisation, removing silica from the melt.
Hotspot magma ascends more slowly, allowing assimilation of mantle metasomatized by silicate fluids.
The higher pressure at hotspots forces the mantle to produce more felsic melt.

questions.explanation: At oceanic hotspots (e.g., Hawaiian), ascending asthenospheric plumes partially melt the thin oceanic crust as they rise, incorporating silica‑rich basaltic material, which raises the SiO₂ content of the erupting lava. Mid‑ocean ridge lavas are generated by decompression melting of peridotitic mantle with little crustal interaction, yielding lower silica basalts. Options B, C, and D describe processes that either do not apply or would lower, not raise, silica content.

questions.question_text: A region has a carrying capacity (K) of 12 million people. Its current population (P₀) is 6 million and the intrinsic growth rate (r) is 0.04 yr⁻¹. Using the logistic growth model \[ P(t)=\frac{K}{1+\frac{K-P_{0}}{P_{0}}e^{-rt}} \], what is the projected population after 10 years?
questions.explanation: First compute the ratio \(\frac{K-P_{0}}{P_{0}} = \frac{12-6}{6}=1\). Then evaluate the exponential term: \(e^{-rt}=e^{-0.04\times10}=e^{-0.4}\approx0.6703\). The denominator becomes \(1+1\times0.6703=1.6703\). Thus \(P(10)=\frac{12}{1.6703}\approx7.19\) million, which rounds to 7.2 million. Hence option A is correct. Options B‑D arise from using a simple exponential model or incorrect arithmetic.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: For detailed land‑cover mapping of a coastal city, which remote‑sensing platform typically provides the highest spatial resolution?

options.text: Low‑Earth‑orbit (LEO) multispectral satellite such as Sentinel‑2 (10 m resolution)
High‑resolution commercial satellite (e.g., WorldView‑3, <0.5 m resolution)
Airborne LiDAR system with point spacing of 1 m

questions.explanation: High‑resolution commercial satellites like WorldView‑3 deliver sub‑meter (often 0.31 m panchromatic) imagery, which is finer than the 10 m resolution of Sentinel‑2, the coarse 0.5‑km+ resolution of geostationary weather satellites, and typical airborne LiDAR point spacing of about 1 m. Therefore option C gives the best spatial detail for land‑cover classification. Option D, while useful for elevation, does not provide the spectral detail of high‑resolution optical sensors.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a city is shown with a central business district (CBD) surrounded by successive rings of land use. Which urban model does this pattern best represent?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='30' fill='#ffcc00' stroke='#000' stroke-width='2'/><text x='150' y='105' font-size='10' fill='#000'>CBD</text><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='70' fill='none' stroke='#ff0000' stroke-width='2'/><text x='120' y='30' font-size='9' fill='#ff0000'>Zone 1</text><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='110' fill='none' stroke='#00aa00' stroke-width='2'/><text x='90' y='20' font-size='9' fill='#00aa00'>Zone 2</text><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='150' fill='none' stroke='#0000ff' stroke-width='2'/><text x='50' y='15' font-size='9' fill='#0000ff'>Zone 3</text></svg>
questions.explanation: The figure shows a central core (CBD) surrounded by a series of concentric rings, each representing a different land‑use zone. This spatial arrangement matches the classic Concentric Zone Model (Ernest Burgess). The Sector Model would display wedge‑shaped sectors radiating outward, the Multiple Nuclei Model would show several distinct centres, and the Linear City Model would depict development along a line, none of which correspond to the diagram.

questions.question_text: A topographic map has a scale of 1 cm : 2 km. On the map, two villages are 3.8 cm apart. What is the actual ground distance between the villages?
questions.explanation: The map scale states that 1 cm on the map represents 2 km on the ground. Multiply the measured map distance by the scale factor: \[ D = 3.8\,\text{cm} \times 2\,\text{km/cm} = 7.6\,\text{km}. \] Therefore the correct answer is 7.6 km. Options A, C and D are obtained by using incorrect scale factors or arithmetic errors.

questions.question_text: Basaltic lava has been observed erupting in a region underlain by thick continental crust. Which tectonic setting best explains this occurrence?
questions.explanation: Continental rift zones involve extensional forces that thin the lithosphere, allowing mantle material to rise and produce basaltic magma even beneath thick continental crust. A transform fault (A) typically produces earthquakes, not basaltic volcanism. Subduction zones (C) generate calc‑alkaline magmas rich in silica. Hotspots (D) can produce basaltic lava but are not specifically linked to thick continental crust, whereas rifting directly explains basaltic eruptions in such settings.

questions.question_text: At a latitude of 45° N, what is the solar altitude angle at local noon on the winter solstice (solar declination = ‑23.44°)?
questions.explanation: The solar altitude at noon is given by \[ h = 90^{\circ} - |\phi - \delta| \] where \(\phi\) is latitude and \(\delta\) is solar declination. Substituting \(\phi = 45^{\circ}\) and \(\delta = -23.44^{\circ}\): \[ |\phi - \delta| = |45 - (-23.44)| = 68.44^{\circ} \] \[ h = 90^{\circ} - 68.44^{\circ} = 21.56^{\circ} \] Rounded to one decimal place, the altitude is 21.6°, matching option A. The other choices arise from sign errors or omission of the absolute‑value step.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 5.0 million in 2015 and has experienced an average annual growth rate of 1.8 % (compounded). What was its population at the end of 2022?
questions.explanation: Use the compound growth formula \[ P = P_{0}(1+r)^{t} \] where \(P_{0}=5.0\) million, \(r=0.018\) and \(t = 2022-2015 = 7\) years. \[ (1+0.018)^{7} \approx 1.132 \] Multiplying: \[ P \approx 5.0 \times 1.132 = 5.66 \text{ million}. \] Hence the population is approximately 5.66 million, option B. Options A and C result from using a lower growth factor, while D assumes a much higher rate.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor orbits 500 km above the Earth's surface and has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.0001 rad. What is the ground‑sample distance (GSD) captured by the sensor?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='160' cy='150' r='40' stroke='black' fill='none'/><line x1='160' y1='0' x2='160' y2='110' stroke='red' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
questions.explanation: The ground‑sample distance is calculated by multiplying the sensor altitude \(H\) by the IFOV angle \(\theta\): \[ \text{GSD} = H \times \theta \] Convert altitude to meters: \(H = 500\,\text{km} = 500{,}000\,\text{m}\). Then \[ \text{GSD} = 500{,}000\,\text{m} \times 0.0001 = 50\,\text{m}. \] Therefore the sensor records a ground resolution of 50 m, option B. Option A uses a missing factor of 10, option C multiplies by 0.001, and option D treats the IFOV as 0.01 rad.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, points P and Q are plotted on a topographic map. The distance between them measured on the map is 4.2 cm. The map scale is 1 cm : 250 m. The bearing of line PQ measured clockwise from north is 135°. What are the true ground distance between P and Q and its east‑ and north‑ward components (in metres)?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='250,50 245,60 255,60' fill='black'/><text x='150' y='100' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle'>135°</text></svg>

options.text: Ground distance = 1050 m; East component ≈ 742 m, North component ≈ ‑742 m
Ground distance = 1050 m; East component ≈ 525 m, North component ≈ ‑525 m
Ground distance = 1060 m; East component ≈ 750 m, North component ≈ ‑750 m
Ground distance = 950 m; East component ≈ 670 m, North component ≈ ‑670 m

questions.explanation: First compute the ground distance: 4.2 cm × 250 m / cm = 1050 m. For a bearing of 135°, the direction is exactly SE, so the east and south components are equal. Using trigonometry, East = D·sin 135° = 1050·(√2/2) ≈ 1050·0.7071 ≈ 742 m. North component = D·cos 135° = 1050·(‑√2/2) ≈ ‑742 m (i.e., 742 m south). Option A matches these values; the other options either use an incorrect distance or incorrect component magnitudes.

questions.question_text: Which tectonic setting is most likely to produce high‑silica (rhyolitic) volcanic eruptions?
questions.explanation: Rhyolitic magmas are rich in silica and form when mantle‑derived basaltic magma evolves through extensive fractional crystallisation or partial melting of continental crust. This process is favoured in a subduction‑zone volcanic arc, where water‑rich fluids induce melting of the over‑riding continental lithosphere, producing high‑silica magmas. Mid‑ocean ridges and oceanic hotspots generate mainly basaltic lava, while continental rifts can produce a range of compositions but are less typical for pure rhyolite.

questions.question_text: At a latitude of 30° N, what is the solar altitude angle at local noon on the summer solstice (solar declination = +23.5°)?
questions.explanation: Solar altitude at noon is given by \( \alpha = 90^{\circ} - |\phi - \delta| \), where \( \phi \) is latitude and \( \delta \) is solar declination. Substituting \( \phi = 30^{\circ} \) and \( \delta = +23.5^{\circ} \): \( \alpha = 90^{\circ} - |30^{\circ} - 23.5^{\circ}| = 90^{\circ} - 6.5^{\circ} = 83.5^{\circ} \). Option B is correct; the other values arise from mis‑applying the absolute value or using the wrong sign for declination.

questions.question_text: A region has a carrying capacity \(K = 50\) million people, an initial population \(P_{0}=5\) million and an intrinsic growth rate \(r = 0.08\) yr⁻¹. Using the logistic growth model \( P(t)=\frac{K}{1+\left(\frac{K-P_{0}}{P_{0}}\right)e^{-rt}} \), what is the projected population after 20 years (rounded to one decimal place)?
questions.explanation: First compute the ratio \( (K-P_{0})/P_{0} = (50-5)/5 = 9 \). Then \( e^{-rt}=e^{-0.08\times20}=e^{-1.6}\approx0.2019 \). Denominator: \(1 + 9\times0.2019 = 1 + 1.8171 = 2.8171\). Finally, \( P(20)=50/2.8171 \approx 17.75\) million, which rounds to 17.8 million. Option C matches this value. The other options result from using the exponential growth formula or mis‑calculating the exponent.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: Which remote‑sensing platform provides the most suitable combination of spatial and temporal resolution for monitoring urban land‑cover change at a five‑year interval?

options.text: MODIS – 250 m spatial resolution, daily revisit
SPOT – 20 m spatial resolution, 1‑3 day revisit

questions.explanation: For a five‑year monitoring program, temporal resolution need not be more frequent than the interval; however, a consistent historical archive is essential. Landsat 8 offers a moderate 30 m spatial resolution sufficient to distinguish urban features and has a continuous data record dating back to the 1970s, enabling reliable change detection over multi‑year periods. Sentinel‑2 provides finer spatial detail but its 5‑day revisit is unnecessary for a 5‑year schedule and its archive is shorter. MODIS lacks the spatial detail required for urban land‑cover, and SPOT’s short revisit adds no advantage for such a long interval. Therefore, Landsat 8 is the optimal choice.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a topographic map shows three closed contour lines representing a hill. The contour interval is 20 m. The innermost contour is labelled 120 m. What is the slope gradient (in degrees) between the 80 m and 120 m contours if the horizontal distance measured on the map between them is 0.5 cm and the map scale is 1 cm : 250 m? (Assume a straight slope.)
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='60' stroke='black' fill='none'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='40' stroke='black' fill='none'/><circle cx='160' cy='100' r='20' stroke='black' fill='none'/></svg>
questions.explanation:
1. Convert the map distance to ground distance: 0.5 cm × 250 m/cm = 125 m.
2. Elevation change between the 80 m and 120 m contours: 120 m – 80 m = 40 m.
3. Slope angle \(	heta\) is given by \(	heta = \arctan\left( rac{rise}{run}ight) = \arctan\left( rac{40}{125}ight)\).
4. \( rac{40}{125}=0.32\).
5. \(\arctan(0.32) \approx 17.7°\), which rounds to 18°.
6. Option B matches this value; the other options are either too low (A) or too high (C, D).

questions.question_text: Which magma composition is most characteristic of volcanic eruptions at a continental hotspot such as the Yellowstone Plateau?
questions.explanation: Continental hotspots involve extensive melting of the thick continental crust, which enriches magma in silica. The resulting eruptions are dominantly rhyolitic, producing highly viscous, silica‑rich lava. Basaltic magma is typical of oceanic hotspots, and andesitic or komatiitic magmas are associated with subduction zones or Archean settings, respectively. Therefore, option C is correct.

questions.question_text: What is the solar altitude angle at local noon on the summer solstice for a location at latitude 60° N? (Solar declination on the summer solstice = +23.5°.)
questions.explanation:
Solar altitude at noon is \(\alpha = 90^{\circ} - (\phi - \delta)\).
Substituting \(\phi = 60^{\circ}\) and \(\delta = 23.5^{\circ}\):
\[\alpha = 90^{\circ} - (60^{\circ} - 23.5^{\circ}) = 90^{\circ} - 36.5^{\circ} = 53.5^{\circ}\]
Option B matches the calculation; the other values result from incorrect subtraction or addition of the declination.

questions.question_text: A region has a carrying capacity \(K = 80\) million, an initial population \(P_{0}=10\) million and an intrinsic growth rate \(r = 0.04\) yr⁻¹. Using the logistic growth model, what is the expected population after 20 years?
questions.explanation:
Logistic model: \(P(t)= rac{K}{1+\left( rac{K-P_{0}}{P_{0}}ight)e^{-rt}}\).
Compute the constant term: \( rac{K-P_{0}}{P_{0}}= rac{80-10}{10}=7\).
Exponent term for \(t=20\): \(e^{-rt}=e^{-0.04	imes20}=e^{-0.8}\approx0.4493\).
Denominator: \(1+7	imes0.4493 = 1+3.1453=4.1453\).
Population: \(P(20)= rac{80}{4.1453}\approx19.30\) million.
Option B matches; the other options are either underestimates (A) or overestimates (C, D) resulting from neglecting the limiting effect of the carrying capacity.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: Which GIS data capture technique is most suitable for quickly updating land‑use information over a large urban area while maintaining high positional accuracy?

options.text: Satellite imagery with 10 m spatial resolution

questions.explanation:
Airborne LiDAR provides point clouds with sub‑meter vertical accuracy and horizontal accuracy of a few centimeters, making it ideal for rapid, precise land‑use mapping of extensive urban areas.
- Ground surveys (A) are accurate but time‑consuming for large extents.
- High‑resolution aerial photos (B) give good visual detail but need additional processing for accurate geometry.
- Satellite imagery (C) offers broad coverage, but 10 m resolution may miss fine‑scale urban features and positional accuracy is lower than LiDAR.
Thus option D is the best choice.

questions.question_text: A topographic map has a scale of 1 cm : 2500 m. The distance measured between Town A and Town B on the map is 4.6 cm and the bearing of the line is 135°. What is the true ground distance between the two towns in kilometres?
questions.explanation:
The map scale tells us that 1 cm on the map equals 2500 m on the ground.
Ground distance = map distance × scale factor
= 4.6 cm × 2500 m / cm = 11 500 m = 11.5 km.
Option B matches this value. Options A, C and D are obtained by using incorrect scale factors (e.g., 2000 m / cm, 2800 m / cm, etc.) and are therefore eliminated.

questions.question_text: Which type of volcanic eruption is most characteristic of a continental rift zone where the crust is being actively thinned?

options.text: Hawaiian‑type shield eruptions producing very fluid basaltic lava
Plinian eruptions of high‑silica rhyolitic magma
Strombolian eruptions of moderately gas‑rich basaltic magma

questions.explanation: Continental rifts involve upward mantle flow and decompression melting, producing basaltic magma that is relatively low in viscosity but can retain enough volatiles to cause mild explosive activity. This style is best described by Strombolian eruptions, which throw short‑range lava bombs and build scoria cones.\n\nOption A describes oceanic hot‑spot shield volcanoes, not typical of rifts. Option B requires highly evolved, silica‑rich magma, which is rare in the early rift stage. Option C refers to massive, low‑viscosity flows that dominate large igneous provinces, not the localized activity of continental rifts. Hence D is correct.

questions.question_text: At a latitude of 35° N, what is the approximate solar altitude angle at solar noon on the summer solstice (solar declination = +23.5°)?
questions.explanation:
The solar altitude at noon is given by \( h = 90^{\circ} - |\phi - \delta| \), where \(\phi\) is latitude and \(\delta\) is solar declination.

Plugging the numbers: \( h = 90^{\circ} - |35^{\circ} - 23.5^{\circ}| = 90^{\circ} - 11.5^{\circ} = 78.5^{\circ}.\)
Option B matches the calculation. Options A and D use the wrong sign or subtract the declination twice, while option C would only occur at the equator on the equinox.

questions.question_text: The population of a town increased from 250,000 to 302,500 over a period of 5 years. Assuming exponential growth, what is the average annual growth rate (to the nearest tenth of a percent)?
questions.explanation:
For exponential growth \(P = P_{0}e^{rt}\). Solving for \(r\):
\[ r =  rac{1}{t}\ln\left( rac{P}{P_{0}}ight) \]

Substituting the values: \(P/P_{0}=302,500/250,000=1.21\), \(t=5\) years.
\[ r =  rac{1}{5}\ln(1.21) =  rac{1}{5}	imes0.19062 = 0.03812\]
Convert to percent: \(0.03812	imes100 \approx 3.8\%\).
Option B is correct. Options A, C and D result from using linear growth, rounding errors, or mis‑applying the formula.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text:
In the diagram below, a satellite sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.0005 rad and orbits at an altitude of 600 km above the Earth's surface. What is the ground swath width (diameter of the footprint) on the surface?
In the diagram below, ...
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="80" y="70" width="160" height="60" fill="none" stroke="black"/><line x1="160" y1="70" x2="160" y2="130" stroke="red" stroke-width="2"/><text x="160" y="60" font-size="14" text-anchor="middle">IFOV</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
The ground swath (footprint) width is given by \( W = 2 H 	an(	heta/2) \), where \(H\) is the sensor altitude and \(	heta\) is the IFOV.

Here \(H = 600\,	ext{km}\) and \(	heta = 0.0005\,	ext{rad}\).
\[ 	heta/2 = 0.00025\,	ext{rad} \]
For such a small angle, \(	an(0.00025) \approx 0.00025\).
\[ W \approx 2 	imes 600\,	ext{km} 	imes 0.00025 = 0.3\,	ext{km} = 300\,	ext{m}. \]
Option B matches the calculation. Option A would correspond to using half the altitude, option C doubles the correct result, and option D quadruples it, all stemming from mis‑applying the tangent factor.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, the distance between Town A and Town B on the map measures 7.8 cm. The map scale is 1 cm : 5 km. What is the actual ground distance between the towns in kilometres?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='100' x2='270' y2='100' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><text x='50' y='120' font-size='12'>A</text><text x='270' y='120' font-size='12'>B</text></svg>
questions.explanation: The map scale states that 1 cm on the map equals 5 km on the ground. Multiply the measured map distance by the scale factor:\n\[\text{Ground distance}=7.8\,\text{cm}\times5\,\frac{\text{km}}{\text{cm}}=39\,\text{km}\]\nOption A matches this calculation. Options B, C and D result from using incorrect scale factors or arithmetic errors.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best explains why basaltic magma produced at a mid‑ocean ridge is less viscous than rhyolitic magma generated in continental collision zones?

options.text: Basaltic magma contains a higher proportion of dissolved gases, which reduces its viscosity.
Rhyolitic magma is typically cooler, and lower temperature increases viscosity.
Basaltic magma has a lower silica (SiO₂) content, resulting in fewer Si–O polymer chains and therefore lower viscosity.
Rhyolitic magma crystallizes earlier, and the presence of crystals raises viscosity.

questions.explanation:
Viscosity of magma is controlled primarily by its silica content.
- High SiO₂ (rhyolite) creates extensive polymerized silica networks, giving high viscosity.
- Low SiO₂ (basalt) forms short chains, producing low viscosity.
Option C directly states this relationship. Option A is incorrect because basaltic magma usually has fewer volatiles. Option B mentions temperature, but mid‑ocean‑ridge basalts are hotter, which also lowers viscosity, yet the dominant factor is silica content. Option D is true for crystals but does not explain the fundamental viscosity difference between basalt and rhyolite.

questions.question_text: At a latitude of 55° N, what is the solar altitude angle at local noon on the summer solstice (solar declination = +23.44°)?
questions.explanation: Solar altitude at noon is given by \(\alpha = 90^{\circ} - |\phi - \delta|\), where \(\phi\) is latitude and \(\delta\) is solar declination.\n\[\alpha = 90^{\circ} - |55^{\circ} - 23.44^{\circ}| = 90^{\circ} - 31.56^{\circ} = 58.44^{\circ}\]\nRounded to one decimal place, the angle is 58.4°, which corresponds to option D. The other options arise from mis‑applying the absolute value or using the wrong sign for declination.

questions.question_text: A country had a population of 12 million in 2015. The crude birth rate was 14 per 1 000, the crude death rate 7 per 1 000, and net migration was +25 000 per year. Assuming these rates remain constant, what was the estimated population at the end of 2020?
questions.explanation:
Natural increase per year = (CBR ‑ CDR)/1000 × P.
- CBR = 14, CDR = 7 → net natural increase factor = 0.007.
- Net migration M = 25 000.
Iterate for 5 years:
\[\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= 12,000,000\\
P_1 &= 12,000,000\times1.007 + 25,000 = 12,109,000\\
P_2 &= 12,109,000\times1.007 + 25,000 = 12,218,763\\
P_3 &= 12,218,763\times1.007 + 25,000 = 12,329,294\\
P_4 &= 12,329,294\times1.007 + 25,000 = 12,440,599\\
P_5 &= 12,440,599\times1.007 + 25,000 \approx 12,552,684\end{aligned}\]
Rounded to two decimal places, the population is about 12.55 million, matching option B. Options A, C, and D result from omitting the migration term, using a wrong growth factor, or rounding incorrectly.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: Which GIS data capture method is most appropriate for producing an up‑to‑date land‑use map of a rapidly expanding metropolitan area, where frequent changes occur and ground access is limited?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='160,30 150,80 170,80' fill='gray'/><rect x='140' y='80' width='40' height='80' fill='lightgray' stroke='black'/><text x='120' y='180' font-size='12'>City</text></svg>

options.text: Photogrammetric aerial survey using manned aircraft
Satellite multispectral imagery with a medium revisit period (e.g., 16 days)
Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) high‑resolution imagery collected on demand
Ground‑based GPS survey of selected parcels

questions.explanation:
UAVs can be deployed quickly, fly at low altitude, and capture very high‑resolution images, making them ideal for frequent updates in areas where access on the ground is difficult.
- Option A provides good resolution but requires flight planning and is less flexible for rapid repeats.
- Option B offers broad coverage but the revisit interval may miss fast‑changing land‑use.
- Option D is too time‑consuming for a whole metro area.
Thus, option C is the most suitable choice.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, points A and B are plotted on a topographic map. The map scale is 1 cm : 500 m. The line AB measures 3.2 cm on the map, and the north‑south grid lines are 0.8 cm apart vertically while the east‑west grid lines are 0.6 cm apart horizontally. What is the bearing of line AB measured clockwise from true north?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='210' y2='70' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><text x='50' y='165' font-size='12'>A</text><text x='210' y='65' font-size='12'>B</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
Convert the map components to ground distances: \(\Delta N = 0.8	ext{ cm}	imes500	ext{ m/cm}=400	ext{ m}\) and \(\Delta E = 0.6	ext{ cm}	imes500	ext{ m/cm}=300	ext{ m}\). The bearing \(	heta\) from true north is \(	heta = \arctan\left( rac{\Delta E}{\Delta N}ight)=\arctan\left( rac{300}{400}ight)\approx36.9^{\circ}\). Rounded to the nearest whole degree the bearing is 037°, which corresponds to option A.

Distractor analysis:
- B (045°) adds an unnecessary 8° correction.
- C (053°) would be the bearing if the east‑west component were larger.
- D (062°) overestimates the eastward component.

questions.question_text: Which type of volcanic eruption is most characteristic of a continental rift zone where the lithosphere is being actively thinned?
questions.explanation:
Continental rift zones involve crustal extension that creates long linear fractures. Low‑viscosity basaltic magma ascends through these fissures, producing extensive fissure eruptions rather than centralized explosive events.

- B describes continental hotspot or caldera settings.
- C is typical of convergent margins.
- D refers to oceanic hotspot shield volcanoes.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 90,000 in 2015. It has experienced an average annual growth rate of 2.4 % (compounded). Using the exponential growth model, what is the projected population in 2025?
questions.explanation: Apply the compound growth formula \(P = P_{0}(1 + r)^{t}\) where \(P_{0}=90{,}000\), \(r = 0.024\) and \(t = 10\) years. \(P = 90{,}000 \times (1.024)^{10}\). \((1.024)^{10} \approx 1.267\). Therefore \(P \approx 90{,}000 \times 1.267 \approx 114{,}030\), which rounds to 114,000 (option C).\n\nDistractors: A underestimates growth (uses 1.02). B is a moderate under‑estimate. D assumes a higher rate (≈2.8 %).

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: For monitoring seasonal changes in rice cultivation across the Niger Delta, which remote‑sensing platform provides the optimal combination of spatial and temporal resolution?
questions.explanation:
Rice phenology in the Niger Delta requires sub‑10 m detail to separate fields, while a 5‑day revisit captures rapid growth stages. Sentinel‑2 offers 10 m spatial resolution with a 5‑day repeat cycle, matching both needs.

- A (Landsat 8) has adequate resolution but its 16‑day revisit misses intermediate stages.
- C (MODIS) provides daily coverage but at coarse 250 m resolution, insufficient to map individual paddies.
- D (WorldView‑3) has very high spatial detail but limited swath width and higher cost; its 4‑day revisit is not a decisive advantage over Sentinel‑2 for regional monitoring.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, the city displays a multiple‑nuclei pattern of development. Nucleus C is located on the northeastern fringe of the urban area. Which land‑use type is most likely to dominate nucleus C?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='80' cy='100' r='30' fill='none' stroke='black'/><circle cx='200' cy='80' r='30' fill='none' stroke='black'/><circle cx='260' cy='150' r='30' fill='none' stroke='black'/><text x='70' y='105' font-size='12'>A</text><text x='190' y='85' font-size='12'>B</text><text x='250' y='155' font-size='12'>C</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
In a multiple‑nuclei model, each nucleus develops around a specialized function. Nuclei on the outer fringe, especially toward the industrial corridors, commonly host heavy‑industry because of easier access to transport links and lower land‑price pressures.

- A (CBD) is usually the innermost core.
- B (high‑density residential) tends to cluster near the CBD or transport hubs, not at peripheral industrial edges.
- D (suburban shopping mall) is a low‑intensity commercial node, less likely to dominate a clearly industrial‑type nucleus.

questions.question_text:
In the diagram below, point A is the origin (0 m E, 0 m N) and point B is located 300 m east and 400 m north of A. What is the bearing of the line AB measured clockwise from true north?

In the diagram below, ...
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='200' y2='50' stroke='black' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arrowhead)'/><defs><marker id='arrowhead' markerWidth='10' markerHeight='7' refX='0' refY='3.5' orient='auto'><polygon points='0 0, 10 3.5, 0 7' fill='black'/></marker></defs><text x='45' y='165' font-size='12'>A</text><text x='205' y='45' font-size='12'>B</text></svg>
questions.explanation: The bearing \(\theta\) from north is given by \(\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\text{East}}{\text{North}}\right)\). Substituting the values, \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(300/400) = \tan^{-1}(0.75) \approx 36.9^{\circ}\). Options B, C and D correspond to larger angles and would require a larger east component relative to north, which is not the case here.

questions.question_text: A mantle plume rises beneath a stable continental lithosphere and produces large-volume eruptions that have high silica content. Which volcanic rock is most characteristic of such eruptions?
questions.explanation: Mantle plumes generate extensive melting of the underlying continental crust, enriching the magma in silica. The resulting lava is highly viscous and silica‑rich, producing rhyolitic eruptions. Basalt is typical of mantle‑derived magma with low silica, while andesite and dacite have intermediate silica levels and are more common in sub‑duction zones.

questions.question_text: Why does a coastal city usually experience a smaller diurnal temperature range in summer than in winter?

options.text: The sea surface temperature is lower than the land temperature in summer.
The high specific heat capacity of water moderates temperature fluctuations.
Summer days are shorter, allowing less time for heating.
Winter winds are weaker, reducing heat exchange with the ocean.

questions.explanation: Water has a high specific heat capacity, meaning it absorbs and releases heat slowly. In summer, the nearby ocean absorbs excess heat during the day and releases it at night, reducing the temperature swing. In winter, the ocean releases stored heat, keeping nights warmer, but the overall daily range becomes larger because the land cools rapidly at night. Options A, C, and D misrepresent the physical processes governing coastal temperature moderation.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 2.5 million in 2010. The natural increase (births minus deaths) is 30 000 persons per year and the net migration is –5 000 persons per year. Assuming these rates stay constant, what will be the population in 2015?
questions.explanation: The annual net change = natural increase + net migration = 30 000 – 5 000 = 25 000 persons. Over 5 years the total increase = 5 × 25 000 = 125 000. Adding this to the 2010 population: 2 500 000 + 125 000 = 2 625 000. Options A, B, and D correspond to incorrect arithmetic or omission of the migration component.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: Which remote‑sensing platform is most suitable for monitoring rapid urban expansion with sub‑meter spatial resolution and a revisit interval of about one week?
questions.explanation: WorldView‑3 and similar commercial satellites provide sub‑meter resolution (≈0.31 m panchromatic) and have revisit times of 1–3 days, meeting the weekly requirement. Landsat 8 (30 m) and Sentinel‑2 (10 m) lack the necessary spatial detail. UAVs can achieve sub‑meter resolution but are limited in coverage area and cannot reliably revisit a large city on a weekly schedule without extensive flight operations, making them less practical for continuous monitoring of rapid urban growth.

questions.question_text: A map is drawn at a scale of 1 cm : 500 m. On the map, point A is located at (2.3 cm, 4.5 cm) and point B at (7.8 cm, 9.1 cm) measured from the same origin. What is the bearing of the line from A to B measured clockwise from true north?
questions.explanation:
1. Find the map‑distance differences:  \(\Delta x = 7.8\,	ext{cm} - 2.3\,	ext{cm} = 5.5\,	ext{cm}\),  \(\Delta y = 9.1\,	ext{cm} - 4.5\,	ext{cm} = 4.6\,	ext{cm}\).
2. Convert to ground distances using the scale (1 cm = 500 m):  \(\Delta E = 5.5 	imes 500 = 2750\,	ext{m}\),  \(\Delta N = 4.6 	imes 500 = 2300\,	ext{m}\).
3. Bearing from north is given by  \(	heta = \arctan\left( rac{\Delta E}{\Delta N}ight)\).  Substituting,  \(	heta = \arctan\left( rac{2750}{2300}ight) \approx \arctan(1.196)\).
4. \(\arctan(1.196)\) is about 50°.
5. Comparing with the options, 50° corresponds to option **B**.
6. Elimination: 45° (A) is too low, 55° (C) and 60° (D) are higher than the computed value.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a cross‑section shows a mantle plume rising beneath continental lithosphere, causing crustal thinning from 40 km to 30 km before melting occurs at 70 km depth. Which of the following statements best explains why volcanism is likely to be more voluminous in this setting compared to a thick‑crust region?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='60' y='80' width='200' height='40' fill='#c2b280' stroke='#000'/><polygon points='160,20 150,80 170,80' fill='#ff9955' stroke='#000'/></svg>

options.text: The reduced pressure lowers the melting point of mantle material, increasing melt generation.
The thicker crust provides more material for melting, decreasing melt volume.
The mantle plume temperature is lower under thin crust, limiting melting.
Crustal thinning accelerates erosion, removing magma before eruption.

questions.explanation:
When the crust is thinned from 40 km to 30 km, the over‑burden pressure on the upwelling mantle is reduced. Lower pressure shifts the solidus of peridotite to lower temperatures, so the same plume temperature can produce a larger degree of partial melting. Hence more magma is generated (Option A).
Option B is incorrect because a thicker crust actually *inhibits* melt ascent and raises the pressure, reducing melt volume.
Option C is wrong; mantle plume temperature is independent of crustal thickness.
Option D confuses surface processes with melt generation; erosion does not remove magma from the mantle source.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 120,000 in 2015. The annual natural increase (births minus deaths) is 1,200 persons, and net migration is –300 persons per year. Assuming these rates remain constant, what will be the projected population at the end of 2020?
questions.explanation:
The net yearly change = natural increase + net migration = 1,200 + (‑300) = 900 persons.
Over 5 years (2015‑2020) the total change = 900 × 5 = 4,500.
Projected population = 120,000 + 4,500 = 124,500, which matches option **A**.
All other options either add too much (B, D) or subtract (C).

questions.question_text: Which of the following settlement patterns is most characteristic of a rapidly growing city where residential, commercial and industrial functions develop around multiple pre‑existing nuclei such as universities and railway stations?
questions.explanation: The Multiple Nuclei Model (Option C) suggests that a city develops several independent centers (nuclei) around which different land‑uses cluster. This fits a city that expands around existing institutions such as universities or railway stations.\nThe Concentric Zone Model (A) assumes a single central business district; the Sector Model (B) predicts development in wedges radiating from the centre; the Central Place Model (D) deals with the hierarchy of settlements, not internal city structure.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: For a farmer monitoring crop health weekly over a 200 km² area, which remote‑sensing platform provides the optimal balance of spatial resolution (≤5 m) and revisit time (≤7 days)?

options.text: Commercial high‑resolution satellite (e.g., PlanetScope, 3 m, daily revisit)

questions.explanation:
The farmer needs ≤5 m spatial detail and a revisit ≤7 days.
- Landsat 8 (A) offers 30 m resolution, too coarse.
- Sentinel‑2 (B) meets the revisit requirement but its best resolution is 10 m, still above the ≤5 m threshold.
- Commercial constellations such as PlanetScope (C) provide ~3 m resolution and a daily revisit, satisfying both criteria.
- MODIS (D) has coarse spatial resolution (250 m) despite daily coverage.
Therefore, option **C** is the best fit.

questions.question_text: A topographic map has a scale of 1 cm : 2 km. The distance measured between City X and City Y on the map is 3.5 cm. What is the actual ground distance between the two cities?
questions.explanation: The map scale indicates that 1 cm on the map equals 2 km on the ground. Multiply the measured map distance by the scale factor: 3.5 cm × 2 km/cm = 7 km. Options A, B, and D are lower or higher than the computed distance, leaving C as the correct answer.

questions.question_text: A volcano displays predominantly explosive eruptions, generates ash clouds that reach 15 km altitude, and its lava is highly viscous. Which magma composition is most likely responsible for this behaviour?
questions.explanation: Rhyolitic magma is silica‑rich, giving it very high viscosity. The high viscosity traps gases, leading to explosive eruptions and high ash plumes. Basaltic magma is low‑viscosity and generally produces effusive flows; andesitic magma is intermediate; komatiitic magma is ultramafic and also low‑viscosity. Therefore the observed explosive, highly viscous behaviour points to rhyolitic composition.

questions.question_text: On March 21 (the vernal equinox) at latitude 30° N, the solar declination is 0°. Using the solar altitude formula \(h = 90^{\circ} - |\phi - \delta|\), where \(\phi\) is latitude and \(\delta\) declination, calculate the solar altitude angle at local noon.
questions.explanation: Insert the values: \(\phi = 30^{\circ}\), \(\delta = 0^{\circ}\). Thus \(h = 90^{\circ} - |30^{\circ} - 0^{\circ}| = 90^{\circ} - 30^{\circ} = 60^{\circ}\). Options B, C, and D are inconsistent with the calculation, so A is correct.

questions.question_text: A region follows logistic population growth with carrying capacity \(K = 500{,}000\), initial population \(P_{0}=50{,}000\) and intrinsic growth rate \(r = 0.08\) yr⁻¹. Using the formula \(P(t)=\frac{K}{1+\left(\frac{K-P_{0}}{P_{0}}\right)e^{-rt}}\), what is the approximate population after 10 years?
questions.explanation: First compute the ratio \((K-P_{0})/P_{0}= (500{,}000-50{,}000)/50{,}000 = 9\). Then \(e^{-rt}=e^{-0.08\times10}=e^{-0.8}\approx0.4493\). Denominator: \(1+9\times0.4493 = 1+4.0437 = 5.0437\). Finally \(P(10)=500{,}000/5.0437 \approx 99{,}124\). Rounded to the nearest thousand, this is about 99,000, matching option B. The other options are either too low or too high relative to the computed value.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.001 rad and orbits at an altitude of 600 km. What is the ground sample distance (GSD) represented by a single pixel?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="160" cy="100" r="50" stroke="black" fill="none"/><line x1="160" y1="100" x2="260" y2="100" stroke="red"/></svg>
questions.explanation: Ground sample distance is the linear dimension on the ground that corresponds to one detector element. It is given by \(GSD = H \times \text{IFOV}\). Substituting \(H = 600\text{ km}\) and \(\text{IFOV}=0.001\text{ rad}\): \(GSD = 600\text{ km}\times0.001 = 0.6\text{ km}\), i.e., 600 m. Option D expresses the result correctly; the other options either omit the conversion or incorrectly double the value.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, the map scale is 1 cm : 2.5 km. The line AB on the map measures 6.2 cm and is drawn with a bearing of 070°. What is the true ground bearing of AB and the actual distance between points A and B on the ground?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='250,50 245,60 255,60' fill='black'/></svg>
questions.explanation: The map scale tells us that each centimetre on the map represents 2.5 km on the ground. Therefore the ground distance is 6.2 cm × 2.5 km/cm = 15.5 km. Scale does not affect direction, so the bearing shown on the map (070°) is the true ground bearing. Options B and D give the opposite bearing, while C uses an incorrect conversion factor (1 cm = 2 km). Hence A is correct.

questions.question_text: Which type of volcanic rock is most likely to be erupted at a continental hotspot such as the Yellowstone plateau, where mantle plume interaction produces high‑silica magmas?
questions.explanation: Continental hotspots often cause extensive melting of crustal material, generating magmas that are enriched in silica. High‑silica magmas solidify as rhyolite, which is characterised by high viscosity and explosive eruptions. Basalt and komatiite are low‑silica, while andesite is intermediate. Therefore the rock most typical of Yellowstone‑type eruptions is rhyolite.

questions.question_text: A coastal town has an initial population of 80 000. Its carrying capacity is estimated at 250 000 and it follows logistic growth with an intrinsic growth rate of 0.08 yr⁻¹. Using the logistic growth equation \[P(t)=\frac{K}{1+\left(\frac{K-P_{0}}{P_{0}}\right)e^{-rt}}\], what is the projected population after 5 years?
questions.explanation: First compute the ratio \(\frac{K-P_{0}}{P_{0}} = \frac{250000-80000}{80000}=2.125\). Then calculate \(e^{-rt}=e^{-0.08\times5}=e^{-0.4}\approx0.6703\). The denominator becomes \(1+2.125\times0.6703\approx1+1.424=2.424\). Hence \(P(5)=\frac{250000}{2.424}\approx103147\), which rounds to 103 000. Options A and C are off by a larger margin, and D is far too high, leaving B as the correct choice.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most significant factor promoting the development of peripheral business districts in a polycentric (multiple‑nuclei) urban model?

options.text: High land prices in the central business district

questions.explanation: In a multiple‑nuclei city, firms and services relocate to peripheral zones primarily to escape the high rents and congestion of the original CBD. This creates secondary business districts. While coastlines, agriculture, and heritage sites can influence location, the dominant driver of peripheral nuclei is the economic pressure of expensive central land. Hence option A is correct.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.001 rad and orbits at an altitude of 700 km. What is the approximate ground resolution (size of one pixel) of the sensor?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='160' y1='20' x2='160' y2='180' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='180' x2='300' y2='180' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='20' x2='300' y2='180' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/><text x='170' y='100' font-size='12'>IFOV</text></svg>
questions.explanation: Ground resolution is obtained by multiplying the IFOV (in radians) by the sensor altitude: \(G = 0.001\ \text{rad} \times 700\,\text{km} = 0.7\,\text{km}\). Converting to metres gives 700 m. Options B–D are orders of magnitude larger, so A is correct.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, the line segment AB is drawn on a map that has a scale of 1 cm : 250 m. The measured length of AB on the map is 4.6 cm and its bearing is shown as N 45° E. What is the true ground distance between points A and B and what is the bearing on the ground?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='150' x2='250' y2='50' stroke='black' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
questions.explanation: First convert the map distance to real distance using the scale: 4.6 cm × 250 m/cm = 1150 m. The bearing on a map is true bearing; it does not change when converting to ground distance. Therefore the correct answer is 1150 m with bearing N 45° E. Options B, C and D either give the wrong direction or ignore the bearing conversion.

questions.question_text: Which characteristic best describes the magma that typically forms at a divergent plate boundary?

options.text: High silica content, high viscosity, explosive eruptions
Low silica content, low viscosity, effusive eruptions
Intermediate silica, moderate viscosity, mixed eruptions
High silica, low temperature, slow eruption

questions.explanation: Magma generated at divergent (mid‑ocean ridge) settings originates from decompression melting of the upper mantle. It is basaltic, meaning it has low silica (~50 %), low viscosity and therefore produces gentle, lava‑flow dominated (effusive) eruptions. Option A describes continental arc magmas, C describes sub‑duction zone intermediate magmas, and D is not typical for divergent boundaries.

questions.question_text: At a latitude of 10° N, the solar declination changes from 0° on the March equinox to +23.4° on the June solstice. Using the solar altitude formula \(h = 90^{\circ} - | \phi - \delta |\), where \( \phi \) is latitude and \( \delta \) is declination, what is the change in the solar altitude angle at local noon between the two dates?
questions.explanation: For the March equinox: \( \phi = 10^{\circ}, \delta = 0^{\circ}\) → \( h_{eq} = 90^{\circ} - |10^{\circ}| = 80^{\circ}\). For the June solstice: \( \delta = 23.4^{\circ}\) → \( h_{sol} = 90^{\circ} - |10^{\circ} - 23.4^{\circ}| = 90^{\circ} - 13.4^{\circ} = 76.6^{\circ}\). The absolute change is \( |80^{\circ} - 76.6^{\circ}| = 3.4^{\circ}\). Thus option A is correct; the other choices overestimate the change.

questions.question_text: A city had a population of 200 000 in the year 2010. Assuming logistic growth with an intrinsic growth rate \(r = 0.03\) yr\(^{-1}\) and a carrying capacity \(K = 800{,}000\), what is the projected population in the year 2020? Use the logistic formula \(P(t)=\frac{K}{1+\left(\frac{K-P_{0}}{P_{0}}\right)e^{-rt}}\).
questions.explanation: First compute the ratio \(\frac{K-P_{0}}{P_{0}} = \frac{800{,}000-200{,}000}{200{,}000}=3\). For \(t = 10\) yr, \(e^{-rt}=e^{-0.03\times10}=e^{-0.3}\approx0.7408\). Plug into the logistic equation: \(P(10)=\frac{800{,}000}{1+3\times0.7408}=\frac{800{,}000}{1+2.2224}=\frac{800{,}000}{3.2224}\approx248{,}400\). Rounded to the nearest thousand gives 248 000, matching option B. The other options either ignore the limiting effect of the carrying capacity (A, C) or over‑estimate growth (D).

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.001 rad and orbits at an altitude of 700 km above the Earth's surface. What is the approximate ground‑sample distance (GSD) represented by one pixel at the nadir point?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='160,10 200,190 120,190' fill='none' stroke='blue' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
questions.explanation: Ground‑sample distance is found by multiplying the sensor altitude by the IFOV: \(H = 700\,\text{km}=700{,}000\,\text{m}\). \(\text{GSD}=700{,}000\,\text{m}\times0.001\,\text{rad}=700\,\text{m}\). Therefore each pixel covers roughly 700 m on the ground, which corresponds to option D. The other choices result from misplaced decimal‑point calculations.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a topographic map shows two points A and B. The map scale is 1 cm : 250 m. On the map, the coordinates of A are (30 mm, 40 mm) and those of B are (95 mm, 150 mm) measured from the origin. Calculate the bearing of the line from A to B to the nearest degree.
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='40' x2='95' y2='150' stroke='black' stroke-width='2' /><circle cx='30' cy='40' r='3' fill='red' /><circle cx='95' cy='150' r='3' fill='blue' /><text x='15' y='35' font-size='12'>A</text><text x='100' y='155' font-size='12'>B</text></svg>
questions.explanation:
Convert the map coordinates to centimetres (1 mm = 0.1 cm):
\[A(3\,	ext{cm},\,4\,	ext{cm}),\; B(9.5\,	ext{cm},\,15\,	ext{cm})\]

Calculate the differences: 
\[\Delta E = 9.5 - 3 = 6.5\,	ext{cm},\quad \Delta N = 15 - 4 = 11\,	ext{cm}\]

The bearing \(	heta\) measured clockwise from north is given by \(	heta = 	an^{-1}(\Delta E/\Delta N)\).
\[	heta = 	an^{-1}\left( rac{6.5}{11}ight) \approx 30.6^{\circ}\]

Rounded to the nearest degree the bearing is 031°, which corresponds to option A. Options B, C and D represent different quadrants or incorrect calculations of the arctangent.

questions.question_text: On the day of the summer solstice, a location at latitude 45° N experiences a solar declination of +23.44°. Using the solar altitude formula at local noon, what is the approximate solar altitude angle?
questions.explanation:
The solar altitude at local noon is \(h = 90^{\circ} - |\phi - \delta|\), where \(\phi\) is latitude and \(\delta\) is declination.
\[h = 90^{\circ} - |45^{\circ} - 23.44^{\circ}| = 90^{\circ} - 21.56^{\circ} = 68.44^{\circ}\]

Rounded to the nearest degree, the altitude is 68°, which matches option A. The other choices result from using incorrect signs or forgetting the absolute value.

questions.question_text: A town had a population of 150,000 in the year 2020. The natural increase (births minus deaths) is 2,000 persons per year and net migration adds another 1,500 persons per year. Assuming these rates stay constant, what will be the population at the end of 2025?
questions.explanation:
Total annual increase = natural increase + net migration = 2,000 + 1,500 = 3,500 persons.

Number of years from the start of 2020 to the end of 2025 is 6 years (2020‑2025 inclusive). However, the increase is applied *after* each full year; from end‑2020 to end‑2025 there are 5 full years.

Population after 5 years: \[P = 150,000 + 3,500 	imes 5 = 150,000 + 17,500 = 167,500\]

Thus the correct answer is 167,500 (option B). Options A and C use incorrect year counts, while D over‑estimates the increase.

questions.question_text: Which urban settlement model best describes a rapidly expanding city where high‑rise residential towers develop around a central business district, accompanied by secondary commercial nodes linked by major arterial highways?
questions.explanation: The Multiple Nuclei Model posits that a city develops several distinct centres (nuclei) of activity—such as secondary commercial nodes—connected by transport corridors, while the original central business district remains dominant. This matches the description of high‑rise development around the CBD together with peripheral nodes linked by highways. The Concentric Zone Model (A) assumes rings, the Sector Model (B) assumes wedges extending from the centre, and the Peripheral Model (D) emphasizes edge‑city development, none of which capture the multiple nuclei pattern.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: A research team needs satellite imagery to map the spatial extent of a recent flood with a ground resolution of ≤5 m and a revisit interval of ≤3 days. Which satellite system is most suitable for this task?
questions.explanation: PlanetScope’s Dove constellation provides imagery at a spatial resolution of about 3 m, comfortably meeting the ≤5 m requirement, and its constellation design yields a daily revisit, satisfying the ≤3‑day criterion. Landsat 8 (A) is too coarse and too infrequent, Sentinel‑2 (B) meets the resolution but its 5‑day revisit is marginal, and MODIS (D) offers excessive pixel size for flood delineation.

questions.question_text: A topographic map of a region is drawn at a scale of 1 cm : 25 km. On the map the straight‑line distance between Town X and Town Y measures 6.4 cm. What is the actual ground distance between the two towns in kilometres?
questions.explanation: Scale 1 cm : 25 km means each centimetre on the map represents 25 km on the ground. Multiply the measured map distance by the scale factor: 6.4 cm × 25 km/cm = 160 km. Therefore option B is correct. Options A, C and D result from using incorrect scale factors or arithmetic errors.

questions.question_text: Which type of igneous rock is most characteristically formed from low‑viscosity, magnesium‑rich magma erupted at a divergent plate boundary?
questions.explanation: Divergent plate boundaries generate magma that is derived from the upwelling mantle. This magma is mafic, low in silica, rich in Mg and Fe, and has low viscosity. When it erupts it solidifies as basalt, a dark fine‑grained rock. Rhyolite and andesite are more felsic and typical of convergent settings; diorite is intrusive. Hence the correct answer is C.

questions.question_text: At latitude 35° N on the June solstice the solar declination is +23.44°. Using the solar altitude formula \(h = 90^\circ - | \phi - \delta |\), what is the solar altitude \(h\) at local solar noon?
questions.explanation: Insert the latitude \(\phi = 35^\circ\) and declination \(\delta = 23.44^\circ\) into the formula: \[ h = 90^\circ - |35^\circ - 23.44^\circ| = 90^\circ - 11.56^\circ = 78.44^\circ \] Rounded to one decimal place this is 78.4°, which corresponds to option B. The other choices arise from using the wrong sign, forgetting the absolute value, or assuming the sun is directly overhead.

questions.subtopic: GIS Data Capture and Remote Sensing Basics
questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a satellite sensor has an instantaneous field of view (IFOV) of 0.002 rad and orbits at an altitude of 700 km above the Earth's surface. What is the approximate ground‑resolution (size of the footprint) on the surface directly beneath the satellite?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="160" cy="100" r="80" stroke="black" stroke-width="2" fill="none"/></svg>
questions.explanation: Ground resolution (footprint size) is given by \(G = H \times \theta\), where \(H\) is the sensor altitude and \(\theta\) is the IFOV in radians. Substituting the values: \[ G = 700\,\text{km} \times 0.002\,\text{rad} = 1.4\,\text{km} \] Therefore option A is correct. Option B would correspond to halving either the altitude or IFOV, option C doubles the product, and option D represents a four‑fold increase, none of which match the given parameters.

questions.question_text: Which urban model best explains the development of several distinct commercial and industrial centres within a rapidly expanding metropolis?
questions.explanation: The Multiple Nuclei Model (Harris & Ullman) proposes that a large city develops several independent centres (nuclei) of specialized activities such as manufacturing, retail, and business districts, rather than a single central business district. This pattern matches the description of several distinct commercial and industrial centres. The Concentric Zone and Sector models assume a single dominant centre, while the Urban‑Realms Model focuses on the functional relationship between a central city and its surrounding commuter zones, not on multiple internal nuclei.

questions.question_text: In the diagram below, a topographic map shows two points P and Q. The map scale is 1 cm : 500 m. The distance between P and Q measured on the map is 6.4 cm, and the bearing of the line PQ measured clockwise from north is 135°. What is the true ground distance between P and Q, and what is the azimuth of the line from Q to P?
questions.diagram_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="80" y1="150" x2="240" y2="30" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="80" cy="150" r="5" fill="red"/><text x="70" y="165" font-size="12">P</text><circle cx="240" cy="30" r="5" fill="blue"/><text x="250" y="35" font-size="12">Q</text></svg>
